### Limbdi

**Location:** Limbdi, Limbdi, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Royal stay


Limbdi, a small princely state in Saurashtra, was visited by Vivekananda. The Thakore Sahib of Limbdi was among the Rajput rulers who welcomed and supported the wandering monk during his extended tour of Gujarat.


**From the Complete Works** (4 references):

> Lord. \
> \
> Recently my health was very bad, and so I have been delayed, otherwise I had intended to leave for the Punjab this \
> week. The doctor had advised me not to go to the plains at the present time, as it is very hot there. Perhaps I may \
> reach Karachi by about the last week of October. Now I am doing somewhat well. There is nobody else with me \
> now excepting two American friends \— ladies. Probably I shall part from them at Lahore. They will wait for me in \
> Calcutta or in Rajputana. I shall probably visit Cutch, Bhuj, Junagad, Bhavnagar, Limbdi, and Baroda and then \
> proceed to Calcutta. My present plan is to go to America via China and Japan in November or December, but it is…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Khetri, \
> 14th December, 1897. \
> \
> \
> I have today sent your power of attorney with my signature. . . . Draw the money as early as you can, and wire to \
> me as soon as you have done so. A Raja of a place in Bundelkhand named Chatrapur has invited me. I shall visit \
> the place on my way to the Math. The Raja of Limbdi, too, is writing earnestly. I cannot avoid going there also. I \
> shall make a lightning tour of Kathiawar \— that is what it will come to. I shall feel great relief as soon as I reach \
> Calcutta. . . . There is no news from Boston as yet; perhaps Sharat is coming; anyway, whenever any news comes \
> from anywhere, write to me immediately. \
> \
> \
> Yours affectionately, \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> 17th February, 1901. \
> \
> DEAR Joe, (Miss Josephine MacLeod.) \
> \
> Just now received your nice long letter. I am so glad that you met and approve Miss Cornelia Sorabji. I knew her \
> father at Poona, also a younger sister who was in America. Perhaps her mother will remember me as the Sannyasin \
> who used to live with the Thakore Sahib of Limbdi at Poona. \
> \
> I hope you will go to Baroda and see the Maharani. \
> \
> I am much better and hope to continue so for some time. I have just now a beautiful letter from Mrs. Sevier in \
> which she writes a whole lot of beautiful things about you. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \'ab Chronology \
> \
> XIX \
> \
> \
> Dear Vehemia Chand Limbdi, \
> \
> \
> Washington, \
> 23rd October, 1894. \
> \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Bhavnagar

**Location:** Bhavnagar city, Bhavnagar, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Important pilgrimage town


Bhavnagar was visited by Vivekananda during his Gujarat wanderings. The port city with its proximity to the Jain pilgrimage site of Palitana made it an important stop. He was received by the Maharaja and gave discourses here.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references):

> Lord. \
> \
> Recently my health was very bad, and so I have been delayed, otherwise I had intended to leave for the Punjab this \

> Calcutta or in Rajputana. I shall probably visit Cutch, Bhuj, Junagad, Bhavnagar, Limbdi, and Baroda and then \
> proceed to Calcutta. My present plan is to go to America via China and Japan in November or December, but it is…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Saheb of Mahabaleshwar, and I am living here with him. I would remain here a week or more and then proceed to \

> Perhaps by this time every hitch has been removed from your way in Junagad; at least I hope so. I am very anxious \

> \
> I saw your friend the Surti tutor to the Prince of Bhavnagar. He is a perfect gentleman. It was quite a privilege to \
> make his acquaintance; he is so good and noble-natured a man. \
> \

### Girnar

**Location:** Girnar, Junagadh, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Ancient sacred mountain


Girnar, the sacred mountain near Junagadh revered by Hindus, Jains, and Buddhists alike, was visited by Vivekananda. He climbed this ancient hill of temples and meditated at its sacred sites, communing with the divine in its ancient sanctuaries.


**From the Complete Works** (3 references):

> N. Y. \
> 2nd July 1895 \
> \
> Dear Mother \— \
> \
> You did not write to me a single line for a long time. Neither did Sister Mary write about the duty paid on the rugs \
> [from the Dewan of Junagadh]. I am afraid the rugs are small. \
> \
> Here is another consignment from Raja Ajit Singh [the Maharaja of Khetri] consisting of carpets, shawls, etc., etc., \
> for which the bill of lading you sent me the other day. This consignment has no duty to pay because it was all \
> prepaid in India, and the bill of lading says so expressly. I will send you the bill of lading and the receipt for the \
> duty. Kindly take one more trouble for me and get it out of the express company. And keep it with you till I come.…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> All these considerations may have had weight with our young Brahmacharin, but we are sure that the chief among \
> them was the thirst for knowledge. Of his travels we kn ow but little, except that, from his knowledge of Dravidian \
> languages, in which a good deal of the literature of his sect is written, and his thorough acquaintance with the old \
> Bengali of the Vaishnavas of Shri Chaitanya's order, we infer that his stay in Southern India and Bengal could not \
> have been very short. \
> \
> But on his visit to one place, the friends of his youth lay great stress. It was on the top of mount Girnar in \
> Kathiawar, they say, that he was first initiated into the mysteries of practical Yoga. \
> \
> It…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> [Cambridge , Mass. \
> 21 December 1894 ] \
> \
> Dear Mother, \
> \
> I am glad that Haridas Viharidas (The Dewanof Junagadh.) has sent the rugs. I am afraid they will take a long time to \
> reach here. The Raja (Maharaja Ajit Singh, the Raja of Khetri.) was very much pleased with the phonograph, as he writes, \
> and has heard my voice several times. Hope he will bring it into life. \
> \
> I have not seen Sister Mary yet, but hope to see her this week as I am going away to New York next Tuesday. \
> Cannot come by any means to Chicago now, for I expect to go to Washington from New York and hope to be \
> pretty busy in New York. \

*— lectures on tone building etc. \*

### Bhuj

**Location:** Bhuj, Kutch, Bhuj, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Kutch wandering


Vivekananda visited Bhuj, the capital of the Kutch region, during his Gujarat wanderings. The arid landscape of Kutch, with its unique culture and proximity to the Rann, offered a stark spiritual backdrop for the wandering monk.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references):

> Lord. \
> \
> Recently my health was very bad, and so I have been delayed, otherwise I had intended to leave for the Punjab this \
> week. The doctor had advised me not to go to the plains at the present time, as it is very hot there. Perhaps I may \
> reach Karachi by about the last week of October. Now I am doing somewhat well. There is nobody else with me \
> now excepting two American friends \— ladies. Probably I shall part from them at Lahore. They will wait for me in \
> Calcutta or in Rajputana. I shall probably visit Cutch, Bhuj, Junagad, Bhavnagar, Limbdi, and Baroda and then \
> proceed to Calcutta. My present plan is to go to America via China and Japan in November or December, but it is…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> now. His brother, the one just next to him in age, is the Commander-in-Chief. Efforts are being made to arrange a \
> lecture under his chairmanship. I shall write all about this afterwards. If the meeting for the lecture is held in a day \
> or two, I shall stay back, otherwise I go out again on my travels. Sevier is still at Murree. His health is very bad \— \
> going about in the jolting tongas and jutkas. The Bengali gentlemen of Murree are very good and courteous. Give \
> my respects to G. C. Ghosh, Atul, Master Mahashay, and others, and keep up the spirits of everybody. What is the \
> news about the house which Yogen suggested we should buy? In October I shall go down from here and shall…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Veraval

**Location:** Veraval port, Veraval, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Somnath pilgrimage gateway


Veraval was visited by Vivekananda as the gateway to the sacred Somnath temple. The ancient port city on the Saurashtra coast has deep historical significance as the site of repeated destruction and reconstruction of the Somnath Jyotirlinga.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Narayan Sarovar

**Location:** Koteshwar, Kutch, Koteshwar, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Sacred pilgrimage lake


Narayan Sarovar, one of the five most sacred lakes (panch sarovars) in Hinduism, was visited by Vivekananda during his Kutch wanderings. The remote sacred lake near the tip of Kutch is a site of immense religious importance.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Mandvi

**Location:** Mandvi port, Kutch, Mandvi, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Port town visited during Kutch journey


Mandvi, the historic port city of Kutch, was visited by Vivekananda during his extensive Gujarat tour. The town is known for its shipbuilding tradition and the beautiful Vijay Vilas Palace overlooking the sea.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*

### Beyt Dwarka

**Location:** Beyt Dwarka Island, Okha, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Sacred island of Lord Krishna


Beyt Dwarka (Bet Dwarka), the sacred island off the coast of Gujarat believed to be the actual residence of Lord Krishna, was visited by Vivekananda during his Saurashtra pilgrimage. The island is considered the actual Dwarka of Krishna's time.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*

Ashapuri near Bhavnagar was visited by Vivekananda during his Saurashtra wanderings. The small town with its ancient temple was part of the pilgrim circuit in Kathiawar that Vivekananda traversed on foot.

### Palitana

**Location:** Shatrunjaya Hills, Palitana, Palitana, India

Palitana, the foremost Jain pilgrimage destination with 900+ temples on Shatrunjaya Hill, was visited by Vivekananda. He was deeply moved by the Jain tradition of ahimsa and the magnificent cluster of marble temples atop the sacred mountain.

### Khandwa

**Location:** Khandwa, Khandwa, India
**Period:** 1890
**Significance:** Wandering stop in central India


Khandwa in central India was visited by Vivekananda during his parivrajaka (wandering monk) years. He traveled extensively through the Narmada valley region of present-day Madhya Pradesh.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references):

> \
> 1892 \
> \
> Dear DlWANJI SAHEB, (Shri Haridas Viharidas Desai) \
> \
> The bearer of this letter, Babu Akshaya Kumar Ghose, is a particular friend of mine. He comes of a respectable \
> family of Calcutta. I found him at Khandwa where I made his acquaintance, although I knew his family long before \
> in Calcutta. \
> \
> He is a very honest and intelligent boy and is an undergraduate of the Calcutta University. You know how hard the \
> struggle is in Bengal nowadays, and the poor boy has been out in search of some job. Knowing your native \
> kindness of heart, I think I am not disturbing you by asking and entreating you to do something for this young man. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> Our congratulations for the successful carrying out of the Mahotsava (Big celebration of Shri Ramakrishna's birthday.) in \
> Madras. Hope you had a good gathering and plenty of spiritual food. We are all so glad that you have girded \
> yourself to teach more of spirituality to the Madras people than those finger twistings and klingphat (Cryptic Mantras \
> or sound formulae.) you are so fond of. Really your lecture on Shriji (Shri Ramakrishna.) was splendid. I could only catch \
> a report in the Madras Mail in Khandwa, and the Math people have not had any. Why don't you send us over a \
> copy? \
> \
> I leam that you complain about my silence, is it? I have written you more letters, however, than…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Ujjain

**Location:** Ujjain, Ujjain, India
**Period:** 1890
**Significance:** Sacred city of Mahakaleshwar


Ujjain, one of the seven sacred cities of Hinduism and home to the Mahakaleshwar Jyotirlinga, was visited by Vivekananda during his wandering years. He was deeply moved by this ancient center of learning and spirituality.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> \
> And to the south lie the deserts of Arabia \— the mother of heroes. Have you ever seen a Bedouin Arab, with a \
> cloak on, and a big kerchief tied on his head with a bunch of woollen strings? \— That gait, that pose of standing, \
> and that look, you will find in no other country. From head to foot emanates the freedom of open unconfined desert \
> air \— there you have the Arab. When the bigotry of the Christians and the barbarity of the Goths extinguished the \
> ancient Greek and Roman civilisation, when Persia was trying to hide her internal putrefaction by adding layer \
> after layer of gold-leaf upon it, when, in India, the sun of splendour of Pataliputra and Ujjain had set, leaving some…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

Maheshwar, the historic riverside town on the Narmada known as the domain of Queen Ahilyabai Holkar, was visited by Vivekananda. He revered the Narmada as a sacred river and spent time at the ancient ghats and temples.

### Mahabaleshwar

**Location:** Mahabaleshwar hill station, Mahabaleshwar, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Hill station rest


Mahabaleshwar, the famous hill station in the Sahyadri mountains, was visited by Vivekananda during his Maharashtra travels. The sacred origin of the Krishna river at Mahabaleshwar temple was of particular spiritual interest.


**From the Complete Works** (3 references):

> Margaon, \
> \
> 1893. \
> \
> \
> I just now received a letter from you. I reached here safe. I went to visit Panjim and a few other villages and \
> temples near by. I returned just today. I have not given up the intention of visiting Gokarna, Mahabaleshwar, and \
> other places. I start for Dharwar by the morning train tomorrow. I have taken the walking-stick with me. Doctor \
> Yagdekar's friend was very hospitable to me. Please give my compliments to Mr. Bhate and all others there. May \
> the Lord shower His blessings on you and your wife. The town of Panjim is very neat and clean. Most of the \
> Christians here are literate. The Hindus are mostly uneducated. \
> \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> Neutral Line, Poona, \
> 15th June, 1892. \
> \
> \
> It is a long time since I heard from you. I hope I have not offended you anyway. I came down with the Thakore \

> In the year 1877, while Naren was a student of the third
> class, his father went to Raipur in the Central Provinces. He
> arranged that his family should follow him later on under the
> charge of Naren. It was a long journey partly by bullock-cart
> via Allahabad and Jubbulpore through dense forests and over
> unfrequented roads, for the railways were in those days con-
> structed only up to Nagpur. An incident happened on the
> way which shows that his spiritual insight was deepening. He
> had had visions and many moods of spiritual consciousness ;
> this experience was induced by contemplating the beauties of
> nature.

Kodungalloor (ancient Cranganore), one of the oldest towns in India with connections to the earliest Jewish, Christian, and Muslim communities in India, was visited by Vivekananda. The Bhagavati temple here is among Kerala's most revered shrines.

> reached a pitch of frenzy. And the…

> heart was gladdened to see such tremendous religious zeal and

> most cordially with the temple priests and referred enthusiasti-

> died. In the evening he entrained for Kumbakonam. All along

### Pondicherry

**Location:** Pondicherry (Puducherry), Puducherry, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** South India coastal visit


Vivekananda visited Pondicherry, the French colonial enclave on the Coromandel coast, during his South India wanderings. The town's unique blend of French and Tamil cultures made it a distinctive stop on his South India journey.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> horrible story that the Lord was here for deluding demons with false doctrines! True indeed, but we are the demons, \
> not those that believed. And just as the Jews denied the Lord Jesus and are since that day wandering over the world \
> as homeless beggars, tyrannised over by everybody, so you are bond-slaves to any nation that thinks it worth while \
> to rule over you. Ah, tyrants! you do not know that the obverse is tyranny, and the reverse slavery. The slave and \
> the tyrant are synonymous. \
> \
> Balaji and G. G. may remember one evening at Pondicherry \— we were discussing the matter of sea-voyage with a \
> Pandit, and I shall always remember his brutal gestures and his Kadapi Na (never)!…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (1 references):

> XIX
> IN MADRAS AND HYDERABAD
>
> Ir was in the last days of the year 1892, when the Swami
> arose from his meditation at Kanydkumari, and wended his way
> to Madras, a centre of orthodox learning and culture. From
> the shrine of the Mother he went afoot, journeying through
> Ramnad and onwards, until he reached Pondicherry, south of
> Madras. Weary from long marches he rested there for some
> days, and met several young men who became his admirers
> and invited him to their homes.
>
> It was at Pondicherry that the Swami had a lengthy and
> bitter discussion with an exceedingly bigoted orthodox Pandit
> upon many important topics relating to Hinduism and its
> reform. The Pandit, being of the old school, antagonised…

*— p. 277*

### R.R. Sabha, Madras

**Location:** Raghunatha Rao's Sabha Hall, Triplicane, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1893
**Significance:** Public lecture before sailing to America


The R.R. Sabha (Raghunatha Rao Sabha) in Triplicane, Madras, was one of the venues where Vivekananda gave his famous lectures before departing for the Parliament of Religions. Madras supporters organized public meetings to raise funds and bid farewell to the Swami.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Castle Kernan, Madras

**Location:** Castle Kernan, Triplicane, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1893
**Significance:** Pre-departure gathering


Castle Kernan in Triplicane, Madras, was one of the important residences where Vivekananda stayed and received visitors before his departure to America in 1893. Madras supporters rallied around him during his preparations for the Parliament of Religions.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Margao

**Location:** Margao, Margao, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Wandering through Goa


Margao (Madgaon), the commercial capital of Goa, was visited by Vivekananda during his South India wanderings. His passage through the former Portuguese colony exposed him to the blend of Indian and European cultures unique to Goa.


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (10 references):

### Sitakund

**Location:** Sitakund, Chittagong, Sitakund, Pre-Independence India
**Period:** 1890
**Significance:** Ancient Shiva pilgrimage


Sitakund (Chandranath Hills) near Chittagong, one of the most ancient pilgrimage sites in Bengal, was visited by Vivekananda. The hilltop Chandranath Shiva temple and the sacred fire that burns perpetually here held deep religious significance.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Sandakphu

**Location:** Sandakphu Peak, Sandakphu, Nepal
**Period:** 1888
**Significance:** Highest point of West Bengal


Sandakphu on the Nepal-India border, the highest peak in West Bengal, was visited by Vivekananda during his early Himalayan wanderings. From here one can see four of the world's five highest peaks. The rarified atmosphere and Himalayan grandeur deeply inspired the young monk.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> \
> \
> Darjeeling, \
> 23 rd April, 1898. \
> \
> \
> My health was excellent on my return from Sandukphu (11,924 ft.) and other places; but after returning to \
> Darjeeling, I had first an attack of fever, and after recovering from that, I am now suffering from cough and cold. I \
> try to escape from this place every day; but they have been constantly putting it off for a long time. However, \
> tomorrow, Sunday, I am leaving; after halting at Kharsana for a day I start again for Calcutta on Monday. I shall \
> send you a wire after starting. We should hold an annual meeting of the Ramakrishna Mission, and also one for the \
> Math. In both the meetings the accounts of famine relief must be submitted, and…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Bareilly

**Location:** Bareilly, Bareilly, India
**Period:** 1888
**Significance:** North India wandering


Bareilly in the Rohilkhand region of Uttar Pradesh was visited by Vivekananda during his wandering years. The city near the foothills of the Himalayas was a stop on his northward journey through the Gangetic plain.


**From the Complete Works** (4 references):

> \
> ON THE WAY TO BARAMULLA \
> \
> PERSONS: The Swami Vivekananda, Gurubhais, and a party of Europeans and disciples, amongst whom were Dhira \
> Mata, the "Steady Mother"; one whose name was Jaya; and Nivedita. \
> \
> Place: From Bareilly to Baramulla, Kashmir. \
> \
> Time: June 14 to 20, 1898. \
> \
> \
> June 14. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Dear Tulsiram:! \
> \
> A basket of roses will be sent to you in a few days at Chitpur. Do you please send them up immediately to Shashi \
> [Swami Ramakrishnananda, at the Baranagore Math]. They would not be sent to the care of Balaram Basu, for \
> there would be such nice delays and that would be death to the flowers. I think if sent to Chitpur, to your depot, it \
> would reach you there at the very place; if not, write sharp. Baburam [Swami Premananda] is here, going up in a \
> day or two to Allahabad. I too am going off from this place very soon. I go perhaps to Bareilly and up. What is \
> Balaram Babu [Balaram Bose] doing? \
> \
> My Pranams etc. to you all. \
> \
> \
> Yours affectionately, \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> audience in English, and another to the Indian residents in Hindi. This was my maiden speech in Hindi, but \
> everyone liked it for all that. Of course the Westerners, as is their wont, were in raptures over it, as coming from a \
> "nigger"! "Oh, how wonderful!" and that sort of thing. Next Saturday there will be another lecture for the \
> Europeans. A big Association has been set on foot here \— let us wait and see how far it works in future. The object \
> of the Association is to impart education and religion. \
> \
> Monday next, trip to Bareilly then to Saharanpur, next to Ambala, thence, most probably, to Mussoorie with \
> Captain Sevier, and as soon as it is a little cool, return to the plains…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> places in the Punjab and Kashmir, came down to the plains.

> composed of the distinguished residents of the city cordially
> welcomed him and took him and his party to their club-house,
> where arrangements had been made for their stay. He had
> hardly arrived when he was attacked with fever. He remained
> in Bareilly four days, and though ill all the time, gave much
> time to religious discourse. On the morning of the tenth he
> visited the Arya Sam4j Orphanage, and on the next day as the
> result of an impressive conversation with a gathering of students

### Belgaum (Belagavi)

**Location:** Belgaum (Belagavi), Belagavi, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** South India journey


Belgaum (now Belagavi) in northern Karnataka was visited by Vivekananda during his South India journey. The town at the edge of the Deccan plateau was an important stop as he traversed the route between Maharashtra and Karnataka.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> \
> Bombay, \
> 24th May, 1893. \
> \
> \
> Very glad to receive your letter and that of dear Haripada. Please do not be sorry that I could not write to you very \
> often. I am always praying to the Lord for your welfare. I cannot go to Belgaum now as arrangements are all ready \
> for my starting for America on the 31st next. The Lord willing, I shall see you on returning from my travels in \
> America and Europe. Always resign yourselves to the Lord Shri Krishna. Always remember that we are but \
> puppets in the Lord's hands. Remain pure always. Please be careful not to become impure even in thought, as also \
> in speech and action; always try to do good to others as far as in you lies. And remember that…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (8 references):

> intention, for he entered MahasamAadhi in the midst of his work.

> Khangi Karbhari, a high state official of Kolhapur, gave the
> Swami a letter of introduction to a Mahratta gentleman of
> Belgaum. One morning at about 6 o'clock in 1892, the Swami
> went to Belgaum. We give below some reminiscences (slightly

### Ernakulam (Kochi)

**Location:** Ernakulam, Kochi, Kochi, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Kerala visit


Ernakulam, the mainland part of Kochi (Cochin), was visited by Vivekananda during his Kerala journey. The gateway to the backwaters and the historic port city of Kerala exposed him to the unique culture and Vedic traditions of this southernmost state.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Panaji, Goa

**Location:** Panaji (Panjim), Panaji, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Goa visit


Panaji, the capital of the former Portuguese colony of Goa, was visited by Vivekananda. He observed the unique Goan culture blending Indian and Portuguese traditions, and visited the famous Basilica of Bom Jesus containing the relics of St. Francis Xavier.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*

### Somnath Temple

**Location:** Somnath, Saurashtra, Somnath, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** First Jyotirlinga


Somnath, the first and most revered of the twelve Jyotirlingas of Lord Shiva, was a major pilgrimage destination for Vivekananda during his Gujarat wanderings. He was deeply moved by the historic significance of this temple, repeatedly destroyed and rebuilt over millennia, as a symbol of India's resilience.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> vitality in religion, but for good or evil it is there; you cannot get out of it, you have it now and for ever, and you \
> have to stand by it, even if you have not the same faith that I have in our religion. You are bound by it, and if you \
> give it up, you are smashed to pieces. That is the life of our race and that must be strengthened. You have \
> withstood the shocks of centuries simply because you took great care of it, you sacrificed everything else for it. \
> Your forefathers underwent everything boldly, even death itself, but preserved their religion. Temple alter temple \
> was broken down by the foreign conqueror, but no sooner had the wave passed than the spire of the temple rose up…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (2 references):

> then he was off again to Verawal and Patan Somnath, popularly
> known as Prabhas. Verawal's title to fame is its antiquity ;
> Patan Somnath’s lies in its great ruined temple. ‘Three times
> it, was destroyed, and three times rebuilt. It is said that in
> olden times ten thousand villages were held by the temple as
> its endowment, and that three hundred musicians were attached
> to it. The Swami paused at this great…

### Puri — Jagannath Dham

**Location:** Jagannath Temple, Puri, Puri, India
**Period:** 1889
**Significance:** One of the four dhams


Puri on the Bay of Bengal, home to the Jagannath Temple and one of the four sacred dhams of Hinduism, was a major pilgrimage destination for Vivekananda. He was deeply moved by the democratic spirit of Jagannath worship that transcended caste distinctions.


**From the Complete Works** (331 references):

> \
> Others stood beside the Swami Vivekananda, on the same platform as he, as apostles of particular creeds and \
> churches. But it was his glory that he came to preach a religion to which each of these was, in his own words, "only \
> a travelling, a coming up, of different men, and women, through various conditions and circumstances to the same \
> goal". He stood there, as he declared, to tell of One who had said of them all, not that one or another was true, in \
> this or that respect, or for this or that reason, but that "All these are threaded upon Me, as pearls upon a string. \
> Wherever thou seest extraordinary holiness and extraordinary power, raising and purifying humanity, know thou…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> single day!
>
> A wandering monk who hitherto had never stayed at a place
> for more than three days, Totapuri remained at Dakshineswar
> for eleven months, imparting his knowledge to his wonderful
> disciple, setting him firmly on the lofty heights of Advaita. The
> disciple, in his turn, became the guide of his teacher and
> enlightened him regarding the reality of the personal aspects
> of Truth which Totapuri had hitherto refused to recognise.
>
> Meanwhile strange stories that he was mad were current in
> his native village. His wife Saradamani Devi resolved to learn

---

### Bhubaneswar

**Location:** Bhubaneswar (Ekamra Kshetra), Bhubaneswar, India
**Period:** 1889
**Significance:** City of temples


Bhubaneswar, the ancient city of temples in Odisha, was visited by Vivekananda during his wandering pilgrimage. The city with its hundreds of temples representing the entire arc of Hindu temple architecture moved him deeply with its concentration of sacred shrines.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Visakhapatnam

**Location:** Visakhapatnam, Visakhapatnam, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Coastal stop, South India tour


Visakhapatnam (Vizag) on the Coromandel coast was a stop during Vivekananda's South India journey. He traveled along the eastern coast of India, meeting devotees and scholars in this important coastal city.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Tirupati

**Location:** Tirumala Venkateswara Temple, Tirupati, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Most visited Hindu pilgrimage


Tirupati, home to the Tirumala Venkateswara Temple on the Tirumala hills, was visited by Vivekananda during his South India pilgrimage. The temple is the world's most visited pilgrimage site, and Vivekananda paid homage to this ancient Vaishnava shrine.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Madurai — Meenakshi Amman

**Location:** Meenakshi Amman Temple, Madurai, Madurai, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Greatest Dravidian temple


Vivekananda visited Madurai and the magnificent Meenakshi Amman temple during his South India pilgrimage. The spectacular Dravidian temple with its towering gopurams (gate towers) made a profound impression on him, representing the pinnacle of South Indian religious art.


**From the Complete Works** (7 references):

> I wish I could live in your midst for several days and fulfil the conditions that have just been pointed out by your \
> most worthy Chairman of relating to you my experiences in the West and the result of all my labours there for the \
> last four years. But, unfortunately, even Swamis have bodies; and the continuous travelling and speaking that I \
> have had to undergo for the last three weeks make it impossible for me to deliver a very long speech this evening. I \
> will, therefore, satisfy myself with thanking you very cordially for the kindness that has been shown to me, and \
> reserve other things for some day in the future when under better conditions of health we shall have time to talk…

### 259, Ram Bagh, Meerut

**Location:** 259, Ram Bagh, Meerut, India
**Period:** 1890–1891
**Significance:** Second Baranagar Math


The house at 259, Ram Bagh, Meerut served as the second Baranagar Math when Vivekananda and several brother-disciples gathered here during the wandering years. They lived and practised together in this garden house — a brief but significant period of collective monastic life during Narendra's parivrajaka years.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

### Naini Tal

**Location:** Nainital, Nainital, India
**Period:** 1890, 1898
**Significance:** Himalayan journey — Kumaon hills


Vivekananda arrived at Nainital on 29 August 1890 with his guru-brother Swami Akhandananda, at the start of their Himalayan wandering. From here they journeyed on foot to Almora, Kakrighat, Rudraprayag, Srinagar (Garhwal) and Tehri. In May 1898, Vivekananda again passed through Nainital with his Western disciples — including Sister Nivedita — on their way to Almora and Mayavati, where the Seviers had established the Advaita Ashrama.


**From the Complete Works** (12 references):

> Time: May 11 to May 25, 1898. \
> \
> \
> We were a large party, or, indeed, two parties, that left Howrah station on Wednesday evening and on Friday \
> morning came in sight of the Himalayas. . . . \
> \
> Naini Tal was made beautiful by three things \— the Master's pleasure in introducing to us his disciple the Raja of \
> Khetri; the dancing girls who met us and asked us where to find him, and were received by him in spite of the \
> remonstrances of others; and by the Mohammedan gentleman who said, "Swamiji, if in after-times any claim you \
> as an Avatara, an especial incarnation of the Deity \— remember that I, a Mohammedan, am the first!" \
> \
> It was here too that we heard a long talk on Ram Mohan…

### Kakrighat

**Location:** Kakrighat, Kosi River, Bhowali, India
**Period:** 1890
**Significance:** Profound spiritual realisation


On the banks of the Kosi river between Nainital and Almora, Vivekananda meditated under a Peepal tree at Kakrighat in 1890 and experienced a profound realisation of the unity of the macrocosm and the microcosm — that the entire universe resides within every living being. This experience directly shaped his later celebrated lectures on 'Cosmos — Microcosm and Macrocosm'. The spot remains a place of pilgrimage for Vivekananda devotees today.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> MACROCOSM AND MICROCOSM \
> \
> \{The Life ofSwami Vivekananda, Vol. I. p. 250.) \
> \
> \
> After his experience of the macrocosm within the microcosm while absorbed in meditation under the peepul tree at \
> Kakrighat, in 1890, Swami Vivekananda jotted down in Bengali fragments of his realization in his notebook. \
> \
> In the beginning was the Word etc. \
> \
> The microcosm and the macrocosm are built on the same plan. Just as the individual soul is encased in the living \
> body, so is the universal Soul in the Living Prakriti [Nature] \— the objective universe. Shiva [i.e. Kali] is \
> embracing Shiva: this is not a fancy. This covering of the one [Soul] by the other [Nature] is analogous to the \

*— NOTES \*

### Garhwal

**Location:** Garhwal Himalayas, Pauri, India
**Period:** 1890
**Significance:** Himalayan wandering — Garhwal region


Vivekananda traversed the Garhwal Himalayas during his 1890 parivrajaka journey, passing through the sacred river confluences and mountain towns of the region. Living solely on madhukari (food obtained by begging), he and brother-monk Swami Akhandananda spent weeks amid the Garhwal forests and peaks in prayer, austerity and deep meditation, absorbing the spiritual energy of this ancient land.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

### Karna Prayag

**Location:** Karnaprayag, Karnaprayag, India
**Period:** 1890
**Significance:** 18-day meditation retreat


At Karnaprayag — the sacred confluence of the Alaknanda and Pindar rivers — Vivekananda stayed and meditated for eighteen days with his brother-monks Swami Turiyananda and Swami Akhandananda. The serenity of the river confluence and the surrounding Garhwal peaks provided the perfect atmosphere for intense spiritual practice and austerities. It was one of the longest halts of his Himalayan wandering in 1890.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (1 references):

> fever whilst staying in a Chati, or halting place for pilgrims.

### Rudra prayag

**Location:** Rudraprayag, Rudraprayag, India
**Period:** 1890
**Significance:** Meditation at sacred Prayag


At Rudraprayag — the confluence of the Alaknanda and Mandakini rivers — Vivekananda paused during his 1890 Himalayan wandering in the company of Swami Akhandananda and Swami Saradananda. The peace and grandeur of this sacred junction fostered deep meditation and scriptural study. The Panch Prayags of Garhwal held profound significance for Vivekananda as living symbols of the unity underlying all creation.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*

### Srinagar, Uttarkand

**Location:** Srinagar, Garhwal, Srinagar, India
**Period:** 1890
**Significance:** Retreat with brother-monks


Srinagar in Pauri Garhwal, on the banks of the Alaknanda river, was a significant halt during Vivekananda's 1890 Himalayan wandering. He spent several days here with Swami Akhandananda and Swami Saradananda in prayer, meditation and study of the scriptures. The ancient town, nestled among the Garhwal hills, offered the solitude and spiritual atmosphere that Vivekananda sought in his years of parivrajaka (wandering ascetic) life.


**From the Complete Works** (32 references):

> To Swami Brahmananda \
> \
> \
> My Dear Rakhal, \
> \
> \
> Srinagar, \
> 17th July, 1898. \
> \
> \

### Karbala stone

**Location:** Bhowali-Almora Road, Satyadi Village, Almora, India
**Period:** 1890
**Significance:** Symbol of interfaith harmony


A memorial stone on the Bhowali–Almora road marks the spot where Vivekananda, exhausted after walking all the way from Kathgodam, collapsed. A Muslim fakir named Zulfikar Ali found him and revived him with cucumber and water — an act of compassion that moved Vivekananda deeply. The Karbala stone stands as a living symbol of the interfaith harmony and universal brotherhood that lay at the heart of Vivekananda's teachings. It remains a place of pilgrimage and morning sessions for Vivekananda devotees.

### Sri Raghunath mandir

**Location:** Raghunath Temple, Almora, Almora, India
**Period:** 1890, 1897
**Significance:** Ancient Vaishnava temple visited by Vivekananda


The Sri Raghunath Mandir is one of the oldest and most revered Vaishnava temples in Almora, dedicated to Lord Rama. Vivekananda visited the temple during his wandering years in 1890 and again during his lecture tour of 1897. The ancient temple, nestled in the heart of the Kumaon hills, resonated with his vision of the perennial spirituality woven into every corner of India.

### Nadiad

**Location:** Nadiad, Nadiad, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Halt during Vivekananda's wandering pilgrimage through Gujarat


Nadiad, a historic town in the Kheda district of Gujarat, was one of the stops Swami Vivekananda made during his extensive wandering pilgrimage through western India in 1892. Travelling incognito as a wandering monk, he absorbed the religious and cultural life of Gujarat before proceeding toward Porbandar and Kathiawar.


**From the Complete Works** (4 references):

> \
> PS. I depend on you to remove any misconception in the mind of your brother about my not getting down and that, \
> even had I been the very devil, I could not forget their kindness and good offices for me. \
> \
> As to the other two Swamis, they were my Gurubhais, who went to you last at Junagad; of them one is our leader. I \
> met them after three years, and we came together as far as Abu and then I left them. If you wish, I can take them \
> back to Nadiad on my way to Bombay. May the Lord shower His blessings on you and yours. \
> \
> \
> Yours, \
> \
> V. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Very happy to receive your kind letter even here. I had not the least difficulty in reaching your house from the \
> station of Nadiad. And your brothers, they are what they should be, your brothers. May the Lord shower his \
> choicest blessings on your family. I have never found such a glorious one in all my travels. Your friend Mr. \

> 28th April, 1893. \

> On my way here, I wanted to go to your place at Nadiad and redeem my pledge, but certain circumstances \
> prevented me, and the greatest of them was that you were not there; and to play Hamlet leaving Hamlet's part out is \
> a ridiculous affair; and as I know for certain that you are to return in a few days to Nadiad, and as I am shortly \
> going back to Bombay, say in 20 days, I thought it better to postpone my visit for that time. \
> \
> Here the Khetri Rajaji was very, very anxious to see me and had sent his Private Secretary to Madras; and so I was \
> bound to leave for Khetri. But the heat is quite intolerable, and so I am flying off very soon. \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Central Provinces - Forest

**Location:** Central Provinces Forest Region, Central Provinces, India
**Period:** 1888-1893
**Significance:** Forest retreat and meditation during Vivekananda's wandering years


During his years of wandering across India (1888-1893), Swami Vivekananda passed through the dense forests of the Central Provinces (present-day Madhya Pradesh and Chhattisgarh). These forest sojourns afforded him deep solitude for meditation and contemplation. Living as a penniless monk, dependent entirely on alms, he gained profound insight into the poverty and spiritual hunger of the Indian masses.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references):

> 10th October, 1897. \
> \
> \
> I am sorry to learn from your letter that you are not doing well. If you can make an unpopular man popular, then I \
> call you a clever fellow. There is no prospect of work there in the future; it would have been better had you gone \
> rather to Dacca, or some other place. However, it is a good thing that the work will close in November. If you get \
> very badly off in health, you should better come away. There is much field for work in the Central Provinces; and \
> even without famine, there is no lack of poverty-stricken people in our country. Wherever it is, if you can choose a \
> site with an eye to prospect, you are sure to turn out good work. However, be not…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> all a musical instrument was formed with a string fixed to a piece of bamboo. Gradually it came to be played by a \

> strings and guts, and the bow also assumed different fonns and names, till at last the highly finished guitar and \
> sarang etc., came into existence. But in spite of this, do not the Mohammedan cabmen even now with a shabby \
> horsehair bow play on the crude instrument made of a bamboo pipe fixed to an earthen pot, and sing the story of \
> Majwar…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Manmathanath Bhattacharya's House

**Location:** Triplicane, Chennai, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** First lodging in Madras during Vivekananda's 1892 visit


In late 1892, Swami Vivekananda arrived in Madras (Chennai) and was first hosted at the residence of Manmathanath Bhattacharya, a Bengali civil servant living in Triplicane. It was here that Vivekananda first met the young men who would become his most devoted Madras disciples — Alasinga Perumal, G.G. Narasimhacharyar, and others — who were captivated by his towering intellect and spiritual fire and urged him to go to America.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### G.G. Narasimhacharyar's House

**Location:** Triplicane, Chennai, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1892-1893
**Significance:** Home of a key Madras disciple who supported Vivekananda's America mission


G.G. Narasimhacharyar was among the devoted young men of Madras who gathered around Swami Vivekananda during his 1892-93 stay. His house was one of the gathering places where Vivekananda held informal discourses on Vedanta and Indian philosophy. Narasimhacharyar actively supported the effort to raise funds and write letters of introduction for Vivekananda's projected voyage to America.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Harmston Grand Circus Pavilion

**Location:** Triplicane, Chennai, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1893
**Significance:** Venue of Vivekananda's farewell address before sailing to America


The Harmston Grand Circus Pavilion in Madras served as the venue for a grand public farewell meeting held in honour of Swami Vivekananda before his departure for the Parliament of Religions in Chicago. His Madras devotees organised the gathering, where he delivered a rousing address. The enthusiastic support from the people of Madras gave him the moral courage and confidence to represent Hinduism on the world stage in 1893.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Justice Subramaniam Iyer's Beach House

**Location:** Marina Beach, Chennai, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1893
**Significance:** Beach house where Vivekananda contemplated his mission before sailing to America


Justice P. Subramania Iyer, a prominent High Court judge and admirer of Vivekananda, offered his beach house on the Marina as a quiet retreat. It was here, gazing at the vast expanse of the sea, that Swami Vivekananda meditated on his forthcoming mission to the West. Local tradition holds that he swam out to a rock off the Cape Comorin coast in meditation — and the Madras beach house was one of the contemplative spaces during those decisive days of early 1893.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Sashi Vilas - Nanjunda Rao's House

**Location:** Triplicane, Chennai, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1892-1893
**Significance:** Residence of supporter Nanjunda Rao who hosted Vivekananda


Sashi Vilas was the home of Nanjunda Rao, one of the Madras devotees who offered hospitality and support to Swami Vivekananda during his stay in Madras in 1892-93. These private homes of devoted supporters formed the network that sustained Vivekananda during his wandering years and helped crystallise the plan for his momentous journey to the Parliament of Religions in Chicago.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Yogi Parthasarathy's House

**Location:** Triplicane, Chennai, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1892-1893
**Significance:** Home of a Madras devotee who gathered around Vivekananda


Yogi Parthasarathy was one of the earnest spiritual seekers in Madras who came into contact with Swami Vivekananda during his visits to the city. His residence was among the gathering places where Vivekananda's disciples met to discuss Vedanta and organise support for his mission. The circle of devoted young men in Triplicane formed the earliest Madras sangha of Vivekananda.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Balaji Rao's House

**Location:** Triplicane, Chennai, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1892-1893
**Significance:** Home of a devoted Madras supporter of Vivekananda


Balaji Rao was among the devoted householder supporters of Swami Vivekananda in Madras. His home in Triplicane was one of the informal meeting points where Vivekananda held intimate discussions on Vedanta philosophy. These households of Tamil devotees played a crucial role in sustaining Vivekananda during his wandering years and in spreading his message across South India.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Alasinga Perumal's House

**Location:** Triplicane, Chennai, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1892-1893
**Significance:** Home of Vivekananda's most devoted Madras disciple


P. Aiyasami Alasinga Perumal was Swami Vivekananda's most devoted Madras disciple. His house in Triplicane served as a hub of activity for the Madras group. Alasinga tirelessly wrote letters to Americans on Vivekananda's behalf, helped raise funds, and edited the journal 'Brahmavadin'. Vivekananda affectionately called him 'my Alasinga' and credited him with doing more for his American mission than any other single person.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Patters Garden

**Location:** Royapettah, Chennai, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1892-1893
**Significance:** Garden residence in Royapettah associated with Vivekananda's Madras stay


Patters Garden in the Royapettah area of Madras was one of the venues associated with Swami Vivekananda's extended stay in the city. During his months in Madras (late 1892 to early 1893), he moved between several residences of supporters, and garden bungalows like this offered a congenial atmosphere for informal discourses and deep philosophical conversations with his growing circle of devotees.


**From the Complete Works** (4 references):

> ills normal size. These symptoms subsided, however, as soon as I reached the Math. It is dreadfully hot this year; \
> but a bit of rain has commenced, and I hope we will soon have the monsoon in full force. I have no plans just now, \
> except that the Bombay Presidency wants me so badly that I think of going there soon. We are thinking of starting \
> touring through Bombay in a week or so. \
> \
> The 300 dollars you speak of sent by Lady Betty have not reached me yet, nor have I any intimation of its arrival \
> from General Patterson. \
> \
> He, poor man, was rather miserable, after his wife and children sailed for Europe and asked me to come and see \
> him, but unfortunately I was so ill, and am so…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Triplicane Literary Society

**Location:** Triplicane, Chennai, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1892-1893
**Significance:** Forum where Vivekananda addressed Madras intellectuals


The Triplicane Literary Society was a prominent intellectual forum in Madras where Swami Vivekananda addressed an audience of educated Tamil youth and scholars. His lectures on Vedanta, Indian philosophy, and the necessity of a spiritual revival captivated the audience. The society provided a formal platform to complement the informal gatherings in private homes, helping to spread his ideas among the English-educated elite of Madras.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE WORK BEFORE US \
> \
> (.Delivered at the Triplicane Literary Society, Madras ) \
> \
> The problem of life is becoming deeper and broader every day as the world moves on. The watchword and the \
> essence have been preached in the days of yore when the Vedantic truth was first discovered, the solidarity of all \
> life. One atom in this universe cannot move without dragging the whole world along with it. There cannot be any \
> progress without the whole world following in the wake, and it is becoming every day dearer that the solution of \
> any problem can never be attained on racial, or national, or narrow grounds. Every idea has to become broad till it \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (1 references):

> (1) My plan of campaign
>
> (2) The Sages of India
>
> (3) Vedanta in its relation to practical life
> (4) The future of India.
>
> The Swami approved of the programme. He also consented
> to deliver an address to the Triplicane Literary Society on
> “Some aspects of my work in India”. In addition, he gave
> two morning sittings at the Castle to meet people who desired
> to ask him questions.
>
> The Swami’s stay in Madras was a nine days’ festival, a
> veritable “Navaratri”. Altogether twenty-four addresses were
> presented to Swami Vivekananda, in English, Sanskrit, Tamil
> and Telugu. He also presided at the Annual Mecting of the

---

### Justice Subramaniam Iyer's House

**Location:** Mylapore, Chennai, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1893
**Significance:** Residence of Justice Subramania Iyer who supported Vivekananda's America voyage


Justice P. Subramania Iyer's residence in Madras was one of the important stops for Swami Vivekananda in the city. As one of the most distinguished supporters in Madras, Justice Iyer lent his prestige and encouragement to the project of sending Vivekananda to represent Hinduism at the Parliament of Religions in Chicago. His patronage helped legitimise the mission in the eyes of conservative Tamil society.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Nagercoil

**Location:** Nagercoil, Nagercoil, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Southernmost stop on Vivekananda's wandering pilgrimage


Nagercoil, the southernmost town of Tamil Nadu near the tip of the Indian subcontinent, was visited by Swami Vivekananda during his wandering pilgrimage through the South in 1892. Named after the ancient Nagaraja (serpent king) temple, the town was one of the stops on his route as he made his way toward Kanyakumari — the land's end of India — where he would have his most transformative meditation on the rock in the sea.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Rameshwaram

**Location:** Rameshwaram, Rameshwaram, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Sacred pilgrimage island visited by Vivekananda


Rameshwaram, one of the holiest pilgrimage sites in India, was visited by Swami Vivekananda during his wandering pilgrimage through South India in 1892. The island shrine of Ramanathaswamy, connected to the mainland by the famous Pamban Bridge, is one of the four sacred dhams of Hinduism. Vivekananda's visit here deepened his direct experience of the living spirituality of the masses of India.


**From the Complete Works** (8 references):

> His Most Holiness, \
> \
> Sri Paramahamsa, Yati-Raja, Digvijaya-Kolahala, Sarvamata-Sampratipanna, Parama-Yogeeswara, Srimat \
> Bhagavan Sree Ramakrishna Paramahamsa Karakamala Sanjata, Rajadhiraja-Sevita, SREE VIVEKANANDA SWAMI, \
> May It Please Your Holiness, \
> \
> We, the inhabitants of this ancient and historic Samsthanam of Sethu Bandha Rameswaram, otherwise known as \
> Ramanathapuram or Ramnad, beg, most cordially, to welcome you to this, our motherland. We deem it a very rare \
> privilege to be the first to pay your Holiness our heartfelt homage on your landing in India, and that, on the shores \
> sanctified by the footsteps of that great Hero and our revered Lord \— Sree Bhagavan Ramachandra.…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> anybody. I have neither the strength nor the money, nor the will to take up anybody with me. Bhaktas (devotees) of \

> again, I am more dead than alive, and strictly refuse to see anybody. If you cannot manage this, I don't go to \
> Madras. I have to become a bit selfish to save my…

### Valangaiman, Kumbakonam

**Location:** Valangaiman near Kumbakonam, Kumbakonam, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Village near Kumbakonam visited during Vivekananda's South India tour


Valangaiman is a small town in the Tiruvarur district near Kumbakonam in the Cauvery delta. During his wandering pilgrimage through Tamil Nadu in 1892, Swami Vivekananda passed through the sacred Cauvery delta region, visiting temple towns and villages. The fertile delta, home to ancient Shaivite and Vaishnavite temples, gave Vivekananda deep insight into the rich religious culture of Tamil Nadu.

### Kunthukal

**Location:** Kunthukal, Ramanathapuram, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Stop on Vivekananda's route through the Ramnad region


Kunthukal is a village in the Ramanathapuram district of Tamil Nadu. During his wandering pilgrimage through the South in 1892, Swami Vivekananda passed through the Ramnad region under the patronage of the Raja of Ramnad, Bhaskara Setupathi, who was one of his most generous supporters in South India. The Raja's influence extended across this region and helped Vivekananda on his journey toward Rameshwaram and Kanyakumari.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> \
> Sri Paramahamsa, Yati-Raja, Digvijaya-Kolahala, Sarvamata-Sampratipanna, Parama-Yogeeswara, Srimat \

> May It Please Your Holiness, \
> \
> We, the inhabitants of this ancient and historic Samsthanam of Sethu Bandha Rameswaram, otherwise known as \
> Ramanathapuram or Ramnad, beg, most cordially, to welcome you to this, our motherland. We deem it a very rare \
> privilege to be the first to pay your Holiness our heartfelt homage on your landing in India, and that, on the shores \
> sanctified by the footsteps of that great Hero and our revered Lord \— Sree Bhagavan Ramachandra. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Pamban

**Location:** Pamban Island, Pamban, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Island gateway to Rameshwaram on Vivekananda's pilgrimage


Pamban, the gateway island to Rameshwaram, was visited by Swami Vivekananda during his pilgrimage to the sacred island shrine in 1892. Pamban island on the Palk Strait separates the Indian mainland from Sri Lanka and forms the approach to Rameshwaram. The Pamban passage — later spanned by the famous Pamban Bridge — was traversed by Vivekananda on his sacred journey to Rameshwaram on foot and by boat.

> pandal which had been decorated with great taste, the following address on behalf of the Pamban people was read: \

### Sandukphu

**Location:** Sandukphu, Sandakphu, Nepal
**Period:** 1888-1893
**Significance:** Himalayan peak on the Nepal-India border visited during wandering years


Sandukphu (Sandakphu), the highest peak in the Darjeeling Himalayan region on the Nepal-India border, was among the Himalayan heights visited by Swami Vivekananda during his years of wandering as a parivrajaka. The breathtaking panorama of the four highest peaks in the world — Kanchenjunga, Everest, Lhotse, and Makalu — visible from its summit, deepened his sense of the infinite and the divine.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> \
> \
> Darjeeling, \
> 23 rd April, 1898. \
> \
> \
> My health was excellent on my return from Sandukphu (11,924 ft.) and other places; but after returning to \
> Darjeeling, I had first an attack of fever, and after recovering from that, I am now suffering from cough and cold. I \
> try to escape from this place every day; but they have been constantly putting it off for a long time. However, \
> tomorrow, Sunday, I am leaving; after halting at Kharsana for a day I start again for Calcutta on Monday. I shall \
> send you a wire after starting. We should hold an annual meeting of the Ramakrishna Mission, and also one for the \
> Math. In both the meetings the accounts of famine relief must be submitted, and…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---


## Phase: First Western Tour & Parliament of Religions (1893–1897)

*Vivekananda's historic journey to the West — through Ceylon, Malaysia, Singapore, China, Japan and Canada — culminating in his electrifying speech at the Parliament of World's Religions in Chicago on 11 September 1893. He spent nearly four years spreading Vedanta across the USA and England.*


---

### Cochin (Kochi)

**Location:** Ernakulam / Fort Cochin, Kochi, India
**Period:** 31 May 1893
**Significance:** Embarkation point for the West


Vivekananda set sail from Cochin on 31 May 1893, beginning his historic first voyage to the West. He boarded the ship Peninsular here, heading for Colombo and then onward to Chicago via the Far East.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*

### Colombo, Sri Lanka

**Location:** Colombo Harbour, Colombo, Sri Lanka
**Period:** 1893 (outward); 15 January 1897 (return)
**Significance:** First reception on return journey


Vivekananda arrived at Colombo on his outward voyage to the Parliament of Religions (1893), and again triumphantly on 15 January 1897 — his first landing in Asia on his return from the West. Thousands of devotees greeted him at the dock; the reception was overwhelming.


**From the Complete Works** (58 references):

> \
> SRIMAT VIVEKANANDA SWAMI \
> \
> \
> Revered Sir, \
> \
> In pursuance of a resolution passed at a public meeting of the Hindus of the city of Colombo, we beg to offer you a \
> hearty welcome to this Island. We deem it a privilege to be the first to welcome you on your return home from \
> your great mission in the West. \
> \

### Jaffna, Sri Lanka

**Location:** Northern Sri Lanka, Jaffna, Sri Lanka
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Return tour lectures


Vivekananda visited Jaffna in the north of Ceylon (Sri Lanka) during his 1897 return tour. He delivered powerful public lectures to large audiences, emphasising the strength and glory of the Hindu tradition.


**From the Complete Works** (4 references):

> May God, who has hitherto crowned your noble work with conspicuous success, spare you long, giving you vigour \
> and strength to continue your noble Mission. \
> \
> \
> We remain, Revered Sir, \
> Yours faithfully, \
> for and on behalf of the HINDUS OF JAFFNA. \
> \
> An eloquent reply was given, and on the following evening the Swami lectured on Vedantism, a report of which is \
> here appended: \
> \
> The subject is very large and the time is short; a full analysis of the religion of the Hindus is impossible in one \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> wrested from them in a short time and placed a Hindu king on the throne. Then came hordes of Europeans \— the \
> Spaniards, the Portuguese, and the Dutch. Lastly the English have made themselves kings. The royal family of \
> Kandy have been sent to Tanjore, where they are living on pension and Mulagutanni Rasam. \
> \
> In northern Ceylon there is a great majority of Hindus, while in the southern part, Buddhists and hybrid Eurasians \
> of different types preponderate. The principal seat of the Buddhists is Colombo, the present capital, and that of the \
> Hindus is Jaffna. The restrictions of caste are here much less than in India; the Buddhists have a few in marriage \
> affairs, but none in matters…

### Kandy, Sri Lanka

**Location:** Temple of the Tooth, Kandy, Sri Lanka
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Buddhism's sacred city


Vivekananda visited Kandy, home of the famous Temple of the Tooth Relic of the Buddha. He reflected on the complementarity of Buddhism and Hinduism and delivered lectures on universal religion.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references):

> her boarding school for Buddhist girls. I also visited the monastery and school of our old acquaintance, the \
> Countess of Canovara. The Countess' house is more spacious and furnished than Mrs. Higgins's. The Countess has \
> invested her own money, whereas Mrs. Higgins has collected the money by begging. The Countess herself wears a \
> Gerua cloth after the mode of the Bengali Sari. The Ceylonese Buddhists have taken a great fancy to this fashion, I \
> found. I noticed carriage after carriage of women, all wearing the same Bengali Sari. \
> \
> The principal place of pilgrimage for the Buddhists is the Dalada Maligawa or Tooth-temple at Kandy, which \
> contains a tooth of Lord Buddha. The Ceylonese…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> open maidan, not on anybody's property \— when a whole host of Buddhist monks and laymen, men and women, \
> came out beating drums and cymbals and set up an awful uproar. The lecture had to stop, of course, and there was \
> the imminent risk of bloodshed. With great difficulty I had to persuade the Hindus that we at any rate might \
> practise a bit of non-injury, if they did not. Then the matter ended peacefully. \
> \
> Gradually Tamilian Hindus from the north began slowly to migrate into Ceylon. The Buddhists, finding themselves \
> in untoward circumstances, left their capital to establish a hill-station called Kandy, which, too, the Tamilians \
> wrested from them in a short time and placed a…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (1 references):

> Indians had appeared. He was evidently annoyed, and feeling
> it his duty, he cautioned them against such slavish imitation.
> He said that European dress did not suit Orientals. It was not
> this dress or.that which he recommended in particular, but it
> was the manner in which he found his countrymen foolishly
> aping foreign ways that called forth his criticism.
>
> In the morning of the 19th the Swami left Colombo for
> Kandy by train in a special saloon. His original intention was
> to take a steamer direct from Colombo to Madras, but on his
> arrival in Ceylon so many telegrams poured in besecching a visit
> to Ceylonese and Southern Indian towns, if only in passing,
> that he was induced to alter his plans…

**Location:** Eastern Sri Lanka, Trincomalee, Sri Lanka
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Coastal visit


Vivekananda visited Trincomalee during his 1897 return tour of Ceylon (Sri Lanka), delivering lectures and receiving the warm welcome of the local community.

### Penang

**Location:** Georgetown, Penang, Penang, Malaysia
**Period:** June 1893
**Significance:** Transit on voyage to Chicago


Vivekananda stopped at Penang (then part of British Malaya) during his outward voyage to the Parliament of World's Religions in Chicago. He observed the vibrant Indian diaspora community and their temples in this colonial port city.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> \
> From Bombay we reached Colombo. Our steamer remained in port for nearly the whole day, and we took the \
> opportunity of getting off to have a look at the town. We drove through the streets, and the only thing I remember \
> was a temple in which was a very gigantic Murti (image) of the Lord Buddha in a reclining posture, entering \
> Nirvana.... \
> \
> The next station was Penang, which is only a strip of land along the sea in the body of the Malaya Peninsula. The \
> Malayas are all Mohammedans and in old days were noted pirates and quite a dread to merchantmen. But now the \
> leviathan guns of modem turreted battleships have forced the Malayas to look about for more peaceful pursuits. On \
> our…

*— Notes from Lectures and Discourses \*

### Singapore city

**Location:** Port of Singapore, Singapore, Singapore
**Period:** June 1893
**Significance:** Transit en route to Chicago


Vivekananda passed through the British colony of Singapore on his voyage to the Parliament of World's Religions. He was struck by the cosmopolitan nature of this trading port where Indians, Chinese and Malays lived together.

### Hong Kong

**Location:** Victoria Harbour, Hong Kong, China
**Period:** July 1893
**Significance:** Transit en route to Chicago


Vivekananda stopped at Hong Kong (then a British crown colony) during his outward voyage to America. He observed the energy and commerce of this China coast port and reflected on the ancient civilisation of the East.


**From the Complete Works** (3 references):

> out to me several places as the favourite haunts of pirates in days gone by. Singapore is the capital of the Straits \
> Settlements. It has a fine botanical garden with the most splendid collection of palms. The beautiful fan-like palm, \
> called the traveller's palm, grows here in abundance, and the bread-fruit tree everywhere. The celebrated \
> mangosteen is as plentiful here as mangoes in Madras, but mango is nonpareil. The people here are not half so dark \
> as the people of Madras, although so near the line. Singapore possesses a fine museum too. \
> \
> Hong Kong next. You feel that you have reached China, the Chinese element predominates so much. All labour, all \
> trade seems to be in their…

*— Notes from Lectures and Discourses \*

> rice-cake given to him from time to time by the madly busy mother. The Chinese child is quite a philosopher and \
> calmly goes to work at an age when your Indian boy can hardly crawl on all fours. He has leamt the philosophy of \
> necessity too well. Their extreme poverty is one of the causes why the Chinese and the Indians have remained in a \
> state of mummified civilisation. To an ordinary Hindu or Chinese, everyday necessity is too hideous to allow him \
> to think of anything else. \
> \
> Hong Kong is a very beautiful town. It is built on the slopes of hills and on the tops too, which are much cooler \
> than the city. There is an almost perpendicular tramway going to the top of the hill,…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> I went to see several Chinese temples. The biggest in Canton is dedicated to the memory of the first Buddhistic \
> Emperor and the five hundred first disciples of Buddhism. The central figure is of course Buddha, and next beneath \
> Him is seated the Emperor, and ranging on both sides are the statues of the disciples, all beautifully carved out of \
> wood. \
> \
> From Canton I returned back to Hong Kong, and from thence to Japan. The first port we touched was Nagasaki. \
> \
> We landed for a few hours and drove through the town. What a contrast! The Japanese are one of the cleanliest \
> peoples on earth. Everything is neat and tidy. Their streets are nearly all broad, straight, and regularly paved.…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Nagasaki

**Location:** Port of Nagasaki, Nagasaki, Japan
**Period:** July 1893
**Significance:** First landing in Japan


Vivekananda's ship first called at Nagasaki during his voyage to America. He was deeply impressed by the order, cleanliness and beauty of Japan. He later wrote enthusiastically to his Indian disciples, urging them to learn from Japan's rapid modernisation.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> \
> I went to see several Chinese temples. The biggest in Canton is dedicated to the memory of the first Buddhistic \
> Emperor and the five hundred first disciples of Buddhism. The central figure is of course Buddha, and next beneath \
> Him is seated the Emperor, and ranging on both sides are the statues of the disciples, all beautifully carved out of \
> wood. \
> \
> From Canton I returned back to Hong Kong, and from thence to Japan. The first port we touched was Nagasaki. \
> \
> We landed for a few hours and drove through the town. What a contrast! The Japanese are one of the cleanliest \
> peoples on earth. Everything is neat and tidy. Their streets are nearly all broad, straight, and regularly paved.…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (2 references):

> of the Bengalis. These evidences. as also his past study of
> Chinese Buddhism, convinced him that Bengal and China had
> at one time been in close communication, that there must have
> been a great influx of Bengali Bhikshus in China, who brought
> to that distant country the gospel of the Blessed One, and that
> Indian thought had dominated Chinese civilisation in a remark-
> able way.
>
> At Nagasaki in Japan, the Swami was greatly impressed
> with everything he saw. In his first letter to his Madras disciples

### Kobe

**Location:** Port of Kobe, Kobe, Japan
**Period:** July 1893
**Significance:** Japan visit


Vivekananda visited Kobe, Japan's major port city. He used his Japan stopover to explore the country's remarkable blend of ancient culture and modern progress — a model he wished India could follow.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> peoples on earth. Everything is neat and tidy. Their streets are nearly all broad, straight, and regularly paved. Their \
> little houses are cage-like, and their pine-covered evergreen little hills form the background of almost every town \
> and village. The short-statured, fair-skinned, quaintly-dressed Japs, their movements, attitudes, gestures, everything \
> is picturesque. Japan is the land of the picturesque! Almost every house has a garden at the back, very nicely laid \
> out according to Japanese fashion with small shrubs, grass-plots, small artificial waters, and small stone bridges. \
> \
> From Nagasaki to Kobe. Here I gave up the steamer and took the land-route to Yokohama, with a view…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Kyoto

**Location:** Temple district, Kyoto, Japan
**Period:** July 1893
**Significance:** Buddhist temples


Japan's ancient imperial capital. Vivekananda visited Kyoto's renowned Buddhist temples and Shinto shrines. He was moved by the depth of Japan's living spiritual tradition and its harmony with modernity.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> is picturesque. Japan is the land of the picturesque! Almost every house has a garden at the back, very nicely laid \
> out according to Japanese fashion with small shrubs, grass-plots, small artificial waters, and small stone bridges. \
> \
> From Nagasaki to Kobe. Here I gave up the steamer and took the land-route to Yokohama, with a view to see the \
> interior of Japan. \
> \
> I have seen three big cities in the interior \— Osaka, a great manufacturing town, Kyoto, the former capital, and \
> Tokyo, the present capital. Tokyo is nearly twice the size of Calcutta with nearly double the population. \
> \
> No foreigner is allowed to travel in the interior without a passport. \
> \
> The Japanese seem now to…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Yokohama

**Location:** Port of Yokohama, Yokohama, Japan
**Period:** July 1893
**Significance:** Gateway to the Pacific


Vivekananda departed from Yokohama across the Pacific Ocean, heading for Canada and then Chicago. This was his final farewell to the East before stepping onto the world stage for the first time.


**From the Complete Works** (3 references):

> thoroughly organised army equipped with guns which one of their own officers has invented and which is said to \
> be second to none. Then, they are continually increasing their navy. I have seen a tunnel nearly a mile long, bored \
> by a Japanese engineer. \
> \
> The match factories are simply a sight to see, and they are bent upon making everything they want in their own \
> country. There is a Japanese line of steamers plying between China and Japan, which shortly intends running \
> between Bombay and Yokohama. \
> \
> I saw quite a lot of temples. In every temple there are some Sanskrit Mantras written in Old Bengali characters. \
> Only a few of the priests know Sanskrit. But they are an intelligent…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> peoples on earth. Everything is neat and tidy. Their streets are nearly all broad, straight, and regularly paved. Their \
> little houses are cage-like, and their pine-covered evergreen little hills form the background of almost every town \
> and village. The short-statured, fair-skinned, quaintly-dressed Japs, their movements, attitudes, gestures, everything \
> is picturesque. Japan is the land of the picturesque! Almost every house has a garden at the back, very nicely laid \
> out according to Japanese fashion with small shrubs, grass-plots, small artificial waters, and small stone bridges. \
> \
> From Nagasaki to Kobe. Here I gave up the steamer and took the land-route to Yokohama, with a view…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

---

### Tokyo

**Location:** Tokyo city, Tokyo, Japan
**Period:** July 1893
**Significance:** Japan's capital


Vivekananda visited Tokyo during his Japan stopover. He was particularly impressed by Japan's rapid transformation into a modern nation while retaining its cultural identity — a lesson he felt India urgently needed to learn.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references):

> out according to Japanese fashion with small shrubs, grass-plots, small artificial waters, and small stone bridges. \
> \
> From Nagasaki to Kobe. Here I gave up the steamer and took the land-route to Yokohama, with a view to see the \
> interior of Japan. \
> \
> I have seen three big cities in the interior \— Osaka, a great manufacturing town, Kyoto, the former capital, and \
> Tokyo, the present capital. Tokyo is nearly twice the size of Calcutta with nearly double the population. \
> \
> No foreigner is allowed to travel in the interior without a passport. \
> \
> The Japanese seem now to have fully awakened themselves to the necessity of the present times. They have now a \
> thoroughly organised army…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> With all love and blessings, \
> VlVEKANANDA \
> \
> \
> Kakudzo [Kakuzo] Okakura Esq. \
> \
> Tokyo,Japan \
> \
> \'bb \
> \
> Chronology \'bb \

### Vancouver

**Location:** Vancouver Harbour, Vancouver, Canada
**Period:** 25 July 1893
**Significance:** First landing in North America


Vivekananda arrived at Vancouver on 25 July 1893 — his first setting foot on North American soil. He then took the transcontinental train eastward to Chicago, observing the vast prairies of Canada and America.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> Metcalf, Mass., \
> 20th August, 1893. \
> \
> Dear Alasinga, \
> \
> Received your letter yesterday. Perhaps you have by this time got my letter from Japan. From Japan I reached \
> Vancouver. The way was by the Northern Pacific. It was very cold and I suffered much for want of warm clothing. \
> However, I reached Vancouver anyhow, and thence went through Canada to Chicago. I remained about twelve \
> days in Chicago. And almost every day I used to go to the Fair. It is a tremendous affair. One must take at least ten \
> days to go through it. The lady to whom Varada Rao introduced me and her husband belong to the highest Chicago \
> society, and they were so very kind to me. I took my departure from Chicago…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (1 references):

> 292 LIFE OF SWAMI VIVEKANANDA
>
> travelling abroad finds in other nations a more modern, organised
> and self-reliant public life and desires it for his native land.

> From Yokohama the ship sailed on to Vancouver—from the
> Old World to the New! As the ship drew near to the port
> of Vancouver in British Columbia the Swami saw from a dis-
> tance the land of his hopes. For want of warm clothing he
> had suffered much on board the ship from cold, for, though
> he was provided with a handsome wardrobe, it did not occur
> to him or to his disciples that this summer voyage by the
> Northern Pacific would be cold. From Vancouver he went by
> train to Chicago through Canada. Through city after city the
