### Chicago — Parliament of Religions

**Location:** Art Institute of Chicago, Michigan Ave, Chicago, USA
**Period:** 11 September 1893
**Significance:** World-historic speech — 'Sisters and Brothers of America'


The greatest moment of Vivekananda's life. On 11 September 1893, he stepped to the podium at the Parliament of World's Religions and began: 'Sisters and Brothers of America!' — a greeting so unexpected and human that the entire audience rose in a two-minute standing ovation. He went on to deliver a series of lectures that electrified the Western world and established India's spiritual prestige on the global stage.


**From the Complete Works** (256 references):

> religion, praying in the mosque of the Mohammedan, worshipping before the fire of the Zoroastrian, and kneeling \
> to the cross of the Christian. We know that all religions alike, from the lowest fetishism to the highest absolutism, \
> are but so many attempts of the human soul to grasp and realise the Infinite. So we gather all these flowers, and, \
> binding them together with the cord of love, make them into a wonderful bouquet of worship." To the heart of this \
> speaker, none was foreign or alien. For him, there existed only Humanity and Truth. \
> \
> Of the Swami's address before the Parliament of Religions, it may be said that when he began to speak it was of \

### New York City

**Location:** Broadway / 54th Street, New York, USA
**Period:** 1894–1896, 1899–1900
**Significance:** Vedanta Society HQ


New York became Vivekananda's base in America. He founded the Vedanta Society of New York (1894) — one of the oldest Vedanta organisations in the West. He lived and lectured here extensively across two visits, drawing audiences that included prominent intellectuals, socialites and spiritual seekers.


**From the Complete Works** (304 references):

> 37. We have been low animals once. We think they are something different from us. I hear, Western people say, \
> "The world was created for us." If tigers could write books, they would say, man was created for them and that man \
> is a most sinful animal, because he does not allow him (the tiger) to catch him easily. The worm that crawls under \
> your feet today is a God to be. \
> \
> 38. "I should very much like our women to have your intellectuality, but not if it must be at the cost of purity", said \
> Swami Vivekananda in New York. "I admire you for all that you know, but I dislike the way that you cover what is \
> bad with roses and call it good. Intellectuality is not the highest good.…

### Boston

**Location:** Cambridge / Harvard area, Boston, USA
**Period:** 1893–1896
**Significance:** Academic connections


Vivekananda visited Boston and interacted with Harvard faculty, including Professor John Henry Wright who famously wrote: 'To ask for your credentials is like asking the sun to state its right to shine.' It was Wright who helped secure Vivekananda's passage to Chicago.


**From the Complete Works** (96 references):

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> IS INDIA A BENIGHTED COUNTRY? \
> \
> The following is a report of a lecture at Detroit, United States, America, with the editorial comments of the Boston \
> Evening Transcript, 5th April, 1894: \
> \
> Swami Vivekananda has been in Detroit recently and made a proofed impression there. All classes flocked to hear \
> him, and professional men in particular were greatly interested in his logic and his soundness of thought. The \
> opera-house alone was large enough for his audience. He speaks English extremely well, and he is as handsome as \
> he is good. The Detroit newspapers have devoted much space to the reports of his lectures. An editorial in the \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> (.Boston Herald, May 15, 1894) \
> \
> Association Hall was crowded with ladies yesterday, to hear Swami Vivekananda, the Brahmin (Meaning Hindu. \— \
> Publisher .) Monk talk about "The Religion of India" [actually "The Manners and Customs of India"], for the benefit \
> of the ward 16 day nursery [actually, Tyler-street Day Nursery]. The Brahmin monk has become a fad in Boston, as \
> he was in Chicago last year, and his earnest, honest, cultured manner has won many friends for him. \
> \

### Thousand Islands

**Location:** Thousand Island Park, St Lawrence River, Thousand Island Park, USA
**Period:** June–July 1895
**Significance:** Vedanta retreat & Raja Yoga


Vivekananda spent two months at Thousand Island Park, New York, living and teaching a small group of dedicated disciples in an intimate setting. Here he wrote most of his landmark book 'Raja Yoga' and gave daily classes. It was a period of intense spiritual depth.


**From the Complete Works** (32 references):

> (Recorded by Miss S. E. Waldo, a disciple) \
> \
> \
> Wednesday, June 19, 1895. \
> \
> \{This day marks the beginning of the regular teaching given daily by Swami Vivekananda to his disciples at \
> Thousand Island Park. We had not yet all assembled there, but the Master's heart was always in his work, so he \
> commenced at once to teach the three or four who were with him. He came on this first morning with the Bible in \
> his hand and opened to the Book of John, saying that since we were all Christians, it was proper that he should \
> begin with the Christian scriptures.) \
> \
> "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God." The Hindu calls this Maya, \
> the…

### Detroit

**Location:** Detroit, Michigan, Detroit, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Lecture tour


Vivekananda lectured in Detroit during his American lecture circuit. He attracted large audiences and enthusiastic press coverage. His lectures on Hinduism's universality resonated strongly with American audiences.


**From the Complete Works** (88 references):

> (\uc0\u9632 Saginaw Courier-Herald, March 22, 1894) \
> \
> Seated in the lobby of the Hotel Vincent yesterday evening was a strong and regular featured man of fine presence, \
> whose swarthy skin made more pronounced the pearly whiteness of his even teeth. Under a broad and high \
> forehead his eyes betoken intelligence. This gentleman was Swami Vive Kananda, the Hindoo preacher. Mr. \
> Kananda's conversation is in pure and grammatically constructed English sentences, to which his slightly foreign \
> accent lends piquancy. Readers of the Detroit papers are aware that Mr. Kananda has lectured in that city a number \
> of times and aroused the animosity of some on account of his strictures upon…

> tomorrow for Chicago, where he has an engagement for the night of the 25th. \

### Minneapolis

**Location:** Minneapolis, Minnesota, Minneapolis, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Lecture tour


Vivekananda delivered lectures in Minneapolis as part of his extensive American lecture circuit following the Parliament of World's Religions. His talks drew curious and enthusiastic audiences across the Midwest.


**From the Complete Works** (4 references):

> \

> \
> Excuse my delay in answering your letter. I do not know when I will be able to see you again. I am starting \
> tomorrow for Madison and Minneapolis. \
> \
> The English gentleman you speak of is Dr. Momerie of London. He is a well- kn own worker amongst the poor of \
> London and is a very sweet man. You perhaps do not know that the English church was the only religious \
> denomination in the world who did not send to us a representative, and Dr. Momerie came to the Parliament in \

### Memphis

**Location:** Memphis, Tennessee, Memphis, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Lecture tour — Deep South


Vivekananda ventured into the American Deep South, lecturing in Memphis. He was deeply disturbed by the racial injustice he witnessed and drew parallels with India's caste system — both symptoms, he felt, of spiritual ignorance.


**From the Complete Works** (9 references):

> \'ab \

> \

> eloquent men who has ever appeared on the religious or lecture platform in this country. His matchless oratory, \
> deep penetration into things occult, his cleverness in debate, and great earnestness captured the closest attention of \
> the world's thinking men at the World's Fair Parliament of Religion, and the admiration of thousands of people who \
> have since heard him during his lecture tour through many of the states of the Union. \
> \
> In conversation he is a most pleasant gentleman; his choice of words are…

### Washington D.C.

**Location:** Capitol Hill / National Mall area, Washington D.C., USA
**Period:** 1894, 1899
**Significance:** Capital lecture


Vivekananda visited Washington D.C. and lectured to distinguished audiences. He met influential politicians and intellectuals in the American capital and spoke on India's spiritual contribution to world civilisation.


**From the Complete Works** (23 references):

> \
> MEDITATION \
> \
> (.Delivered at the Washington Hall, San Francisco, April 3, 1900*) \
> \
> Meditation has been laid stress upon by all religions. The meditative state of mind is declared by the Yogis to be \
> the highest state in which the mind exists. When the mind is studying the external object, it gets identified with it, \
> loses itself. To use the simile of the old Indian philosopher: the soul of man is like a piece of crystal, but it takes the \
> colour of whatever is near it. Whatever the soul touches ... it has to take its colour. That is the difficulty. That \
> constitutes the bondage. The colour is so strong, the crystal forgets itself and identifies itself with the colour. \

### London

**Location:** Wimbledon / Belgravia, London, England
**Period:** 1895–1896, 1899–1900
**Significance:** Vedanta lectures in England


Vivekananda visited London twice, lecturing to packed audiences and attracting distinguished English disciples including Sister Nivedita (Margaret Noble) and Josephine MacLeod. He taught classes in London drawing rooms and lecture halls, establishing Vedanta firmly in England.


**From the Complete Works** (185 references):

> Now, coming back to the practical. The subject of the practical application of psychology has been taken up in \
> India from very early times. About fourteen hundred years before Christ, there flourished in India a great \
> philosopher, Patanjali by name. He collected ah the facts, evidences, and researches in psychology and took \
> advantage of ah the experiences accumulated in the past. Remember, this world is very old; it was not created only \

> ugly. The Sari of our women, and the Choga, Chapkan, and turban of our men defy \

> suited for doing work; in doing work, they are spoiled and done for. There is such a thing \
> as fashion in the West. Their fashion is in dress, ours in ornaments, though nowadays it is \

### Oxford

**Location:** University of Oxford, Oxford, England
**Period:** 1896
**Significance:** Met Max Müller


Vivekananda visited Oxford and met the great German-born Sanskrit scholar Friedrich Max Müller, who had spent his life translating the Vedas and was deeply moved by meeting Vivekananda. Their conversations about Ramakrishna and Vedanta were recorded by Max Müller.


**From the Complete Works** (8 references):

> 528 Fifth Avenue] \
> New York \
> 10 April 1894 \
> \
> Dear Mother, \
> \
> I just now received your letter. I have the greatest regard for the Salvationists; in fact, they and the Oxford Mission \
> gentlemen are the only Christian missionaries for whom I have any regard at all. They live with the people, as the \
> people, and for the people of India. Lord bless them. But I would be very, very sorry of any trick being played by \
> them. I never have heard of any Lord in India, much less in Ceylon. (Now Sri Lanka.) The people of Ceylon and \
> northern India differ more than Americans and Hindus. Nor is there any connection between the Buddhist priest \
> and the Hindu. Our dress, manners, religion, food,…

*— lectures are a great feature in this city, and more can be made by each such lecture than even platform talks in \*

> as brother and then hastily went to publish my short answer and make a show. But you know what people here \
> think of such gentlemen. Moreover, the same missionary went privately to some of my friends to ask them not to \
> befriend me. Of course he met with universal contempt. I am quite astonished at this man\'s behaviour \— a preacher \
> of religion to take to such underhand dealings! Unfortunately too much of that in every country and in every \
> religion. Last winter I travelled a good deal in this country although the weather was very severe. I thought it would \
> be dreadful, but I did not find it so after all. You remember Col. Neggenson, President of the Free Religious \
> Society. He…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Relative to such questions as to widows not marrying: among the first two castes, the number of women is \
> disproportionately large, and here is a dilemma. Either you have a non-marriageable widow problem and misery, or \
> the non-husband-getting young lady problem. To face the widow problem, or the old maid problem? There you \
> are; either of the two. Now, go back again to the idea that the Indian mind is socialistic. It says, "Now look here! \
> we take the widow problem as the lesser one." Why? "Because they have had their chance; they have been \
> married. If they have lost their chance, at any rate they have had one. Sit down, be quiet, and consider these poor \
> girls-they have not had one…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Miss Muller was the person who offered that money I promised. I have told her about your new proposal. She is \
> thinking about it. In the meanwhile I think it is better to give her some work. She has consented to be the agent for \
> the Brahmavadin and Awakened India. Will you write to her about it? Her address is Airlie Lodge, Ridgeway \
> Gardens, Wimbledon, England. I was living with her over there for the last few weeks. But the London work \
> cannot go on without my living in London. As such I have changed quarters. I am sorry it has chagrined Miss \
> Muller a bit. Cannot help. Her full name is Miss Henrietta Muller. Max Muller is getting very friendly. I am soon \
> going to deliver two…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Swampscott, Massachusetts

**Location:** Rockland Street, Swampscott, Swampscott, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Summer stay


Swampscott on the Massachusetts North Shore was a summer destination for Vivekananda during his first American tour. The seaside town provided a peaceful retreat between his busy schedule of lectures and classes throughout New England.


**From the Complete Works** (5 references):

> \
> Our Sanskrit poets lavish all the power of expression they have upon the soft body of women \— the Sanskrit word \
> for women is "Komala", the soft body; but the mailclad ones of this country are "armadillas", I think. You cannot \
> imagine how ludicrous it appears to a foreigner who never saw it before. Shiva, Shiva. \
> \
> Now Narasimha\'s Mrs. Smith does not torture you anymore with letters, I hope. Did I tell you I met your friend \
> Mrs. H. O. Quarry at Swampscott? \— she can swamp a house for all that, not to speak of a cott \— and that I met \
> there the woman that pulls by the nose Mr. Pullman?! And I also heard there the best American singer, (Miss Emma \
> Thursby.) they said \— she sang…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> To the Hale Sisters \
> \
> \
> Dear Babies, \
> \
> \
> Swampscott, \
> 26th July, 1894. \
> \
> \
> Now don't let my letters stray beyond the circle, please. I had a beautiful letter from sister Mary. See how I am \
> getting the dash, sister Jeany teaches me all that. She can jump and run and play and swear like a devil and talk \
> slang at the rate of 500 a minute; only she does not much care for religion, only a little. She is gone today home, \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Dear Mother, \
> \

> and am very glad to see my friend Mazoomdar\'s (Pratap Chandra Mazumdar.) book spoken of so highly. Mazoomdar is \
> a great and a good man and has done much for his fellow beings. \
> \
> It is a lovely summer place, this Cedar Lawn of the Guernseys. Miss Guernsey has gone on a visit to Swampscott. \
> \
> I had also an invitation there, but I thought [it] better to stay here in the calm and silent place full of trees and with \
> the beautiful Hudson flowing by and mountain in the background. \
> \
> I am very thankful for Miss Howe\'s suggestion, and I am also thinking of…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \

> Boston, \

> I have received both of your kind notes. I will have to go back to Melrose on Saturday and remain there till \

> Of course, much less space will suffice me than what you have kindly proposed to put at my disposal, I can bundle \
> myself up anywhere and feel quite comfortable. \

> \
> Dear Mother \
> \
> I have received the letter you sent over to me coming from India. \
> \
> I am going to leave this place on Monday next for Plymouth [Massachusetts], where the Free Religious \
> Association* is holding its session. They will defray my expenses, of course. \
> \
> I am all right, enjoying nice health, and the people here are very k in d and nice to me. Up to date I had no occasion \
> to cash any cheque as everything is going on smoothly. I have not heard anything from the Babies. Hope they are \
> doing well. You also had nothing to write; however, I feel that you are doing well. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> 27]. He was a representative of his country and creed at the recent parliament of religions assembled in Chicago \

> his audience, commenced his lecture, the subject of which was "Hindoo Religion". His lecture was not confined to \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> Minneapolis \
> 24 November 1893. \
> \
> \
> I am still in Minneapolis. I am to lecture this afternoon, and the day after tomorrow go to Des Moines. \
> \
> The day I came here they had their first snow, and it snowed all through the day and night, and I had great use for \
> the arctics. (A waterproof overshoe.) I went to see the frozen Minnehaha Falls. They are very beautiful. The \
> temperature today is 21\'b0 below zero, but I had been out sleighing and enjoyed it immensely. I am not the least \
> afraid of losing the tips of my ears or nose. \
> \

> I tried to get the clergymen's ticket, but they could not give me any, not being the headquarters. The thing to be \

> the permits for me. If it is so, I hope he will take the trouble to send them over to me to Minneapolis if they can \

> money in a draft on the bank, which cost me 400.…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> the World's Fair, instead of in London, as it ought to have been. The Raja of Mysore and some other friends sent \
> me to America as the Hindu representative. I stayed there three years, with the exception of last summer and this \
> summer, when I came to lecture in London. The Americans are a great people, with a future before them. I admire \
> them very much, and found many kind friends among them. They are less prejudiced than the English, more ready \
> to weigh end examine anew idea, to value it in spite of its newness. They are most hospitable too; far less time is \
> lost in showing one's credentials, as it were. You travel in America, as I did, from city to city, always lecturing \
> among…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Nashville, Tennessee

**Location:** Nashville, TN, Nashville, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Southern lecture tour


Nashville, the Athens of the South, was one of the Southern American cities where Vivekananda lectured. Despite the racial tensions of the post-Civil War South, he spoke on the unity of all religions and the equality of all human beings before the Absolute.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Cambridge, Massachusetts

**Location:** Cambridge, MA, Cambridge, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Harvard University connections


Cambridge, home to Harvard University, was a key intellectual center for Vivekananda during his American tours. He met Harvard professors including William James and John Henry Wright, who played a crucial role in recommending him to the Parliament of Religions organizers.


**From the Complete Works** (24 references):

> London, S.W. \
> 6th July, 1896. \
> \
> Dear Friend And Brother, (to Dr. Lewis i. Janes.) \
> \
> Yours of the 25th June has duly reached and gave me great pleasure. I am so glad to see the noble work \
> progressing. I had leamt with the greatest delight from Mrs. Bull of the work that is going to be done in Cambridge \
> this winter and no better person could have been selected to direct it as yourself. May all power attend you. I will \
> be only too glad to write for the magazine from time to time and my first instalment was to be in a few weeks, \
> when I hope to get some leisure. Certainly it goes without saying that no one of the types we call religious ought to \
> die \— they like races require…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> cheque. Perhaps you have come to know by this time of my being in Greenacre. I had a very nice time here and am \
> enjoying it immensely. In the fall I am going to lecture in Brooklyn, New York. Yesterday I got news that they \
> have completed all the advertising there. I have an invitation today from a friend in New York to go with him to \
> some mountains north of this state of Maine. I do not know whether I will go or not. I am doing pretty well. \
> Between lecturing, teaching, picnicking and other excitements the time is flying rapidly. I hope you are doing very \
> well and that Father Pope is in good trim. It is a very beautiful spot \— this Greenacre \— and [I] have very nice \
> company…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> C/o E. Guernsey, M.D., \
> The Madrid, 180 W. 59, \
> 15th November, 1899. \
> \
> My Dear Mrs. Bull, \
> \
> After all I decide to come to Cambridge just now. I must finish the stories I began. The first one I don't think was \
> given back to me by Margo. \
> \
> My clothes will be ready the day after tomorrow, and then I shall be ready to start; only my fear is, it will be for \
> the whole winter a place for becoming nervous and not for quieting of nerves, with constant parties and lectures. \
> Well, perhaps you can give me a room somewhere, where I can hide myself from all the goings on in the place. \
> Again I am so nervous of going to a place where indirectly the Indian Math will be. The very name of…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> 1719 Turk Street, \
> San Francisco, \
> 12th March, 1900. \
> \
> My Dear Dhira Mata, \
> \
> Your letter from Cambridge came yesterday. Now I have got a fixed address, 1719 Turk Street, San Francisco. \
> Hope you will have time to pen a few lines in reply to this. I had a manuscript account sent me by you. I sent it \
> back as you desired; besides that, I had no other accounts. It is all right. \
> \
> I had a nice letter from Miss Souter from London. She expects to have Mr. ... to dine with her. \
> \
> So glad to hear of Margot's success. I have given her over to you, and am sure you will take care of her. I will be \

### Edinburgh

**Location:** Edinburgh, Scotland, Edinburgh, United Kingdom
**Period:** 1895
**Significance:** UK lecture tour


Vivekananda visited Edinburgh during his UK lecture tour in 1895. The Scottish capital, known as the Athens of the North for its rich intellectual tradition, provided a receptive audience for his talks on Vedanta and Indian philosophy.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (1 references):

> tion, new contrast, or intellectual discovery. The varied and
> artistic exhibits pleased the fastidious eye of the Swami, and

> the Exposition received him with honour and he was accorded
> every opportunity for original observation.
>
> Among the distinguished persons with whom he came into
> intimate contact during his stay in Paris, were Professor Patrick
> Geddes of Edinburgh University, Monsieur Jules Bois, Pére
> Hyacinthe, Mr. Hiram Maxim, Madame Calvé, Madame Sarah
> Bernhardt, Princess Demidoff, and his own countryman,
> Dr. J. C. Bose, who had also been invited to attend the Exposi-
> tion in connection with the Congress of Scientists,…

*— p. 700*


---

### Manchester

**Location:** Manchester, England, Manchester, United Kingdom
**Period:** 1895
**Significance:** UK lecture tour


Manchester, the industrial heartland of England, was visited by Vivekananda during his 1895 UK tour. He lectured on Vedanta in this mill city, bringing the message of Indian spirituality to working-class and middle-class audiences in the North of England.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> them with simple justice. \
> \

*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Bristol

**Location:** Bristol, England, Bristol, United Kingdom
**Period:** 1895
**Significance:** UK lecture


Bristol on the River Avon was one of the English cities visited by Vivekananda during his first UK tour. The historic port city, with its long connections to trade and the Americas, hosted him for a public lecture on Indian philosophy.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Mannar

**Location:** Mannar Island, Sri Lanka, Mannar, Sri Lanka
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Rameswaram-Lanka crossing


Mannar, on the northwestern coast of Sri Lanka near the Palk Strait, was associated with Vivekananda's return to India. His triumphant return journey from America passed through Ceylon, and he was given a hero's welcome as he made his way northward.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### The Art Institute of Chicago

**Location:** Michigan Ave, Chicago, Chicago, USA
**Period:** 11 September 1893
**Significance:** Parliament of World's Religions opening


The Hall of Columbus at the Art Institute of Chicago was the venue for the Parliament of the World's Religions where Vivekananda delivered his famous opening address beginning with 'Sisters and Brothers of America' on September 11, 1893. His speech brought the audience of 7,000 to their feet in sustained applause.


**From the Complete Works** (223 references):

> speaker, none was foreign or alien. For him, there existed only Humanity and Truth. \
> \
> Of the Swami's address before the Parliament of Religions, it may be said that when he began to speak it was of \
> "the religious ideas of the Hindus", but when he ended, Hinduism had been created. The moment was ripe with this \
> potentiality. The vast audience that faced him represented exclusively the occidental mind, but included some \
> development of all that in this was most distinctive. Every nation in Europe has poured in its human contribution \
> upon America, and notably upon Chicago, where the Parliament was held. Much of the best, as well as some of the \
> worst, of modern effort and struggle, is…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Vivekananda's Chicago Residence

**Location:** 5432 South Dearborn Street, Chicago, Chicago, USA
**Period:** 1893–1894
**Significance:** Chicago base between lectures


Vivekananda stayed at various addresses in Chicago between and after the Parliament. The Hale family at 541 Dearborn Avenue were key supporters. He conducted Vedanta classes and met seekers at multiple Chicago residences during his extended stay in the city.

### Vedanta Society of New York-History

**Location:** 54 West 33rd Street, New York, New York, USA
**Period:** 1894–1895
**Significance:** First Vedanta Society in the world


Vivekananda founded the Vedanta Society of New York in November 1894, the first Vedanta Society in the Western world. He conducted classes and delivered lectures here, building the foundation of organized Vedanta in America.


**From the Complete Works** (300 references):

> 37. We have been low animals once. We think they are something different from us. I hear, Western people say, \
> "The world was created for us." If tigers could write books, they would say, man was created for them and that man \
> is a most sinful animal, because he does not allow him (the tiger) to catch him easily. The worm that crawls under \
> your feet today is a God to be. \
> \
> 38. "I should very much like our women to have your intellectuality, but not if it must be at the cost of purity", said \
> Swami Vivekananda in New York. "I admire you for all that you know, but I dislike the way that you cover what is \
> bad with roses and call it good. Intellectuality is not the highest good.…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> 78. "No", said the Swami, very softly, in answer to a question in New York, "I do not believe in the occult. If a \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> love of God. It is the one motive power that is in the universe. Under the impetus of that love, Christ gives his life \
> for humanity, Buddha even for an animal, the mother for the child, the husband for the wife. It is under the impetus \

> there, but it is misdirected. So, in all crimes, as well as in all virtuous actions, behind stands that eternal…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Thousand Island Park

**Location:** Thousand Island Park, NY, Thousand Island Park, USA
**Period:** July 1895
**Significance:** Vedanta classes in nature


In the summer of 1895, Vivekananda held informal Vedanta classes at Thousand Islands Park on the St. Lawrence River. These intimate sessions with a small group of devoted students produced some of his most profound teachings, later compiled in 'Inspired Talks.'


**From the Complete Works** (32 references):

> (Recorded by Miss S. E. Waldo, a disciple) \
> \
> \
> Wednesday, June 19, 1895. \
> \
> \{This day marks the beginning of the regular teaching given daily by Swami Vivekananda to his disciples at \
> Thousand Island Park. We had not yet all assembled there, but the Master's heart was always in his work, so he \
> commenced at once to teach the three or four who were with him. He came on this first morning with the Bible in \
> his hand and opened to the Book of John, saying that since we were all Christians, it was proper that he should \
> begin with the Christian scriptures.) \
> \

### Kamakura

**Location:** Great Buddha, Kamakura, Kamakura, Japan
**Period:** 1893
**Significance:** Great Buddha visit


Vivekananda visited Kamakura during his Japan sojourn en route to the Parliament of Religions in 1893. He was moved by the great bronze Buddha (Kotoku-in Daibutsu), seeing in it a symbol of the universal spiritual aspiration that transcended national boundaries.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Swami Ramakrishnananda's Cottage

**Location:** Triplicane, Chennai, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Madras Math cottage managed by Swami Ramakrishnananda


Swami Ramakrishnananda, one of the direct disciples of Sri Ramakrishna, was sent by Vivekananda to Madras to carry on the work of Vedanta in South India. He managed the early Madras Math from this modest cottage in Triplicane. When Vivekananda returned from the West in 1897, he visited this centre, which served as the nucleus of what later became the Ramakrishna Math, Chennai.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Flora Cottage

**Location:** Triplicane, Chennai, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Early residence of Swami Ramakrishnananda in Madras


Flora Cottage in Triplicane was the initial residence taken by Swami Ramakrishnananda when he came to Madras to establish the Vedanta work. He lived here simply, conducting daily worship and receiving visitors, before moving to a more permanent location for the Madras Math. Vivekananda visited the cottage on his triumphant return to Madras in February 1897 after his first Western tour.

### Victoria Hall

**Location:** Chennai, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Venue of Vivekananda's first major public lecture in Madras after returning from the West


Victoria Hall in Madras was the venue for Swami Vivekananda's first major public lecture after his triumphant return from the West in February 1897. Before a vast and enthusiastic audience, he delivered his stirring address on the message of Vedanta and the future of India. The lecture electrified Madras and marked the beginning of his transformative lecturing tour across India following his success at the Parliament of Religions in Chicago.


**From the Complete Works** (10 references):

> \
> 1896 \
> \
> \
> I have returned about three weeks from Switzerland but could not write you further before. I have sent you by last \
> mail a paper on Paul Deussen of Kiel. Sturdy's plan about the magazine is still hanging fire. As you see, I have left \
> the St. George's Road place. We have a lecture hall at 39 Victoria Street. C/o E. T. Sturdy will always reach me for \
> a year to come. The rooms at Grey Coat Gardens are only lodgings for self and the other Swami taken for three \
> mouths only. The work in London is growing apace, the classes are becoming bigger as they go on. I have no doubt \
> this will go on increasing at this rate and the English people are steady and loyal. Of course, as soon…

### Pachaiyappa's Hall

**Location:** Pachaiyappa's College, Chennai, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Venue of public lecture by Vivekananda in Madras


Pachaiyappa's Hall, part of the historic Pachaiyappa's College in Madras, was one of the prominent venues where Swami Vivekananda delivered public lectures during his 1897 visit to the city. The college, founded by the philanthropist Pachaiyappa Mudaliar, had a long tradition of public discourse, and Vivekananda's addresses on Vedanta and the mission of India drew large and enthusiastic audiences.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Hindu Theological High School

**Location:** Triplicane, Chennai, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Educational institution associated with Vivekananda's Madras disciples


The Hindu Theological High School in Triplicane, Madras, was closely associated with the circle of Vivekananda's Madras devotees. Alasinga Perumal and other disciples were connected with efforts to promote Hindu religious education in the city. Vivekananda visited the institution during his 1897 lecture tour, encouraging the effort to combine modern education with the spiritual and ethical foundations of Vedanta.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Sundar Nivas

**Location:** Triplicane, Chennai, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Residence associated with Vivekananda's 1897 Madras visit


Sundar Nivas was one of the residences associated with Swami Vivekananda's triumphant return to Madras in February 1897. After his spectacular success at the Parliament of Religions, Vivekananda was received with immense enthusiasm by the people of Madras. Sundar Nivas was among the places where he stayed and received visitors during his memorable visit, before continuing his all-India lecture tour.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Erskine Mason Phelps house

**Location:** Erskine Mason Phelps House, Chicago, Chicago, USA
**Period:** 1893
**Significance:** Private home where Vivekananda stayed in Chicago


Erskine Mason Phelps was one of Vivekananda's early supporters in Chicago. Vivekananda was hosted at his residence during the period of the Parliament of the World's Religions in September 1893, when he had first arrived in Chicago without a formal invitation or accommodation, and kind-hearted supporters opened their homes to him.

### George W. Hale Home

**Location:** George W. Hale Home, Chicago, Chicago, USA
**Period:** 1893
**Significance:** Home of astronomer supporter George E. Hale's family


George W. Hale was the father of the renowned astronomer George Ellery Hale. The Hale family in Chicago were warm supporters of Swami Vivekananda during his time in Chicago for the Parliament of the World's Religions in 1893. The Hale home provided Vivekananda with a welcoming base in the city and the family maintained contact with him throughout his American tours.

### Lyon Home

**Location:** Lyon Home, Chicago, Chicago, USA
**Period:** 1893
**Significance:** Residence of Chicago supporter of Vivekananda


The Lyon Home in Chicago was one of the residences that welcomed Swami Vivekananda during his stay in the city for the Parliament of the World's Religions in 1893. The network of sympathetic American householders who opened their homes provided the hospitality that sustained Vivekananda as he began his historic mission to introduce Vedanta to the West.

### Third Unitarian Church

**Location:** Third Unitarian Church, Chicago, Chicago, USA
**Period:** 1893-1894
**Significance:** Lecture venue for Vivekananda in Chicago


The Third Unitarian Church in Chicago was one of the venues where Swami Vivekananda delivered lectures on Vedanta during his extended stay in Chicago following the Parliament of the World's Religions in 1893. Unitarian and Universalist congregations across America were among the most receptive audiences for his philosophical message, sharing his emphasis on the universality of religion and the dignity of all spiritual traditions.

### Highland Park

**Location:** Highland Park, Illinois, Highland Park, USA
**Period:** 1893
**Significance:** Chicago suburb visited by Vivekananda


Highland Park, a prosperous suburb north of Chicago on Lake Michigan, was visited by Swami Vivekananda during his time in the Chicago area. He was invited to the homes of supporters and delivered informal talks on Vedanta philosophy in this well-to-do community. Many of his early American devotees lived in the suburbs surrounding Chicago.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (1 references):

> 406 LIFE OF SWAMI VIVEKANANDA
>
> As early as September 1893, immediately following his appear-
> ance at the Parliament, he was introduced to a group of noted
> scientists at a vegetarian dinner given especially in his honour
> by Professor Elisha Grey, the electrical inventor, and his wife
> in their beautiful residence, Highland Park, Chicago. It was at
> the time that the Electrical Congress was being held, and
> amongst other distinguished guests who were invited to meet the
> Swami, there were Sir Willian Thompson, afterwards Lord
> Kelvin, Professor Helmholtz and Ariton Hopitallia. The
> Swami’s knowledge of clectricity amazed the scientists, and his
> shining repartecs bearing on the matters of science…

*— p. 417*


---

### The Hull-House

**Location:** Hull House, South Halsted Street, Chicago, Chicago, USA
**Period:** 1893
**Significance:** Jane Addams's settlement house visited by Vivekananda


Hull-House, the famous settlement house founded by Jane Addams in 1889, was visited by Swami Vivekananda during his time in Chicago. Jane Addams and her colleagues were deeply interested in social reform and comparative religion. Vivekananda's vision of service to the poor as worship of God — Daridra Narayana — resonated with the settlement house movement's ideals. Hull-House was one of the most progressive institutions in America, and Vivekananda's visit reflected his engagement with American social reformers.

### Hinsdale Unitarian Church

**Location:** Hinsdale Unitarian Church, Hinsdale, Illinois, Hinsdale, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Lecture venue in Chicago suburb


The Hinsdale Unitarian Church, in the western Chicago suburb of Hinsdale, was one of the many Unitarian congregations where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his American tours. He found particularly receptive audiences in Unitarian churches, where his universalist message of Vedanta and the unity of all religions aligned naturally with their liberal theological outlook.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Masonic Temple

**Location:** Masonic Temple, State and Randolph Streets, Chicago, Chicago, USA
**Period:** 1893
**Significance:** Major lecture venue in Chicago during Parliament period


The Masonic Temple in Chicago, at the time one of the tallest buildings in the world, served as a lecture venue during the Parliament of the World's Religions period. Swami Vivekananda addressed audiences here, capitalising on the excitement generated by his sensational debut at the Parliament. The Temple's large assembly rooms provided a prestigious stage for his continuing lectures on Vedanta and the religion of India.

### First Universalist Church

**Location:** First Universalist Church, Chicago, Chicago, USA
**Period:** 1893-1894
**Significance:** Lecture venue for Vivekananda in Chicago


The First Universalist Church in Chicago was among the prominent religious institutions where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his Chicago period. Universalist congregations, with their belief in universal salvation and openness to diverse spiritual paths, were natural venues for his message of Vedanta. He drew large audiences from these progressive religious communities.


**From the Complete Works** (223 references):

> speaker, none was foreign or alien. For him, there existed only Humanity and Truth. \

> worst, of modern effort and struggle, is…

### Central Music Hall

**Location:** Central Music Hall, Chicago, Chicago, USA
**Period:** September 1893
**Significance:** Major lecture hall where Vivekananda gave talks during the Parliament


Central Music Hall in Chicago was one of the principal venues where Swami Vivekananda lectured during and after the Parliament of the World's Religions in September 1893. It was a prestigious concert and lecture hall in downtown Chicago, and his appearances there were part of the extension lectures that he gave to the broader public who could not attend the Parliament itself. His talks on Hinduism and Vedanta drew enthusiastic crowds.

### Auditorium Theater

**Location:** Auditorium Theatre, Congress Parkway, Chicago, Chicago, USA
**Period:** 1893
**Significance:** Prestigious lecture venue in Chicago for Vivekananda


The Auditorium Theatre in Chicago, designed by Adler and Sullivan and one of the most magnificent buildings in America, was a lecture venue associated with Swami Vivekananda's Chicago period. The Parliament extension lectures were hosted at various prestigious venues across the city, and the Auditorium Theatre — with its superb acoustics — was among the most celebrated. Vivekananda's commanding voice and presence were perfectly suited to such grand stages.


**From the Complete Works** (227 references):

### Cobb Hall

**Location:** Cobb Hall, University of Chicago, Chicago, USA
**Period:** 1893-1894
**Significance:** University of Chicago hall where Vivekananda lectured


Cobb Hall at the newly founded University of Chicago was one of the venues where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his Chicago period. The University of Chicago, opened in 1892, was a centre of progressive scholarship, and Vivekananda engaged with its academic community. He presented Vedanta philosophy to American university audiences, arguing for its rational, scientific foundations and its compatibility with modern thought.

### Dr. Perkins' house

**Location:** Dr. Perkins' House, Chicago, Chicago, USA
**Period:** 1893
**Significance:** Residence of Chicago supporter Dr. Perkins


Dr. Perkins was among the sympathetic Americans who welcomed Swami Vivekananda into their home during his Chicago sojourn in 1893. Vivekananda was received at many private residences where he held intimate discussions on Vedanta philosophy with small gatherings of interested Americans. These informal parlour talks complemented his public lectures and helped build his early American circle of devotees.


**From the Complete Works** (223 references):

> speaker, none was foreign or alien. For him, there existed only Humanity and Truth. \

> potentiality. The vast audience that faced him represented exclusively the occidental mind, but included some \
> development of all that in this was most distinctive. Every nation in Europe has poured in its human contribution \
> upon America, and notably upon Chicago, where the Parliament was held. Much of the best, as well as some of the \
> worst, of modern effort and struggle, is…

### First Congretational Church

**Location:** First Congregational Church, Evanston, Illinois, Evanston, USA
**Period:** 1893-1894
**Significance:** Evanston church where Vivekananda lectured


The First Congregational Church in Evanston, Illinois, was one of the venues where Swami Vivekananda spoke during his Illinois tour following the Parliament of the World's Religions. Evanston, home to Northwestern University, provided an educated and culturally receptive audience. Congregational churches, with their tradition of rational and liberal theology, were frequent hosts for his lectures on Vedanta.


**From the Complete Works** (4 references):

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> REINCARNATION \
> \
> (Evanston Index, October 7, 1893) \
> \
> At the Congregational Church, during the past week, there have been given a course of lectures which in nature \
> much resembled the Religious Parliament which has just been completed. The lecturers were Dr. Carl van Bergen \
> of Sweden, and Suami Vivekananda, the Hindu monk. ... Suami Vivekananda is a representative from India to the \
> Parliament of Religions. He has attracted a great deal of attention on account of his unique attire in Mandarin \
> colors, by his magnetic presence and by his brilliant oratory and wonderful exposition of Hindu philosophy. His \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Annisquam, \
> 20th August, 1894. \
> \
> \
> Your very kind letter duly reached me at Annisquam. I am with the Bagleys once more. They are kind as usual. \
> Professor Wright was not here. But he came day before yesterday and we have very nice time together. Mr. \
> Bradley of Evanston, whom you have met at Evanston, was here. His sister-in-law had me sit for a picture several \
> days and had painted me. I had some very fine boating and one evening overturned the boat and had a good \
> drenching \— clothes and all. \
> \
> I had very very nice time at Greenacre. They were all so earnest and kind people. Fanny Hartley and Mrs. Mills \
> have by this time gone back home I suppose. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> which I got $43.1 met a good many Boston people \— Mrs. Smith Junior, who said she kn ows Harriet, and Mrs. \
> Smith the elder, [who] knows you well. \
> \
> In Boston the other day I met a Unitarian clergyman who said he lives next to you in Chicago. I have unfortunately \
> forgotten his name. Mrs. Smith is a very nice lady and treated me with all courtesy. Mrs. Bagley is kind as ever, \
> and I will have to remain here a few days more, I am afraid. Prof. Wright and I are having a good time. Prof. \
> Bradley of Evanston! has gone home. If you ever meet him at Evanston, give him my best love and regards. He is \
> really a spiritual man. \
> \
> I do not find anything more to write. \
> \
> Some u nkn own…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Dharmapala, the orator Mazoomdar \— and realise that in that far-off and poor India there are hearts that beat in \
> sympathy to yours, born and brought up in this mighty and great country. \
> \
> My eternal respects to your holy wife; and to your sweet children my eternal love and blessings. \
> \
> Col. Higginson, a very broad man, told me that your daughter had written to his daughter about me; and he was \
> very sympathetic to me. I am going to Evanston tomorrow and hope to see Prof. Bradley there. \
> \
> May He make us all more and more pure and holy so that we may live a perfect spiritual life even before throwing \
> off this earthly body. \
> \
> \
> VlVEKANANDA. \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*

### Houses visited in Evanston

**Location:** Evanston, Illinois, Evanston, USA
**Period:** 1893-1894
**Significance:** Private homes in Evanston where Vivekananda was hosted


During his visits to Evanston, Illinois, Swami Vivekananda was hosted in the private homes of several sympathetic residents. Evanston, a prosperous university town adjacent to Chicago, had a number of progressive, spiritually inclined families who opened their homes to him. These intimate gatherings in Evanston parlours were some of the earliest forums where Vivekananda introduced Vedanta to American householders.

### Colonel Ralph Plumb

**Location:** Colonel Ralph Plumb's Residence, Streator, Illinois, Streator, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Home of Streator supporter Colonel Ralph Plumb


Colonel Ralph Plumb was a civic leader and supporter in Streator, Illinois, who hosted Swami Vivekananda during his lecture tour through the state. Vivekananda visited numerous smaller cities and towns during his American tours, bringing the message of Vedanta to communities beyond the major metropolitan centres. Colonel Plumb's hospitality and introduction helped bring Vivekananda before the audiences of Streator.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references):

> 10th October, 1893. \
> \
> \
> I received your letter yesterday. Just now I am lecturing about Chicago \— and am doing as I think very well; it is \
> ranging from 30 to 80 dollars a lecture, and just now I have been so well advertised in Chicago gratis by the \
> Parliament of Religions that it is not advisable to give up this field now. To which I am sure you will agree. \
> However I may come soon to Boston, but when I cannot say. Yesterday I returned from Streator where I got 87 \
> dollars for a lecture. I have engagements every day this week. And hope more will come by the end of the week. \
> My love to Mr. Woods and compliments to all our friends. \
> \
> \
> Yours truly, \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> HINDU CIVILISATION \
> \
> [Although the lecture at Streator on October 9 was well ahended, the Streator Daily Free Press of October 9 ran \
> the following somewhat dreary review:] \
> \
> \
> The lecture of this celebrated Hindoo at the Opera House, Saturday night, was very interesting. By comparative \
> philology, he sought to establish the long admitted relationship between the Aryan races and their descendants in \
> the new world. He mildly defended the caste system of India which keeps three-fourths of the people in utter and \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Plumb Opera House

**Location:** Plumb Opera House, Streator, Illinois, Streator, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Lecture venue in Streator, Illinois


The Plumb Opera House in Streator, Illinois was the venue for Swami Vivekananda's public lecture in the town. Named after the Plumb family who were prominent in Streator civic life, the opera house served as the principal public gathering place. Vivekananda's lecture on Vedanta and the universal religion drew a curious and enthusiastic audience from this Illinois coal-mining town, demonstrating the wide reach of his American lecture circuit.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references):

### First Congregational Church

**Location:** First Congregational Church, Madison, Wisconsin, Madison, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Madison venue where Vivekananda lectured


The First Congregational Church in Madison, Wisconsin was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his Midwestern tour. Madison, the state capital and home of the University of Wisconsin, provided a receptive intellectual audience for his message of Vedanta philosophy. Congregational churches across the American Midwest regularly invited him to address their congregations and the wider public.


**From the Complete Works** (5 references):

> \'ab \
> \
> AN INTERESTING LECTURE \
> \
> (Wisconsin State Journal, November 21, 1893) \
> \
> The lecture at the Congregational Church [Madison] last night by the celebrated Hindoo monk, Vivekananda, was \
> an extremely interesting one, and contained much of sound philosophy and good religion. Pagan though he be, \
> Christianity may well follow many of his teachings. His creed is as wide as the universe, taking in all religions, and \
> accepting truth wherever it may be found. Bigotry and superstition and idle ceremony, he declared, have no place \
> in "the religions of India". \
> \
> \'bb \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> Minneapolis \
> 21 November 1893 \
> \
> \
> I reached Madison safely, went to a hotel, and sent a message to Mr. Updike. He came to see me. He is a \
> Congregational and so, of course, was not very friendly at first; but in the course of an hour or so became very \
> kind to me, and took me over the whole place and the University. I had a fine audience and $100. Immediately \
> after the lecture I took the night train to Minneapolis. \
> \
> I tried to get the clergymen's ticket, but they could not give me any, not being the headquarters. The thing to be \
> done is to get a permit from every head office of every line in Chicago. Perhaps it is possible for Mr. Hale to get \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> 541 Dearborn Avenue, Chicago, \
> 19th November, 1893. \
> \
> \
> Excuse my delay in answering your letter. I do not know when I will be able to see you again. I am starting \
> tomorrow for Madison and Minneapolis. \
> \
> The English gentleman you speak of is Dr. Momerie of London. He is a well- kn own worker amongst the poor of \
> London and is a very sweet man. You perhaps do not know that the English church was the only religious \
> denomination in the world who did not send to us a representative, and Dr. Momerie came to the Parliament in \
> spite of the Archbishop of Canterbury's denouncing of the Parliament of Religions. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> C/o Leon Landsberg \

### First Unitarian Church

**Location:** First Unitarian Society, Minneapolis, Minnesota, Minneapolis, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Minneapolis venue where Vivekananda lectured


The First Unitarian Church of Minneapolis was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his extensive Midwestern lecture tour. Minneapolis had an active community of liberal religious thinkers, and the Unitarian congregation was among the most receptive to his message of the universality of religion and the philosophical underpinnings of Vedanta. His lectures in Minneapolis drew large and engaged audiences.


**From the Complete Works** (7 references):

> such a manner as to place the faith of Brahma before all. The all-pervading thought and leading principle of the \
> Hindoo religion is the inherent divinity of the soul; the soul is perfect, and religion is the manifestation of divinity \
> already existing in man. The present is merely a line of demarkation between the past and future, and of the two \
> tendencies in man, if the good preponderates he will move to a higher sphere, if the evil has power, he degenerates. \
> These two are continually at work within him; what elevates him is virtue, that which degenerates is evil. \
> \
> Kananda will speak at the First Unitarian Church tomorrow morning. \
> \
> * * * \
> \
> (Des Moines News, November 28,…

> \
> (.Minneapolis Star, November 25, 1893) \
> \

> subject which held an audience in closest attention last evening at the First Unitarian Church [Minneapolis], while \
> Swami Vive Kananda expounded the Hindoo faith. It was an audience which included thoughtful women and men, \
> for the lecturer had been invited by the "Peripatetics," and among the friends who shared the privilege with them \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> WAY TO SALVATION \
> \
> (.Report of a lecture delivered in Oakland on Monday, March 12, 1900, with \
> editorial comments of the Oakland Enquirer) \
> \
> Wendte Hall of the First Unitarian Church was crowded last evening with a large audience to hear the "Way to \
> Salvation" from the standpoint of the Hindu priest, Swami Vivekananda. This was the last lecture of a series of \
> three which the Swami has delivered. He said in part: \
> \
> One man says God is in heaven, another that God is in nature and everywhere present. But when the great crisis \
> comes, we find the goal is the same. We all work on different plans, but the end is not different. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \

> deeply interested in the address and applauded several remarks of the Brahman with much enthusiasm. \

> The love that was dwelt upon by the speaker was not the love that goes with passion, but a pure and holy love that \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> one hand and the Bible in the other, or in South Africa allow children
> of the same Father to cut cach other to pieces. In contrast to this condition
> of things, he described what took place during the last great famine in
> India where men would die of starvation beside their cattle (cows) rather
> than stretch forth a hand to kill... 2”
>
> When the Swami left Los Angeles he was to become the
> guest of the Reverend Dr. Benjamin Fay Mills of Oakland, at
> whose church, the First Unitarian Church of Oakland, he gave
> eight lectures to crowded audiences numbering often as many
> as two thousand persons ; and the mornings following, he would
> find his name blazoned in all the leading newspapers of the
> State.…

*— p. 678*

> Christians and clergymen were his avowed admirers, and in many
> instances even his followers.
>
> Thus one finds the Swami, now in brilliant flashes of wit
> or repartee, now in terms of scathing criticism, uttering his
> views concerning the worth of Westcrn culture, Perhaps, on
> one occasion he would retort with sarcastic humour as he did
> to his questioner in one of his lectures in Minneapolis, when
> asked if Hindu mothers threw their children to the crocodiles in
> the rivers, by saying, “Yes, Madain! ‘Chey threw me in, but like
> your fabled Jonah I got out again.” Or on another occasion he
> would condemn with all seriousness and with remarkable pene-
> tration the aggressive and destructive…

*— p. 330*


---

### Quincy House Hotel

**Location:** Quincy House Hotel, Boston, Massachusetts, Boston, USA
**Period:** 1893
**Significance:** Hotel where Vivekananda first stayed in Boston


The Quincy House Hotel in Boston was among the lodgings used by Swami Vivekananda during his Boston visits. Boston was one of his most important centres in America, and he made multiple extended stays in the city between 1893 and 1896. The intellectual and religious openness of Boston — the home of Transcendentalism, Unitarianism and many progressive movements — made it fertile ground for his message of Vedanta.


**From the Complete Works** (96 references):

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> IS INDIA A BENIGHTED COUNTRY? \
> \
> The following is a report of a lecture at Detroit, United States, America, with the editorial comments of the Boston \
> Evening Transcript, 5th April, 1894: \
> \
> Swami Vivekananda has been in Detroit recently and made a proofed impression there. All classes flocked to hear \
> him, and professional men in particular were greatly interested in his logic and his soundness of thought. The \
> opera-house alone was large enough for his audience. He speaks English extremely well, and he is as handsome as \
> he is good. The Detroit newspapers have devoted much space to the reports of his lectures. An editorial in the \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE MANNERS AND CUSTOMS OF INDIA \
> \
> (.Boston Herald, May 15, 1894) \
> \
> Association Hall was crowded with ladies yesterday, to hear Swami Vivekananda, the Brahmin (Meaning Hindu. \— \
> Publisher .) Monk talk about "The Religion of India" [actually "The Manners and Customs of India"], for the benefit \
> of the ward 16 day nursery [actually, Tyler-street Day Nursery]. The Brahmin monk has become a fad in Boston, as \
> he was in Chicago last year, and his earnest, honest, cultured manner has won many friends for him. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE RELIGIONS OF INDIA \
> \
> (Boston Herald, May 17, 1894) \
> \
> The Brahmin monk, Swami Vivekananda, lectured yesterday afternoon in Association Hall on "The Religions of \
> India", in aid of the Ward 16 Day Nursery. There was a large attendance. \
> \
> The speaker first gave an account of the Mahommedans, who formed, he said, one-fifth of the population. They \
> believed in both Old and New Testaments, but Jesus Christ they regarded only as a prophet. They had no church \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> "When I was in India and saw these things from the outside, I was told it was all right, it was mere pleasantry and I \
> believed it. But I have travelled since then, and I know it is not right. It is wrong, only you of the West shut your \
> eyes and call it good. The trouble with the nations of the West is that they are young, foolish, fickle, and wealthy. \
> What mischief can come of one of these qualities; but when all three, all four, are combined beware!" \
> \
> But severe as the Swami was upon all, Boston received the hardest blow: \
> \
> "Of all, Boston is the worst. There the women are all faddists, all fickle, merely bent on following something new \
> and strange." \
> \
> 39. "Where is the…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> most ardent disciple, even to the point of attending to the
> Swami's personal needs. He would work day and night over
> the Swami’s lecture, taking them down stenographically and
> then typewriting them, all in the same day, in order to hand
> over the manuscripts to the newspapers for publication and

> Swami prized “my faithful Goodwin” as he was wont to speak

> tions, he was frequently the guest of Mr. George W. Hale of
> Chicago. After giving 4 series of lectures in the Unitarian
> Church at Detroit in February, the Swami spent the months of

> acre in New England, where the “Greenacre Conferences”

*— p. 346*

> and to settle permanently in the metropolis. But he thought
> it would be best to give the seeds sown in London time to
> germinate. Besides, he felt that he was being called by the
> Lord. He promised to return to England in the following
> summer and continue the work begun there. He was gratified
> with what he said the Lord had accomplished through him
> and with a new spirit and renewed enthusiasm he turned his
> face again to the group of ardent followers in America. A
> rich Boston lady had promised to support his work throughout
> the coming winter in New York, and everything seemed bright
> and prosperous. Before he left, he advised those who were
> more particularly interested in his teaching to form…

*— p. 390*

> India and its culture. It was also a way to become independent
> and get funds for the various philanthropic and religious works
> in India which he had in mind. Now we see the Swami travell-
> ing hither and thither in Aincrica, visiting numerous cities,
> telling of the glories of India and the greatness of Indian
> culture. He visited all the larger cities of the Eastern and Mid-
> Western States lecturing in Chicago, Iowa City, Des Moines,
> Memphis, Indianapolis, Minneapolis. Detroit, Hartford, Buffalo,
> Boston, Cambridge, Baltimore, Washington, Brooklyn, New York
> and other places. Unfortunately there are but few details of
> this noteworthy tour. Here and there one catches glimpses of
> his illuminating…

*— p. 327*

### Hotel Bellevue

**Location:** Hotel Bellevue, Boston, Massachusetts, Boston, USA
**Period:** 1894-1895
**Significance:** Hotel used by Vivekananda during Boston stays


Hotel Bellevue in Boston was one of the hotels where Swami Vivekananda lodged during his extended visits to the city. Boston remained one of his principal American bases, and he delivered numerous lectures, classes and private talks from various addresses in the city. The Bellevue was part of the network of accommodations that supported his lecture circuit across New England.

### Women's Club of Julia Ward Howe

**Location:** Women's Club, Boston, Massachusetts, Boston, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Prominent Boston club where Vivekananda addressed women reformers


The Women's Club associated with Julia Ward Howe — poet, reformer and author of the 'Battle Hymn of the Republic' — was one of the prestigious venues where Swami Vivekananda spoke in Boston. Julia Ward Howe was a towering figure in American progressive life, and her circle included many of the educated, reform-minded women who became some of Vivekananda's most devoted American supporters, including Sara Bull (Mrs. Ole Bull) and others.

### Round Table

**Location:** Round Table Club, Boston, Massachusetts, Boston, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Boston intellectual society that hosted Vivekananda


The Round Table was an intellectual discussion club in Boston that invited Swami Vivekananda to address its members. Boston's rich tradition of intellectual societies and discussion clubs — a legacy of the Transcendentalist movement — provided an ideal forum for his teachings on Vedanta. Members of such clubs were typically drawn from the educated professional class who were open to exploring Indian philosophy.

### Association Hall

**Location:** Association Hall, Boston, Massachusetts, Boston, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Lecture hall in Boston used by Vivekananda


Association Hall in Boston was one of the public lecture venues used by Swami Vivekananda during his Boston period. He delivered a series of popular lectures here on topics including Hinduism, the philosophy of Vedanta, and the unity of all religions. Boston audiences were among the most intellectually curious he encountered, and Association Hall drew large crowds to hear his talks.


**From the Complete Works** (169 references):

> \'ab \
> \
> SOME CUSTOMS OF THE HINDUS \
> \
> (.Brooklyn Standard Union, April 8, 1895) \
> \
> A special meeting of the Brooklyn Ethical Association with an address by Swami \
> Vivekananda, the Hindu monk as the main feature, was held at the Pouch Gallery, of \
> Clinton avenue, last night. "Some customs of the Hindus what they mean, and how they \
> are misinterpreted," was the subject treated. A large throng of people fdled the spacious \
> gallery. \
> \
> Dressed in his Oriental costume, his eyes bright, and a flush mantling his face, Swami \

> of his Madras friends wrote to some acquaintances in Chicago
> about the Swami, and he was not forced to the extreme

> lasted as long as the Swami lived. All the members of the

> gifts, and to admire the purity and simplicity of his character;
> to which they often bore willing and loving testimony.

> The Swami was told that Boston was much less expensive
> than Chicago. So thinking it advisable he Ieft Chicago for

> he went instantly to the palace. where His Highness warmly
> welcomed him. After the usual formalities he asked him,
> “Swaniiji, what is life? “he monk replied, “Life is the unfold-
> ment and development of a being under circumstances tending
> to press it down.” The Swaini’s own life of hardship and renun-
> ciation caused a world of feeling to appear in his words. Im-
> pressed, the Maharaja next asked, “Well, Swamiji, what then is
> education?” The response was, “I should say, education is the
> nervous association of certain ideas.” And he went on to
> explain this statement, saying that not until ideas had been
> inade instincts could they be reckoned. as real and vital posses-
> sions of consciousness.…

>
> “He is a great favourite at the Parliament from the grandeur of his

### Procopeia Club

**Location:** Procopeia Club, Boston, Massachusetts, Boston, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Boston private club that hosted Vivekananda


The Procopeia Club in Boston was among the private intellectual and social clubs that hosted Swami Vivekananda. Such clubs were a key part of the social infrastructure through which his ideas spread among Boston's educated elite. Members would invite him for intimate lectures and discussions, creating personal connections that formed the backbone of his New England following.


**From the Complete Works** (97 references):

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> IS INDIA A BENIGHTED COUNTRY? \
> \
> The following is a report of a lecture at Detroit, United States, America, with the editorial comments of the Boston \
> Evening Transcript, 5th April, 1894: \
> \
> Swami Vivekananda has been in Detroit recently and made a proofed impression there. All classes flocked to hear \
> him, and professional men in particular were greatly interested in his logic and his soundness of thought. The \
> opera-house alone was large enough for his audience. He speaks English extremely well, and he is as handsome as \
> he is good. The Detroit newspapers have devoted much space to the reports of his lectures. An editorial in the \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE MANNERS AND CUSTOMS OF INDIA \
> \
> (.Boston Herald, May 15, 1894) \
> \
> Association Hall was crowded with ladies yesterday, to hear Swami Vivekananda, the Brahmin (Meaning Hindu. \— \
> Publisher .) Monk talk about "The Religion of India" [actually "The Manners and Customs of India"], for the benefit \
> of the ward 16 day nursery [actually, Tyler-street Day Nursery]. The Brahmin monk has become a fad in Boston, as \
> he was in Chicago last year, and his earnest, honest, cultured manner has won many friends for him. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \

> \'ab \
> \
> THE RELIGIONS OF INDIA \
> \
> (Boston Herald, May 17, 1894) \
> \
> The Brahmin monk, Swami Vivekananda, lectured yesterday afternoon in Association Hall on "The Religions of \
> India", in aid of the Ward 16 Day Nursery. There was a large attendance. \
> \
> The speaker first gave an account of the Mahommedans, who formed, he said, one-fifth of the population. They \
> believed in both Old and New Testaments, but Jesus Christ they regarded only as a prophet. They had no church \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> "When I was in India and saw these things from the outside, I was told it was all right, it was mere pleasantry and I \
> believed it. But I have travelled since then, and I know it is not right. It is wrong, only you of the West shut your \
> eyes and call it good. The trouble with the nations of the West is that they are young, foolish, fickle, and wealthy. \
> What mischief can come of one of these qualities; but when all three, all four, are combined beware!" \
> \
> But severe as the Swami was upon all, Boston received the hardest blow: \
> \
> "Of all, Boston is the worst. There the women are all faddists, all fickle, merely bent on following something new \
> and strange." \
> \
> 39. "Where is the…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Chicago. After giving 4 series of lectures in the Unitarian

> and to settle permanently in the metropolis. But he thought
> it would be best to give the seeds sown in London time to

> with what he said the Lord had accomplished through him
> and with a new spirit and renewed enthusiasm he turned his
> face again to the group of ardent followers in America. A

> and prosperous. Before he left, he advised those who were
> more particularly interested in his teaching to form…

### Hall at 56 St Botolph St

**Location:** 56 St Botolph Street, Boston, Massachusetts, Boston, USA
**Period:** 1894-1895
**Significance:** Boston address associated with Vivekananda's class lectures


The hall at 56 St Botolph Street in Boston was associated with the series of classes and lectures that Swami Vivekananda delivered during his Boston period. The Back Bay neighbourhood, in which St Botolph Street is located, was the heart of Boston's cultivated intellectual and artistic community. Vivekananda held classes here on Raja Yoga, Jnana Yoga and the philosophy of Vedanta for small gatherings of devoted students.

### Twentieth Century Club

**Location:** Twentieth Century Club, Boston, Massachusetts, Boston, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Progressive Boston club that hosted Vivekananda


The Twentieth Century Club in Boston, a forum for progressive discussion on social, literary and philosophical topics, invited Swami Vivekananda to address its members. The club attracted Boston's intellectually progressive community, and Vivekananda's message of Vedanta — with its emphasis on the universality of religion, the divinity of the human soul, and practical ethics — resonated deeply with its membership.

### Charles Fairchild home

**Location:** Charles Fairchild Home, Boston, Massachusetts, Boston, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Boston home of devoted supporter Charles Fairchild


Charles Fairchild was one of the devoted Boston supporters of Swami Vivekananda. His home in Boston was a gathering place for Vivekananda's circle of admirers in the city, who included prominent figures from Boston's intellectual and social elite. The Fairchild household was part of the network of private homes that sustained Vivekananda's work in New England and helped spread the message of Vedanta among Boston's educated community.

### Breezy Meadows

**Location:** Breezy Meadows, Metcalf, Massachusetts, Metcalf, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Summer retreat in Metcalf associated with Vivekananda


Breezy Meadows in Metcalf, Massachusetts was a summer retreat where Swami Vivekananda stayed during his New England period. The pleasant countryside around Metcalf provided a welcome respite from the busy lecture circuit, and Vivekananda used such retreats for rest, study and private instruction of his closer disciples. The rural Massachusetts landscape offered a contemplative setting for his ongoing teaching work.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references):

> \
> \'ab Addressee \
> \
> IV \
> \
> \

### Boston Ladies Club

**Location:** Boston Ladies Club, Massachusetts, Boston, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Women's club where Vivekananda lectured in Massachusetts


The Boston Ladies Club was one of the women's clubs in the Boston area that hosted Swami Vivekananda for lectures. Women formed a significant proportion of his American audiences and supporters, attracted by his message of the spiritual equality of all human beings regardless of gender, caste or nationality. His addresses to women's clubs across New England helped build his broad network of supporters.


**From the Complete Works** (96 references):

> him, and professional men in particular were greatly interested in his logic and his soundness of thought. The \
> opera-house alone was large enough for his audience. He speaks English extremely well, and he is as handsome as \
> he is good. The Detroit newspapers have devoted much space to the reports of his lectures. An editorial in the \

### Sherborn Reformatory

**Location:** State Industrial School for Girls, Sherborn, Massachusetts, Sherborn, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Massachusetts reformatory where Vivekananda gave a talk


Swami Vivekananda delivered a talk at the State Industrial School (Reformatory) in Sherborn, Massachusetts, in 1894. His willingness to speak at such an institution — reaching out to society's most marginalised — reflected his deep conviction that the divine exists in all human beings, however unfortunate their circumstances. This visit embodied his practical application of the Vedantic principle that service to humanity is service to God.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (1 references):

> 296 LIFE OF SWAMI VIVEKANANDA
>
> ing that nothing worth while was ever accomplished without
> suffering and sacrifice. Here he met the Lady Superintendent
> of the Sherborn Reformatory for women, who had come to see
> him at the wish of his hostess. She invited him to visit the
> Reformatory. The Swami writes thus of his thoughts and
> impressions of the visit to a disciple:
>
> “They do not call it prison but reformatory here. It is the grandest
> thing I have seen in Amcrica. How the inmates are benevolently treated ;
> how they are reformed, and sent back as uscful members of society ; how

*— p. 307*

### Charlotte Lane Boarding house

**Location:** Charlotte Lane, Annisquam, Massachusetts, Gloucester, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Summer boarding house in Annisquam where Vivekananda stayed


The Charlotte Lane Boarding House in Annisquam, the picturesque fishing village within Gloucester on Cape Ann, was a summer residence for Swami Vivekananda during his New England tours. He spent time in Annisquam in 1894 at the invitation of his Boston supporters, and the peaceful seaside village offered an ideal setting for writing, teaching and rest. Several of his American disciples joined him there for instruction.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

---

### The Hyatt House

**Location:** The Hyatt House, Annisquam, Massachusetts, Gloucester, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Annisquam retreat house associated with Vivekananda


The Hyatt House in Annisquam, Massachusetts, was one of the summer residences associated with Swami Vivekananda's stays in this Cape Ann village. He spent several weeks in Annisquam during the summer of 1894, working on his lectures and classes on Raja Yoga, which were later published as the foundational Vedanta text 'Raja Yoga'. The tranquil coastal setting supported his intensive period of writing and teaching.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

### Universalist Church

**Location:** Universalist Church, Annisquam, Massachusetts, Gloucester, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Annisquam church where Vivekananda addressed the community


The Universalist Church in Annisquam, Massachusetts was a venue where Swami Vivekananda addressed the local community during his summer visits to this Cape Ann village. Universalist congregations, with their belief in universal salvation and spiritual openness, provided a welcoming platform for his message of Vedanta. Even in the small fishing community of Annisquam, his presence drew curious and receptive audiences.


**From the Complete Works** (3 references):

### Mechanics Hall

**Location:** Mechanics Hall, Boston, Massachusetts, Boston, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Large public hall in Boston where Vivekananda lectured


Mechanics Hall in Boston was one of the large public lecture venues where Swami Vivekananda addressed audiences during his Boston period. The hall had a long tradition of hosting public lectures, and Vivekananda's talks on Vedanta, Raja Yoga and the science of religion drew sizeable crowds. His Boston lectures were among the most widely attended of his American tour, reflecting the city's keen intellectual interest in Indian philosophy.
