### Kate Tannatt Woods's house

**Location:** Kate Tannatt Woods's House, Salem, Massachusetts, Salem, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Salem home of author and supporter Kate Tannatt Woods


Kate Tannatt Woods was an American author and journalist who became a supporter of Swami Vivekananda. Her home in Salem, Massachusetts was one of the private residences where he was received during his New England lecture tours. Woods was part of the circle of progressive, literary-minded American women who were drawn to Vivekananda's teachings and helped promote his work in their communities.


**From the Complete Works** (19 references):

> The speaker explained his mission in his country to be to organize monks for industrial purposes, that they might \

> by invitation of the pastor and Professor Wright of Harvard, who has shown him great kindness. \
> \

### Wesley Church

**Location:** Wesley Church, Salem, Massachusetts, Salem, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Salem church where Vivekananda lectured


Wesley Church in Salem, Massachusetts was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his New England tour. Salem, the historic city associated with the Puritan tradition and the witch trials, had by the late nineteenth century developed a thriving intellectual and reform community. Methodist congregations like Wesley Church occasionally opened their pulpits to Vivekananda as interest in comparative religion grew across American denominations.


**From the Complete Works** (19 references):

> \
> The speaker explained his mission in his country to be to organize monks for industrial purposes, that they might \

### East Church

**Location:** East Church, Salem, Massachusetts, Salem, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Salem church venue for Vivekananda lectures


The East Church in Salem, Massachusetts was among the historic Congregational churches in this ancient New England town that hosted Swami Vivekananda for a lecture during his New England tour. Salem's long tradition of religious inquiry — even if turbulent in its early history — had evolved by Vivekananda's time into a thoughtful openness to comparative religion, and he found receptive audiences here.


---

### Northampton City Hall

**Location:** Northampton City Hall, Northampton, Massachusetts, Northampton, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Civic venue in Northampton where Vivekananda lectured


Northampton City Hall in western Massachusetts was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his New England tour. Northampton, a prosperous town in the Connecticut River valley and home to Smith College, provided a cultivated audience for his talks on Vedanta and the universal religion. His willingness to lecture in civic halls as well as churches reflected his democratic approach to sharing the message of Vedanta.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> AN EVENING WITH OUR HINDU COUSINS \
> \
> (.Northampton Daily Herald, April 16, 1894) \
> \
> For Swami Vive Kananda proved conclusively that all our neighbors across the water, even the remotest, are our \
> close cousins differing only a trifle in color, language, customs and religion, the silver-tongued Hindu monk \
> prefacing his address in city hall Saturday evening [April 14] by an historic sketch of the origin of his own and all \
> other leading nations of the earth which demonstrated the truth that race-kinship is more of a simple fact than many \
> kn ow or always care to admit. \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Smith College

**Location:** Smith College, Northampton, Massachusetts, Northampton, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Women's college where Vivekananda lectured


Smith College in Northampton, Massachusetts — one of the leading women's colleges in America — was a venue where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his New England tour. His message of the spiritual equality of women and the recognition of the divine in all human beings was naturally welcomed at a women's institution. Educated American women formed a crucial constituency among his earliest American devotees.


**From the Complete Works** (16 references):

### Oxford Club lecture hall

**Location:** Oxford Club, Lynn, Massachusetts, Lynn, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Lynn venue for Vivekananda lecture


The Oxford Club lecture hall in Lynn, Massachusetts was a venue where Swami Vivekananda addressed a local audience during his New England circuit. Lynn, the historic shoe-manufacturing city north of Boston, had a community of progressive thinkers who invited him to speak. His lecture on Vedanta philosophy and the universal principles of religion drew an engaged audience from this working-class Massachusetts city.


**From the Complete Works** (13 references):

> \
> Detroit, \
> 15th March, 1894. \
> \
> \
> I am pulling on well with old Palmer. He is a very jolly, good old man. I got only 127 dollars by my last lecture. I \
> am going to speak again in Detroit on Monday. Your mother asked me to write to a lady in Lynn. I have never \
> seen her. Is it etiquette to write without any introduction? Please post me a little letter about this lady. Where is \
> Lynn? The funniest thing said about me here was in one of the papers which said, "The cyclonic Hindu has come \
> and is a guest with Mr. Palmer. Mr. Palmer has become a Hindu and is going to India; only he insists that two \
> reforms should be carried out: firstly that the Car of Jagannath should be drawn by…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> 528 Fifth Avenue] \
> New York \
> 10 April 1894 \
> \
> Dear Mother, \
> \
> I just now received your letter. I have the greatest regard for the Salvationists; in fact, they and the Oxford Mission \
> gentlemen are the only Christian missionaries for whom I have any regard at all. They live with the people, as the \
> people, and for the people of India. Lord bless them. But I would be very, very sorry of any trick being played by \
> them. I never have heard of any Lord in India, much less in Ceylon. (Now Sri Lanka.) The people of Ceylon and \
> northern India differ more than Americans and Hindus. Nor is there any connection between the Buddhist priest \
> and the Hindu. Our dress, manners, religion, food,…

> befriend me. Of course he met with universal contempt. I am quite astonished at this man\'s behaviour \— a preacher \
> of religion to take to such underhand dealings! Unfortunately too much of that in every country and in every \

> Society. He…

### Rockland Street

**Location:** Rockland Street, Swampscott, Massachusetts, Swampscott, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Swampscott address associated with Vivekananda's stay


Rockland Street in Swampscott, Massachusetts was associated with Swami Vivekananda's visits to this seaside town north of Boston. Swampscott, a pleasant resort community on the Atlantic shore, attracted Boston's educated middle class in summer, and several of Vivekananda's supporters had residences here. He visited Swampscott during his New England tours and held informal gatherings and talks for interested residents.


**From the Complete Works** (5 references):

> \
> Our Sanskrit poets lavish all the power of expression they have upon the soft body of women \— the Sanskrit word \
> for women is "Komala", the soft body; but the mailclad ones of this country are "armadillas", I think. You cannot \
> imagine how ludicrous it appears to a foreigner who never saw it before. Shiva, Shiva. \
> \
> Now Narasimha\'s Mrs. Smith does not torture you anymore with letters, I hope. Did I tell you I met your friend \
> Mrs. H. O. Quarry at Swampscott? \— she can swamp a house for all that, not to speak of a cott \— and that I met \
> there the woman that pulls by the nose Mr. Pullman?! And I also heard there the best American singer, (Miss Emma \
> Thursby.) they said \— she sang…

### Library Hall

**Location:** Library Hall, Lawrence, Massachusetts, Lawrence, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Lawrence venue for Vivekananda lecture


Library Hall in Lawrence, Massachusetts was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his New England tour. Lawrence, the textile-manufacturing city on the Merrimack River, had a diverse immigrant population and a tradition of public lectures. Vivekananda's appearances in industrial cities like Lawrence demonstrated his commitment to bringing the message of Vedanta to all segments of American society, not merely the educated elite.

### Rogers Hall

**Location:** Rogers Hall, Melrose, Massachusetts, Melrose, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Melrose lecture hall used by Vivekananda


Rogers Hall in Melrose, Massachusetts was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his tour of the towns north of Boston. Melrose, a prosperous residential community, had an active cultural and lecture-going community. Vivekananda's talks in such suburban venues helped spread the message of Vedanta beyond the major cities and into the comfortable New England communities that formed the backbone of his American support.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> \
> \
> Boston, \
> 26th Sept., 1894. \
> \
> \
> I have received both of your kind notes. I will have to go back to Melrose on Saturday and remain there till \
> Monday. On Tuesday I will come over to your place. But I have forgotten the exact location. If you kindly write \
> me that, I cannot express my gratitude for your kindness. For that is exactly what I wanted, a quiet place to write. \
> Of course, much less space will suffice me than what you have kindly proposed to put at my disposal, I can bundle \
> myself up anywhere and feel quite comfortable. \
> \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*

### Evans Caswell's house

**Location:** Evans Caswell's House, Melrose, Massachusetts, Melrose, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Melrose private home where Vivekananda was hosted


Evans Caswell's home in Melrose, Massachusetts was one of the private residences where Swami Vivekananda was received during his New England tours. The pattern of visits to private homes for intimate discussions and talks was central to his method of teaching in America, where the personal connection established in a drawing-room setting often proved more transformative than public lectures.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> \
> \
> Boston, \

### Magnolia Library

**Location:** Magnolia Library, Magnolia, Massachusetts, Gloucester, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Library in Magnolia where Vivekananda spoke


The Magnolia Library in Magnolia, Massachusetts — a fashionable summer colony within Gloucester on Cape Ann — was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his New England visits. Libraries and reading rooms were important community gathering places in Victorian America, and Magnolia's summer residents, drawn from Boston's professional class, provided an educated and receptive audience for his talks on Vedanta philosophy.


**From the Complete Works** (3 references):

> Annisquam \
> Date do not know \
> [Postmarked: August 28, 1894] \
> \
> Dear Mother \
> \
> I have been for three days at Magnolia. Magnolia is one of the most fashionable and beautiful seaside resorts of \
> this part. I think the scenery is better than that of Annisquam. The rocks there are very beautiful, and the forests run \
> down to the very edge of the water. There is a very beautiful pine forest. A lady of Chicago and her daughter, Mrs. \
> Smith and Mrs. Sawyer, were the friends that invited me up there. They had also arranged a lecture for me, out of \
> which I got $43.1 met a good many Boston people \— Mrs. Smith Junior, who said she kn ows Harriet, and Mrs. \
> Smith the elder, [who] knows you…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> What is Father Pope doing? What the Babies are doing and where are they? What news of our Sam?! Hope he is \
> prospering. Kindly give him my best love. Where is Mother Temple now? \
> \
> Well, after all, I could fill up two pages. Yes, there was a Miss Bam (?) who said she met me at your house. She is \
> a young lady of Chicago. \
> \
> Magnolia is a good bathing place and I had two baths in the sea. A large concourse of men and women go to bathe \
> there every day \— the most part men. And strange, women do not give up their coat of mail even while bathing. \
> That is how these mailclad she-warriors of America have got the superiority over men. \
> \
> Our Sanskrit poets lavish all the power of…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \

> \

> The bundle was the report of the meeting. Hope you will succeed in publishing some in the Chicago papers. \

---

### Gray Beach

**Location:** Gray Beach, Magnolia, Massachusetts, Gloucester, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Coastal location in Magnolia associated with Vivekananda


Gray Beach in Magnolia, Massachusetts was associated with Swami Vivekananda's summer stays in this Cape Ann resort village. The rocky Atlantic coastline of Magnolia, with its bracing sea air and dramatic scenery, provided a contemplative backdrop for his work. Like the rocks at Kanyakumari where he had his famous meditation in 1892, the seashore held a special significance for him as a place of spiritual reflection.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> To Mrs. G. W. Hale \
> \
> \
> Dear Mother \
> \
> \
> [Gloucester, Massachusetts] \
> 4 September 1894 \
> \
> \
> The bundle was the report of the meeting. Hope you will succeed in publishing some in the Chicago papers. \
> \
> Here is a letter from Dcwanji! to you which will explain his sending a pamphlet to Mr. Hale. * The rugs are \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Davis Opera House

**Location:** Davis Opera House, Plymouth, Massachusetts, Plymouth, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Plymouth venue for Vivekananda lecture


The Davis Opera House in Plymouth, Massachusetts, the historic 'Plymouth Rock' town that was the landing place of the Pilgrims, was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his New England tour. The irony was not lost on him that he was bringing the ancient wisdom of India to the very town most associated with the Puritan founding of America. He saw in such encounters a fulfilment of the destiny of East and West to meet and enrich each other.


**From the Complete Works** (3 references):

> think of such gentlemen. Moreover, the same missionary went privately to some of my friends to ask them not to \
> befriend me. Of course he met with universal contempt. I am quite astonished at this man\'s behaviour \— a preacher \
> of religion to take to such underhand dealings! Unfortunately too much of that in every country and in every \
> religion. Last winter I travelled a good deal in this country although the weather was very severe. I thought it would \
> be dreadful, but I did not find it so after all. You remember Col. Neggenson, President of the Free Religious \
> Society. He makes very kind inquiries about you. I met Dr. Carpenter of Oxford (England) the other day. He \
> delivered an…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> will never put me in. His help alone is sufficient for me. I have not heard anything from you nor from Mother. \
> Neither have I any news from India as to the arrival of the phonograph. \
> \
> If there was anything in my letter to you which was offensive, I hope you all know that I meant everything in love. \
> It is useless to express my gratitude to you for your kindness. Lord bless you and shower His choicest blessings on \
> you and those you love. To your family I am ever, ever beholden. You know it. You feel it. I cannot express it. On \
> Sunday I am going to lecture at Plymouth at the "Sympathy of Religions" meetings of Col. Higginson. Herewith I \
> send a photograph Cora Stockham took of the…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> 8 August 1894 \
> \
> Dear Mother \
> \
> I have received the letter you sent over to me coming from India. \
> \
> I am going to leave this place on Monday next for Plymouth [Massachusetts], where the Free Religious \
> Association* is holding its session. They will defray my expenses, of course. \
> \
> I am all right, enjoying nice health, and the people here are very k in d and nice to me. Up to date I had no occasion \
> to cash any cheque as everything is going on smoothly. I have not heard anything from the Babies. Hope they are \
> doing well. You also had nothing to write; however, I feel that you are doing well. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*

### Radcliffe College(Fay House)

**Location:** Radcliffe College, Fay House, Cambridge, Massachusetts, Cambridge, USA
**Period:** 1896
**Significance:** Women's college at Harvard where Vivekananda lectured


Radcliffe College (now Radcliffe Institute for Advanced Study at Harvard), with its historic Fay House, was a venue where Swami Vivekananda addressed students and faculty during his Cambridge period. As the coordinate women's college affiliated with Harvard, Radcliffe attracted the most intellectually ambitious women in America. Vivekananda's series of lectures in Cambridge in 1896 were among the most academically rigorous he delivered in America.


**From the Complete Works** (24 references):

> London, S.W. \
> 6th July, 1896. \
> \
> Dear Friend And Brother, (to Dr. Lewis i. Janes.) \
> \
> Yours of the 25th June has duly reached and gave me great pleasure. I am so glad to see the noble work \
> progressing. I had leamt with the greatest delight from Mrs. Bull of the work that is going to be done in Cambridge \
> this winter and no better person could have been selected to direct it as yourself. May all power attend you. I will \
> be only too glad to write for the magazine from time to time and my first instalment was to be in a few weeks, \
> when I hope to get some leisure. Certainly it goes without saying that no one of the types we call religious ought to \
> die \— they like races require…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> cheque. Perhaps you have come to know by this time of my being in Greenacre. I had a very nice time here and am \
> enjoying it immensely. In the fall I am going to lecture in Brooklyn, New York. Yesterday I got news that they \
> have completed all the advertising there. I have an invitation today from a friend in New York to go with him to \
> some mountains north of this state of Maine. I do not know whether I will go or not. I am doing pretty well. \
> Between lecturing, teaching, picnicking and other excitements the time is flying rapidly. I hope you are doing very \
> well and that Father Pope is in good trim. It is a very beautiful spot \— this Greenacre \— and [I] have very nice \
> company…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> C/o E. Guernsey, M.D., \
> The Madrid, 180 W. 59, \
> 15th November, 1899. \
> \
> My Dear Mrs. Bull, \
> \
> After all I decide to come to Cambridge just now. I must finish the stories I began. The first one I don't think was \
> given back to me by Margo. \
> \
> My clothes will be ready the day after tomorrow, and then I shall be ready to start; only my fear is, it will be for \
> the whole winter a place for becoming nervous and not for quieting of nerves, with constant parties and lectures. \
> Well, perhaps you can give me a room somewhere, where I can hide myself from all the goings on in the place. \
> Again I am so nervous of going to a place where indirectly the Indian Math will be. The very name of…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> San Francisco, \
> 12th March, 1900. \
> \
> My Dear Dhira Mata, \
> \
> Your letter from Cambridge came yesterday. Now I have got a fixed address, 1719 Turk Street, San Francisco. \

> back as you desired; besides that, I had no other accounts. It is all right. \

### Sever Hall

**Location:** Sever Hall, Harvard University, Cambridge, Massachusetts, Cambridge, USA
**Period:** 1896
**Significance:** Harvard hall where Vivekananda lectured


Sever Hall at Harvard University was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his Cambridge period in 1896. Designed by H.H. Richardson, Sever Hall was one of Harvard's principal lecture and classroom buildings. Vivekananda's lectures at Harvard — before one of the world's most distinguished academic audiences — represented a high point in his American mission, as he presented Vedanta as a rigorous philosophical system worthy of the highest intellectual engagement.


---

### Sara Ole Bull House

**Location:** Sara Ole Bull's House, Cambridge, Massachusetts, Cambridge, USA
**Period:** 1894-1896
**Significance:** Cambridge home of devotee Sara Bull who was Vivekananda's principal American patron


The Cambridge home of Sara Chapman Bull (Mrs. Ole Bull), widow of the Norwegian violinist Ole Bull, was one of Swami Vivekananda's most important American addresses. Sara Bull was his most devoted and generous American patron, providing him with financial support and a welcoming home during his Boston and Cambridge periods. She later funded the founding of Vedanta Society centres and continued to support his work until her death. Her elegant Cambridge home was a hub of Vivekananda's New England circle.

> when I hope to get some leisure. Certainly it goes without saying that no one of the types we call religious ought to \
> die \— they like races require…

### Mrs. Anna Richards House

**Location:** Mrs. Anna Richards' House, Cambridge, Massachusetts, Cambridge, USA
**Period:** 1894-1896
**Significance:** Cambridge home of devotee Mrs. Anna Richards


Mrs. Anna Richards' home in Cambridge, Massachusetts was one of the private residences associated with Swami Vivekananda's Cambridge circle. Anna Richards was among the devoted women supporters who formed the core of his New England following. Her home provided a welcoming space for the intimate gatherings and discussions that complemented his public lectures and helped deepen the commitment of his closest American disciples.

### Dane Hall

**Location:** Dane Hall, Harvard University, Cambridge, Massachusetts, Cambridge, USA
**Period:** 1896
**Significance:** Harvard hall associated with Vivekananda's Cambridge lectures


Dane Hall at Harvard University was associated with Swami Vivekananda's lectures and meetings during his Cambridge period. Harvard's academic buildings provided the setting for some of his most intellectual engagements in America. His lectures in Cambridge were later reported in newspapers and drew the attention of distinguished scholars including William James and other leaders of American academic life.


**From the Complete Works** (28 references):

> \
> During the London season, Swami Vivekananda has been teaching and lecturing to considerable numbers of people \
> who have been attracted by his doctrine and philosophy. Most English people fancy that England has the practical \
> monopoly of missionary enterprise, almost unbroken save for a small effort on the part of France. I therefore \
> sought the Swami in his temporary home in South Belgravia to enquire what message India could possibly send to \
> England, apart from the remonstrances she has too often had to make on the subject of home charges, judicial and \
> executive functions combined in one person, the settlement of expenses connected with Sudanese and other \
> expeditions. \
> \
> "It is…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> compelled to resort to small country-boats on account of the shallowness of the river at Santipur. In 1797 Captain \
> Colebrook writes that country-boats could not ply in the Hooghly and the Jalangi during summer. During the years \
> 1822-1884, the Hooghly was closed to all boat-traffic. For twenty-four years within this period the water was only \
> two or three feet deep. In the seventeenth century, the Dutch planted a trade settlement at Chinsura, one mile below \
> Hooghly. The French, who came still later, established their settlement at Chandemagore, still further down the \
> river. In 1723 the German Ostend Company opened a factory at Bankipore, five miles below Chandemagore on the \
> other…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> utility which brings to man creature-comforts. Even religion, God, eternity, soul, none of these is of any use to \
> them, as they do not bring them money or physical comfort. To such, all those things which do not go to gratify the \
> senses and appease the appetites are of no utility. In every mind, utility, however, is conditioned by its own \
> peculiar wants. To men, therefore, who never rise higher than eating, drinking, begetting progeny, and dying, the \
> only gain is in sense enjoyments; and they must wait and go through many more births and reincarnations to leam \
> to feel even the faintest necessity for anything higher. But those to whom the eternal interests of the soul are of \
> much…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Prof.J.H Wright's house

**Location:** Prof. John Henry Wright's House, Cambridge, Massachusetts, Cambridge, USA
**Period:** 1893
**Significance:** Cambridge home of Prof. John Henry Wright who helped Vivekananda attend the Parliament


Professor John Henry Wright of Harvard University played a pivotal role in Swami Vivekananda's American career. When Vivekananda arrived in Boston in 1893 without credentials to attend the Parliament of the World's Religions, Prof. Wright — a Greek scholar who was deeply impressed by his knowledge — wrote him a letter of introduction, famously saying: 'To ask for your credentials is like asking the sun to state its right to shine.' This letter secured Vivekananda's place at the Parliament. Wright's Cambridge home was where Vivekananda stayed before travelling to Chicago.


**From the Complete Works** (24 references):

> London, S.W. \
> 6th July, 1896. \
> \
> Dear Friend And Brother, (to Dr. Lewis i. Janes.) \
> \
> Yours of the 25th June has duly reached and gave me great pleasure. I am so glad to see the noble work \
> progressing. I had leamt with the greatest delight from Mrs. Bull of the work that is going to be done in Cambridge \
> this winter and no better person could have been selected to direct it as yourself. May all power attend you. I will \
> be only too glad to write for the magazine from time to time and my first instalment was to be in a few weeks, \
> when I hope to get some leisure. Certainly it goes without saying that no one of the types we call religious ought to \
> die \— they like races require…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> cheque. Perhaps you have come to know by this time of my being in Greenacre. I had a very nice time here and am \
> enjoying it immensely. In the fall I am going to lecture in Brooklyn, New York. Yesterday I got news that they \
> have completed all the advertising there. I have an invitation today from a friend in New York to go with him to \
> some mountains north of this state of Maine. I do not know whether I will go or not. I am doing pretty well. \
> Between lecturing, teaching, picnicking and other excitements the time is flying rapidly. I hope you are doing very \
> well and that Father Pope is in good trim. It is a very beautiful spot \— this Greenacre \— and [I] have very nice \
> company…

> \

> \

> the whole winter a place for becoming nervous and not for quieting of nerves, with constant parties and lectures. \
> Well, perhaps you can give me a room somewhere, where I can hide myself from all the goings on in the place. \

### Memorial Hall

**Location:** Memorial Hall, Harvard University, Cambridge, Massachusetts, Cambridge, USA
**Period:** 1896
**Significance:** Harvard's great hall where Vivekananda lectured


Memorial Hall at Harvard University — the great Victorian Gothic building erected in memory of Harvard's Civil War dead — was a prominent lecture venue associated with Swami Vivekananda's Cambridge period. His lectures at Harvard in the spring of 1896, attended by faculty, students and Cambridge society, were a landmark in his American mission. The Harvard lectures demonstrated that Vedanta philosophy could hold its own in the most demanding academic environment in America.

### Holworthy Hall

**Location:** Holworthy Hall, Harvard University, Cambridge, Massachusetts, Cambridge, USA
**Period:** 1896
**Significance:** Harvard dormitory associated with Vivekananda's Cambridge visit


Holworthy Hall, one of the oldest dormitories at Harvard University, was associated with Swami Vivekananda's visits to the Harvard campus during his Cambridge period in 1896. The ancient Harvard Yard, with its historic brick buildings, provided the atmospheric setting for his lectures and meetings with American scholars and students who were deeply curious about Indian philosophy and the Vedantic tradition.

### Prof. James' house

**Location:** Prof. William James' House, Cambridge, Massachusetts, Cambridge, USA
**Period:** 1896
**Significance:** Home of philosopher William James who met Vivekananda


The Cambridge home of Professor William James — the eminent philosopher and psychologist and founder of pragmatism — was where Swami Vivekananda met one of America's greatest thinkers. James and Vivekananda had a profound mutual respect. James, who had written 'The Varieties of Religious Experience,' was deeply interested in the psychology of mysticism and found in Vivekananda a living embodiment of the mystical consciousness he had theorised. James reportedly said of Vivekananda: 'He is a man who is above everything.' Their exchanges in Cambridge enriched both thinkers.

### MacLeod's house

**Location:** Josephine MacLeod's House, Cambridge, Massachusetts, Cambridge, USA
**Period:** 1896
**Significance:** Cambridge home of devotee Josephine MacLeod


The Cambridge home of Josephine MacLeod (affectionately called 'Joe Joe' by Vivekananda) was one of his important addresses during his New England period. Josephine MacLeod was one of his most devoted American disciples, who later followed him to India and remained a lifelong friend and supporter. Her home in the Cambridge area was a gathering place for his inner circle of American devotees during his Cambridge lecture series of 1896.


**From the Complete Works** (29 references):

> \
> \
> MARRIAGE \
> \
> (New Discoveries , Vol. 5, p. 138.) \
> \
> From Miss Josephine MacLeod's February 1908 letter to Mary Hale, in which she described Swami Vivekananda's \
> response to Alberta Sturges's question: \
> \
> Alberta Sturges: Is there no happiness in marriage? \
> \
> Swami Vivekananda: Yes, Alberta, if marriage is entered into as a great austerity \— and everything is given up \
> \— even principle! \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> 6th July, 1896. \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> Srinagar, Kashmir, \
> 28th August, 1898. \
> \
> \
> I could not make an earlier opportunity of writing you, and knowing that you were in no hurry for a letter, I will \
> not make apologies. You are learning all about Kashmir and ourselves from Miss MacLeod's letter to Mrs. Leggett, \
> I hear \— therefore needless going into long rigmaroles about it. \
> \
> The search for Heinsholdf s Mahatmas in Kashmir will be entirely fruitless; and as the whole thing has first to be \

### Grand Army Hall

**Location:** Grand Army Hall, Medford, Massachusetts, Medford, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Medford civic hall where Vivekananda lectured


The Grand Army Hall in Medford, Massachusetts — named after the Grand Army of the Republic, the Civil War veterans' organisation — was a civic lecture venue where Swami Vivekananda addressed a local audience. These community halls in the towns north of Boston provided accessible public forums for his message of Vedanta, reaching audiences beyond the metropolitan centres and the private clubs of the intellectual elite.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*

### Mrs. Emily Ruth Gibson's house

**Location:** Mrs. Emily Ruth Gibson's House, Medford, Massachusetts, Medford, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Medford home of supporter Mrs. Emily Ruth Gibson


Mrs. Emily Ruth Gibson's home in Medford, Massachusetts was one of the private residences where Swami Vivekananda was hosted during his New England lecture circuit. Mrs. Gibson was part of the network of progressive, spiritually interested women supporters who organised his visits to communities north of Boston. Her home was likely the setting for an intimate lecture or discussion session for a small circle of interested Medford residents.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*

### Sanatorium

**Location:** Sanatorium, Saratoga Springs, New York, Saratoga Springs, USA
**Period:** 1895
**Significance:** Saratoga Springs health resort visited by Vivekananda


Swami Vivekananda visited a sanatorium in Saratoga Springs, the famous spa and resort town in upstate New York. Saratoga Springs, known for its natural mineral springs and fashionable social scene, attracted America's wealthy elite. Vivekananda visited the town during his New York period, meeting with interested supporters and lecturing to the summer society that gathered here. The mineral springs and health-seeking atmosphere of Saratoga resonated with his interest in the relationship between physical and spiritual wellbeing.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references):

> I am so glad you are enjoying Japan \— especially Japanese art. You are perfectly correct in saying that we will \
> have to learn many things from Japan. The help that Japan will give us will be with great sympathy and respect, \
> whereas that from the West unsympathetic and destructive. Certainly it is very desirable to establish a connection \
> between India and Japan. \
> \
> As for me, I was thrown hors de combat in Assam. The climate of the Math is just reviving me a bit. At Shillong \
> \— the hill sanatorium of Assam \— I had fever, asthma, increase of albumen, and my body swelled to almost twice \
> ills normal size. These symptoms subsided, however, as soon as I reached the Math. It is…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Your professor of Sanskrit in his note to Miss Sanborn mistakes me for Purushottama Joshi and states that there is a \
> Sanskrit library in Boston the like of which can scarcely be met with in India. I would be so happy to see it. \
> \
> Mr. Sanborn has written to me to come over to Saratoga on Monday and I am going accordingly. I would stop then \
> at a boarding house called Sanatorium. If any news come from Chicago in the meanwhile I hope you will kindly \
> send it over to the Sanatorium, Saratoga. \
> \
> You and your noble wife and sweet children have made an impression in my brain which is…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Town Hall

**Location:** Town Hall, Saratoga Springs, New York, Saratoga Springs, USA
**Period:** 1895
**Significance:** Saratoga Springs civic venue for Vivekananda lecture


The Town Hall in Saratoga Springs, New York was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his visits to this famous upstate New York spa resort. Saratoga Springs attracted summer visitors from New York City's wealthy and intellectual classes, and the Town Hall provided a dignified civic setting for his public lectures on Vedanta, the universal religion and the philosophies of ancient India.

### Fishkill Landing - Beacon

**Location:** Fishkill Landing (now Beacon), New York, Beacon, USA
**Period:** 1895
**Significance:** Hudson River town where Vivekananda visited supporters


Fishkill Landing (renamed Beacon in 1913) is a town on the eastern shore of the Hudson River in Dutchess County, New York. Swami Vivekananda visited this Hudson Valley community during his New York period, meeting with local supporters. The picturesque Hudson River valley, with its dramatic landscapes, was a region he traversed in his visits to various New York communities outside the city.


**From the Complete Works** (12 references):

> But to those whose thirst for life has been quenched for ever by drinking from the stream of immortality that flows \
> from far away beyond the world of the senses, whose souls have cast away \— as a serpent its slough \— the \
> threefold bandages of lust, gold, and fame, who, from their height of calmness, look with love and compassion \
> upon the petty quarrels and jealousies and fights for little gilded puff-balls, filled with dust, called "enjoyment" by \
> those under a sense-bondage; to those whose accumulated force of past good deeds has caused the scales of \
> ignorance to fall off from their eyes, making them see through the vanity of name and fonn \— to such wheresoever \
> they be,…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Think of all this mass of energy that our nation displays \— where does it get it? In India, they are the producers \
> and you are the enjoyers, no doubt. They produced this \— the patient, toiling millions of Hindus under the whip \
> and slavery of everyone. Even the missionaries, who stand up to curse the millions of India, have been fattened \

### Dr. Guernsey's home

**Location:** Dr. Guernsey's Home, New York City, New York City, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** New York home of early supporter Dr. Guernsey


Dr. Guernsey was one of Swami Vivekananda's early New York supporters who hosted him at his home during the initial period of his American mission. New York City became his principal American base, and a network of sympathetic hosts — particularly from the medical and professional class — helped sustain his work there. Dr. Guernsey's home was one of the early gathering places for his New York circle of devotees.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references):

> \
> New York \
> 28 June 1894 \
> \
> Dear Mother, \
> \
> Arrived safely two hours ago. Landsbergl was waiting at the station. Came to Dr. Guernsey's house. Nobody was \
> there except a servant. I took a bath and strolled with Landsberg to some restaurant where I had a good meal. \
> \
> Then, I have just now returned to Landsberg's rooms in the Theosophical Society and am writing you this letter. \
> \
> I haven't been to see my other friends yet. After a good and long rest through the night I hope to see most of them \
> tomorrow. My Love to you all. By the by, somebody stepped on my umbrella on board the train and broke its nose \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> 19 West 38th Street, \
> New York , August, 1895. \
> \
> \
> . . . I am now in New York City. The city is hot in summer, exactly like Calcutta. You perspire profusely, and there \
> is not a breath of air. I made a tour in the north for a couple of months. Please answer this letter by return of post to \
> England, for which I shall start before this will have reached you. \
> \
> \
> Yours affectionately, \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*

### 54 West 33rd St (First HQ)

**Location:** 54 West 33rd Street, New York City, New York City, USA
**Period:** 1894-1895
**Significance:** First headquarters of Vedanta work in New York


54 West 33rd Street in Manhattan was the first headquarters for Swami Vivekananda's organised Vedanta work in New York City. It was here that he established the first classes and meetings that would eventually evolve into the Vedanta Society of New York. From this address he conducted regular lectures and classes on Raja Yoga, Jnana Yoga and Bhakti Yoga for his growing circle of New York students, marking the formal beginning of the institutionalised Vedanta movement in America.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> England, for which I shall start before this will have reached you. \
> \
> \
> Yours affectionately, \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*

### Waldorf Hotel

**Location:** Waldorf Hotel, Fifth Avenue, New York City, New York City, USA
**Period:** 1894-1895
**Significance:** Prestigious Manhattan hotel where Vivekananda stayed and met supporters


The original Waldorf Hotel on Fifth Avenue (later part of the Waldorf-Astoria) was among the prestigious Manhattan hotels where Swami Vivekananda stayed and met supporters during his New York period. The Waldorf was one of the most fashionable addresses in America, and Vivekananda's presence there reflected the high social circles in which he moved in New York. Wealthy patrons and society figures who supported his work would often arrange meetings at such establishments.


**From the Complete Works** (6 references):

> This year I could hardly keep my head up, and I did not go about lecturing. The three great commentaries on the \
> Vedanta philosophy belonging to the three great sects of dualists, qualified dualists, and monists are being sent to \
> me from India. Hope they will arrive safe. Then I will have an intellectual feast indeed. I intend to write a book this \
> summer on the Vedanta philosophy. This world will always be a mixture of good and evil, of happiness and \
> misery; this wheel will ever go up and come down; dissolution and resolution is the inevitable law. Blessed are \
> those who struggle to go beyond. Well, I am glad all the babies are doing well but sorry there was no "catch" even \
> this…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

**Location:** Mary Phillips's Home, New York City, New York City, USA
**Period:** 1894-1895
**Significance:** New York home of devoted supporter Mary Phillips


Mary Phillips was one of the devoted New York supporters of Swami Vivekananda who opened her home to him and his circle. Her home in Manhattan was a gathering place for his New York devotees, hosting the private talks and informal discussions that complemented his public lectures. Women like Mary Phillips formed the inner circle of practical supporters who managed the logistics of his American work and provided the domestic base from which he operated.

### Leon Landsberg's Home

**Location:** Leon Landsberg's Home, New York City, New York City, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** New York home of early disciple Leon Landsberg


Leon Landsberg (Swami Kripananda) was one of Swami Vivekananda's first American monastic disciples. A Jewish American journalist and intellectual, Landsberg was drawn to Vivekananda's teachings on Vedanta and became one of his earliest initiated disciples in America. His home in New York City was one of the gathering places for Vivekananda's early New York circle, and he played an important role in the early organisation of Vedanta work in the city.

### Emma Thursby's house

**Location:** Emma Thursby's House, New York City, New York City, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Home of celebrated soprano Emma Thursby who supported Vivekananda


Emma Cecilia Thursby was one of America's most celebrated soprano singers of the nineteenth century, and she became a devoted supporter of Swami Vivekananda. Her New York home was an important social venue where he met members of New York's cultural and artistic elite. Thursby's support lent Vivekananda considerable social prestige in New York, and her circle included many of the city's most distinguished artistic and intellectual figures.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references):

> \
> THE RELIGION OF INDIA \
> \
> \{New Discoveries, Vol. 2, pp. 145-49, 155-56.) \
> \
> These notes of daily morning classes delivered at Greenacre, Maine, in the summer of 1894 and recorded by Miss \
> Emma Thursby were discovered among Miss Emma Thursby's papers at the New-York Historical Society. They \
> have been lightly edited in order to conform to the style of the Complete Works. \
> \
> Notes taken miscellaneously from discourses given by Swami Vivekananda under the "Pine" at Greenacre in July \
> and August 1894. \
> \
> The name of Swami's master was Ramakrishna Paramahamsa. The signification of Vivekananda is conscious bliss. \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> 19 West 38th Street, \
> New York , August, 1895. \
> \
> \
> . . . I am now in New York City. The city is hot in summer, exactly like Calcutta. You perspire profusely, and there \
> is not a breath of air. I made a tour in the north for a couple of months. Please answer this letter by return of post to \
> England, for which I shall start before this will have reached you. \
> \
> \
> Yours affectionately, \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*

### Corbin mansion

**Location:** Corbin Mansion, New York City, New York City, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** New York mansion of supporter associated with Vivekananda


The Corbin mansion in New York City was one of the elegant residences associated with Swami Vivekananda's New York social circle. New York's wealthy elite — including figures from the finance and business world — were among the supporters who attended his lectures and hosted him at their homes. The parlours of such mansions provided settings for the more intimate and exclusive gatherings that complemented his large public lectures.

### Home of Lorenzo Barbers

**Location:** Home of Lorenzo Barbers, New York City, New York City, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** New York home of supporter Lorenzo Barbers


The home of Lorenzo Barbers in New York City was one of the private residences where Swami Vivekananda was received during his New York period. Like the private homes of supporters across America, Barbers' residence provided a domestic space for the intimate discussions on Vedanta that formed a crucial complement to his public lectures. These personal connections with individual American families helped root his teachings in the daily lives of his earliest American disciples.

### Hall of Universal Brotherhood

**Location:** Hall of Universal Brotherhood, New York City, New York City, USA
**Period:** 1894-1895
**Significance:** New York hall associated with Vivekananda's lectures


The Hall of Universal Brotherhood in New York City was a lecture venue associated with Swami Vivekananda's New York work. The name of the hall resonated with his core teaching of the unity of all existence and the brotherhood of all human beings, which was a central theme of his American mission. He used such venues for public lectures on Vedanta, Raja Yoga and the universal principles of spiritual life that transcend all sectarian boundaries.

**Significance:** New York club where Vivekananda lectured


The Exodus Club in New York City was one of the private clubs and societies where Swami Vivekananda was invited to speak during his New York period. Such clubs provided an intimate setting for his talks on Vedanta philosophy and the universal religion, reaching an educated audience of New York professionals and intellectuals. The variety of venues he addressed — from fashionable clubs to working-class lecture halls — reflected the breadth of his American appeal.

### Ramakrishna-Vivekananda Center of New York

**Location:** Ramakrishna-Vivekananda Center of New York, New York, USA
**Period:** 1894-1895
**Significance:** Vedanta center founded in Vivekananda's legacy


The Ramakrishna-Vivekananda Center of New York traces its spiritual lineage directly to Swami Vivekananda's pioneering Vedanta work in New York. Vivekananda's efforts during 1894-1895 laid the foundation for this enduring centre of Vedantic study and practice in New York City.


**From the Complete Works** (300 references):

> 37. We have been low animals once. We think they are something different from us. I hear, Western people say, \
> "The world was created for us." If tigers could write books, they would say, man was created for them and that man \
> is a most sinful animal, because he does not allow him (the tiger) to catch him easily. The worm that crawls under \
> your feet today is a God to be. \
> \
> 38. "I should very much like our women to have your intellectuality, but not if it must be at the cost of purity", said \
> Swami Vivekananda in New York. "I admire you for all that you know, but I dislike the way that you cover what is \
> bad with roses and call it good. Intellectuality is not the highest good.…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> thing be unreal, it is not. What is unreal does not exist. Strange things are natural phenomena. I know them to be \
> matters of science. Then they are not occult to me. I do not believe in occult societies. They do no good, and can \
> never do good." \

### The Long Island Historical Society

**Location:** The Long Island Historical Society, Brooklyn, USA
**Period:** 1895
**Significance:** Cultural institution visited during Brooklyn stay


The Long Island Historical Society (now the Brooklyn Historical Society) in Brooklyn Heights is associated with Swami Vivekananda's engagement with New York cultural institutions during his first Western tour in 1895.

### Sarah Ellen Waldo's house

**Location:** Sarah Ellen Waldo's house, New York, USA
**Period:** 1894-1895
**Significance:** Home of a devoted follower and host


Sarah Ellen Waldo was an early American devotee and supporter of Swami Vivekananda in New York. Her home served as a place of Vedantic discussion and spiritual fellowship during Vivekananda's first Western tour, and she contributed to spreading his teachings among New York society.


**From the Complete Works** (300 references):

> 37. We have been low animals once. We think they are something different from us. I hear, Western people say, \
> "The world was created for us." If tigers could write books, they would say, man was created for them and that man \
> is a most sinful animal, because he does not allow him (the tiger) to catch him easily. The worm that crawls under \
> your feet today is a God to be. \
> \
> 38. "I should very much like our women to have your intellectuality, but not if it must be at the cost of purity", said \
> Swami Vivekananda in New York. "I admire you for all that you know, but I dislike the way that you cover what is \
> bad with roses and call it good. Intellectuality is not the highest good.…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> you know that they all rose to their feet. You may wonder what made them do this, you may wonder if I had some \
> strange power. Let me tell you that I did have a power and this is it \— never once in my life did I allow myself to \
> have even one sexual thought. I trained my mind, my thinking, and the powers that man usually uses along that line \
> I put into a higher channel, and it developed a force so strong that nothing could resist it." (ND 6: 155) \
> \
> \
> 38. In a conversation with Swami Turiyananda, which probably took place in New York: \
> \
> "The call has come from Above: 'Come away, just come away \— no need of troubling your head to teach others'. It \
> is now the will of the Grand Old…

*— lectures in San Francisco in 1900: \*

> as "socialprogress"! \
> \
> 76. Things are not bettered, but we are bettered, by making changes in them. \
> \
> 77. Let me help my fellow men; that is all I seek. \
> \
> 78. "No", said the Swami, very softly, in answer to a question in New York, "I do not believe in the occult. If a \
> thing be unreal, it is not. What is unreal does not exist. Strange things are natural phenomena. I know them to be \
> matters of science. Then they are not occult to me. I do not believe in occult societies. They do no good, and can \
> never do good." \
> \
> 79. There are four general types of men \— the rational, the emotional, the mystical, and the worker. For each of \
> these we must provide suitable forms of worship.…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> love of God. It is the one motive power that is in the universe. Under the impetus of that love, Christ gives his life \
> for humanity, Buddha even for an animal, the mother for the child, the husband for the wife. It is under the impetus \
> of the same love that men are ready to give up their lives for their country, and strange to say, under the impetus of \
> the same love, the thief steals, the murderer murders. Even in these cases, the spirit is the same, but the \
> manifestation is different. This is the one motive power in the universe. The thief has love for gold; the love is \
> there, but it is misdirected. So, in all crimes, as well as in all virtuous actions, behind stands that eternal…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

>
> This series of lectures constituted the real beginning of the
> Swami’s work in America. He had already anticipated the
> serious character of his future activity by breaking away from
> social invitations and establishing himself in quarters of his own
> in the city of New York. He was tired and disgusted with
> the fame he had acquired ; and he felt that the interest he had
> awakened was not what he wanted; to his mind it was too
> superficial. He desired carnest-minded followers whom he could

*— p. 348*

### Ridgely Manor

**Location:** Ridgely Manor, Stone Ridge, USA
**Period:** 1895
**Significance:** Country retreat visited during first Western tour


Ridgely Manor in Stone Ridge, New York, was the country estate where Swami Vivekananda rested and engaged in spiritual discussions with American devotees during the summer of 1895. These peaceful retreats in the Hudson Valley allowed him time for reflection between his busy lecture engagements.


**From the Complete Works** (20 references):

> to Dr. T. K. Cheyne: \
> \
> "He could not imagine himself the teacher of anyone. He was like a man playing with balls of many colours, and \
> leaving it to others to select which they would for themselves." ( LSN II: 1110) \
> \
> \
> 46. Sister Nivedita\'s reminiscences of a conversation with Swami Vivekananda at Ridgely Manor, recorded in \
> an 1899 letter written from Ridgely Manor to Miss Josephine MacLeod: \
> \
> I have never heard the Prophet talk so much of Shri Ramakrishna. He told us what I had heard before of [his \
> master's] infallible judgement of men. . . . \
> \
> "And so", Swami said, "you see my devotion is the dog's devotion. I have been wrong so often and he has always \

*— lectures in San Francisco in 1900: \*

> letter to Miss Josephine MacLeod: \
> \
> "Only when they go away will they know how much they have received." ( LSN 11: 686) \
> \
> \
> 43. Sister Nivedita\'s reminiscences in a November 5, 1904 letter to Alberta Sturges (Lady Sandwich) of Swami \
> Vivekananda's talk on renunciation while he was staying at Ridgely Manor: \
> \
> "In India we never say that you should renounce a higher thing for a lower. It is better to be absorbed in music or in \
> literature than in comfort or pleasure, and we never say otherwise." (LSN II: 690) \
> \
> \
> 44. In Sister Nivedita's November 19, 1909 letter to Miss Josephine MacLeod: \

*— lectures in San Francisco in 1900: \*

> \
> \

> "In one\'s greatest hour of need one stands alone." (ND 5: 122) \
> \
> \
> 19. From Sister Nivedita's October 27, 1899 diary entry at Ridgely Manor, in which Swami Vivekananda \
> expressed his concern for Olea Bull Vaughn : \
> \
> \
> "Nightmares always begin pleasantly \— only at the worst point [the] dream is broken \— so death breaks [the] \
> dream of life. Love death." (ND 5: 138) \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> To Miss Mary Hale \
> \
> \
> My Dear Mary, \
> \
> \
> Ridgely Manor, \
> September 1899. \
> \
> \
> Yes, I have arrived. I had a letter from Isabelle from Greenacre. I hope to see her soon and Harriet. Harriet \
> Woolley has been uniformly silent. Never mind, I will bide my time, and as soon as Mr. Woolley becomes a \
> millionaire, demand my money. You did not write any particulars about Mother Church and Father Pope, only the \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> ‘is the very essence of poetry?”
>
> “We reached New York all too soon, fecling that we never could be
> grateful cnough for those blessed, intimate ten days with the Guru. . . .”
>
> The very afternoon of his arrival in New York from Glasgow,
> after visiting the residence of Mr. and Mrs. Leggett, the Swami
> with his Gurubhai accompanied them to their beautiful country-
> home called Ridgely Manor, on the Hudson, in the Catskill
> mountains, about one hundred and fifty miles from New York.
> He waited there “for the leading that he confidently expected,
> to show him where his next effort was to lie’. A month later
> he was joined there by Sister Nivedita. The hosts with their
> family were devoted to the Swami,…

### Lyndhurst

**Location:** Lyndhurst, Irvington, USA
**Period:** 1895
**Significance:** Hudson Valley estate visited during first Western tour


Lyndhurst, the historic Gothic Revival estate in Irvington along the Hudson River, is connected with Swami Vivekananda's travels through the Hudson Valley area during his first Western tour in 1895, part of his engagements with prominent American families.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*

### Central Church of Christ

**Location:** Central Church of Christ, Des Moines, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Lecture venue in Des Moines


Swami Vivekananda delivered lectures at the Central Church of Christ in Des Moines, Iowa, during his first Western tour in 1894. His message of universal religion and Vedanta resonated with American audiences, including those gathered at this prominent religious venue.


**From the Complete Works** (4 references):

> These two are continually at work within him; what elevates him is virtue, that which degenerates is evil. \
> \
> Kananda will speak at the First Unitarian Church tomorrow morning. \
> \
> * * * \
> \
> (Des Moines News, November 28, 1893) \
> \
> Swami Vivekananda, the talented scholar from the far-off India, spoke at the Central church last night [November \
> 27]. He was a representative of his country and creed at the recent parliament of religions assembled in Chicago \
> during the world\'s fair. Rev. H. O. Breeden introduced the speaker to the audience. He arose and after bowing to \
> his audience, commenced his lecture, the subject of which was "Hindoo Religion". His lecture was not confined to \
> any…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> Minneapolis \
> 24 November 1893. \
> \
> \
> I am still in Minneapolis. I am to lecture this afternoon, and the day after tomorrow go to Des Moines. \
> \
> The day I came here they had their first snow, and it snowed all through the day and night, and I had great use for \
> the arctics. (A waterproof overshoe.) I went to see the frozen Minnehaha Falls. They are very beautiful. The \
> temperature today is 21\'b0 below zero, but I had been out sleighing and enjoyed it immensely. I am not the least \
> afraid of losing the tips of my ears or nose. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> kind to me, and took me over the whole place and the University. I had a fine audience and $100. Immediately \
> after the lecture I took the night train to Minneapolis. \
> \
> I tried to get the clergymen's ticket, but they could not give me any, not being the headquarters. The thing to be \
> done is to get a permit from every head office of every line in Chicago. Perhaps it is possible for Mr. Hale to get \
> the permits for me. If it is so, I hope he will take the trouble to send them over to me to Minneapolis if they can \
> reach me by the 25th, or to Des Moines if by the 29th. Else I would do it the next time in Chicago. I have taken the \
> money in a draft on the bank, which cost me 400.…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> the World's Fair, instead of in London, as it ought to have been. The Raja of Mysore and some other friends sent \
> me to America as the Hindu representative. I stayed there three years, with the exception of last summer and this \
> summer, when I came to lecture in London. The Americans are a great people, with a future before them. I admire \
> them very much, and found many kind friends among them. They are less prejudiced than the English, more ready \
> to weigh end examine anew idea, to value it in spite of its newness. They are most hospitable too; far less time is \
> lost in showing one's credentials, as it were. You travel in America, as I did, from city to city, always lecturing \
> among…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (1 references):

> broadcast the ideas with which his mind teemed, and to dis-
> illusionise the Western mind of its crroneous notions concerning
> India and its culture. It was also a way to become independent
> and get funds for the various philanthropic and religious works
> in India which he had in mind. Now we see the Swami travell-
> ing hither and thither in Aincrica, visiting numerous cities,
> telling of the glories of India and the greatness of Indian
> culture. He visited all the larger cities of the Eastern and Mid-
> Western States lecturing in Chicago, Iowa City, Des Moines,
> Memphis, Indianapolis, Minneapolis. Detroit, Hartford, Buffalo,
> Boston, Cambridge, Baltimore, Washington, Brooklyn, New York
> and other…

### Rev. Breeden's home

**Location:** Rev. Breeden's home, Des Moines, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Hosted Vivekananda during Des Moines visit


The home of Reverend Breeden in Des Moines, Iowa, was one of the residences where Swami Vivekananda was hosted during his visit to Des Moines in 1894, reflecting the openness of American clergy to engage with his teachings on universal religion.


**From the Complete Works** (4 references):

> These two are continually at work within him; what elevates him is virtue, that which degenerates is evil. \

> (Des Moines News, November 28, 1893) \
> \
> Swami Vivekananda, the talented scholar from the far-off India, spoke at the Central church last night [November \
> 27]. He was a representative of his country and creed at the recent parliament of religions assembled in Chicago \
> during the world\'s fair. Rev. H. O. Breeden introduced the speaker to the audience. He arose and after bowing to \
> his audience, commenced his lecture, the subject of which was "Hindoo Religion". His lecture was not confined to \
> any…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> Minneapolis \
> 24 November 1893. \
> \
> \
> I am still in Minneapolis. I am to lecture this afternoon, and the day after tomorrow go to Des Moines. \
> \
> The day I came here they had their first snow, and it snowed all through the day and night, and I had great use for \
> the arctics. (A waterproof overshoe.) I went to see the frozen Minnehaha Falls. They are very beautiful. The \
> temperature today is 21\'b0 below zero, but I had been out sleighing and enjoyed it immensely. I am not the least \
> afraid of losing the tips of my ears or nose. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> kind to me, and took me over the whole place and the University. I had a fine audience and $100. Immediately \
> after the lecture I took the night train to Minneapolis. \
> \
> I tried to get the clergymen's ticket, but they could not give me any, not being the headquarters. The thing to be \
> done is to get a permit from every head office of every line in Chicago. Perhaps it is possible for Mr. Hale to get \
> the permits for me. If it is so, I hope he will take the trouble to send them over to me to Minneapolis if they can \
> reach me by the 25th, or to Des Moines if by the 29th. Else I would do it the next time in Chicago. I have taken the \
> money in a draft on the bank, which cost me 400.…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> the World's Fair, instead of in London, as it ought to have been. The Raja of Mysore and some other friends sent \

### La Salette Academy

**Location:** La Salette Academy, Memphis, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Lecture venue in Memphis


La Salette Academy in Memphis, Tennessee, was one of the venues where Swami Vivekananda spoke during his visit to Memphis in 1894. His lectures on Vedanta and universal brotherhood drew audiences from the city's educated and religious circles.


**From the Complete Works** (9 references):

> \'ab \
> \
> THE HINDOO MONK \
> \
> \{Appeal-Avalanche, January 16, 1894) \
> \
> Swami Vive Kananda, the Hindoo monk, who is to lecture at the Auditorium [Memphis] tonight, is one of the most \
> eloquent men who has ever appeared on the religious or lecture platform in this country. His matchless oratory, \
> deep penetration into things occult, his cleverness in debate, and great earnestness captured the closest attention of \
> the world's thinking men at the World's Fair Parliament of Religion, and the admiration of thousands of people who \
> have since heard him during his lecture tour through many of the states of the Union. \
> \
> In conversation he is a most pleasant gentleman; his choice of words are…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \'ab \
> \
> MANNERS AND CUSTOMS IN INDIA \
> \
> \{Appeal-Avalanche, January 21, 1894) \
> \
> Swami Vive Kananda, the Hindoo monk, delivered a lecture at La Salette Academy [Memphis] yesterday \
> afternoon. Owing to the pouring rain, a very small audience was present. \
> \
> The subject discussed was "Manners and Customs in India." Vive Kananda is advancing theories of religious \
> thought which find ready lodgment in the minds of some of the most advanced thinkers of this as well as other \
> cities of America. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> PLEA FOR TOLERANCE \
> \
> (.Memphis Commercial , January 17, 1894) \

> \
> He was introduced in a brief but informing address by Judge R. J. Morgan, who gave a sketch of the development \
> of the great Aryan race, from which development have come the Europeans and the Hindus alike, so tracing a \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Auditorium

**Location:** Auditorium, Memphis, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Major public lecture venue in Memphis


The Auditorium in Memphis, Tennessee, was one of the major public venues where Swami Vivekananda delivered lectures during his visit to Memphis in 1894, attracting large audiences drawn by his eloquence and the novelty of his Vedantic teachings.

> \'ab \

> THE LOVE OF GOD-I \
> \
> (.Report of a lecture in the Chicago Herald, September 25, 1893 ) \
> \
> An audience that fdled the auditorium of the Third Unitarian Church at Laflin and Monroe streets heard Swami \
> Vivekananda preach yesterday morning. The subject of his sermon was the love of God, and his treatment of the \
> theme was eloquent and unique. He said that God was worshipped in all parts of the world, but by different names \
> and in different ways. It is natural for men, he said, to worship the grand and the beautiful, and that religion was a \
> portion of their nature. The need of God was felt by all, and His love prompted them to deeds of charity, mercy, \
> and justice. All men…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> editorial comments of the Oakland Enquirer) \

> main auditorium and ante-rooms were packed, the annexed auditorium of Wendte Hall was thrown open, and this \

> room where they could hear conveniently, were turned away. \
> \

**Location:** The Woman's Council, Memphis, USA

**Significance:** Women's organization that hosted Vivekananda


The Woman's Council of Memphis, Tennessee, hosted Swami Vivekananda during his 1894 visit to the city. American women's organizations were among his most enthusiastic supporters, and Vivekananda frequently addressed such groups with his empowering message of spiritual equality.

> the world's thinking men at the World's Fair Parliament of Religion, and the admiration of thousands of people who \
> have since heard him during his lecture tour through many of the states of the Union. \

> In conversation he is a most pleasant gentleman; his choice of words are…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> MANNERS AND CUSTOMS IN INDIA \
> \
> \{Appeal-Avalanche, January 21, 1894) \
> \
> Swami Vive Kananda, the Hindoo monk, delivered a lecture at La Salette Academy [Memphis] yesterday \
> afternoon. Owing to the pouring rain, a very small audience was present. \
> \
> The subject discussed was "Manners and Customs in India." Vive Kananda is advancing theories of religious \
> thought which find ready lodgment in the minds of some of the most advanced thinkers of this as well as other \
> cities of America. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Mrs.Bagley residence

**Location:** Mrs.Bagley residence, Detroit, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Hosted Vivekananda in Detroit


The residence of Mrs. Bagley in Detroit, Michigan, was among the homes where Swami Vivekananda was graciously hosted during his visit to Detroit in 1894. Such private residences provided him with rest and intimate spiritual conversations with American devotees during his first Western tour.


**From the Complete Works** (88 references):

> (\uc0\u9632 Saginaw Courier-Herald, March 22, 1894) \
> \
> Seated in the lobby of the Hotel Vincent yesterday evening was a strong and regular featured man of fine presence, \
> whose swarthy skin made more pronounced the pearly whiteness of his even teeth. Under a broad and high \
> forehead his eyes betoken intelligence. This gentleman was Swami Vive Kananda, the Hindoo preacher. Mr. \
> Kananda's conversation is in pure and grammatically constructed English sentences, to which his slightly foreign \
> accent lends piquancy. Readers of the Detroit papers are aware that Mr. Kananda has lectured in that city a number \
> of times and aroused the animosity of some on account of his strictures upon…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> Swami Vivi Kananda will appear at the Y. M. H. A. Hall to-night for the last time in this city. He is under contract \
> with the "Slayton Lyceum Bureau," of Chicago, to fill a three-years' engagement in this country. He will leave \
> tomorrow for Chicago, where he has an engagement for the night of the 25th. \
> \
> \
> \{Detroit Tribune, February 15, 1894) \
> \
> Last evening a good sized audience had the privilege of seeing and listening to the famous Hindu Monk of the \
> Brahmo Samaj, Swami Vive Kananda, as he lectured at the Unitarian Church under the auspices of the Unity Club. \
> He appeared in native costume and made with his handsome face and stalwart figure a distinguished appearance. \
> His…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> and expected to throw his former belief all away and accept the newer in its entirety. "It is an idle dream when all \
> of us will have the same religious views," said he. "No emotion can be produced except by clashing elements \
> acting upon the mind. It is the revulsion of change, the new light, the presentation of the new to the old, that elicits \
> sensation." \
> \
> [As the first lecture had antagonised some people, the Free Press reporter was very cautious. Fortunately, however, \
> the Detroit Tribune consistently upheld Swamiji, and thus in its report of February 16 we get some idea of his \
> lecture on "Hindu Philosophy," although the Tribune reporter seems to have taken somewhat sketchy…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> SWAMI VIVEKANANDA ON INDIA \
> \
> (Bay City Da/7v Tribune , March 21, 1894) \
> \
> Bay City had a distinguished visitor yesterday in the person of Swami Vive Kananda, the much talked of Hindoo \
> monk. He arrived at noon from Detroit where he has been the guest of Senator Palmer and proceeded immediately \
> to the Fraser house. There he was seen by a reporter for The Tribune. \
> \
> Kananda spoke entertainingly of his country and his impressions of this country. He came to America via the \
> Pacific and will return via the Atlantic. "This is a great land," he said, "but I wouldn\'t like to live here. Americans \
> think too much of money. They give it preference over everything else. Your people have…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> study. Vivekananda and his cause found a place in the hearts of all true
> Christians.”’
>
> The Swami had no patience with small-mindedness or
> fanaticism. He had great reverence for Christ and His teach-
> ings; but he pointed out the faults and defects of Christian
> civilisation in unmistakable terms, and occasionally was sternly
> critical. As an instance may be cited the following from one
> of his lectures at Detroit in the February of 1894:
>
> “One thing I would tell you, and I do not mean any unkind criticism.
> Yon train and educate and clothe and pay men to do what?—to come
> over to my country and curse and abuse all my forefathers, my religion
> and my everything. They walk near a temple and say,…

*— p. 328*

> and editors literally besieged him on all sides. He was made to
> auswer innumerable questions with regard to his habits of life,
> his religion, his philosophy, his views of Western civilisation,
> his scheme of future work, his diet, his antecedents, the manners
> and customs of his people, the political conditions of his land,
> and a host of other subjects. In this manner the newspapers
> made the American public acquainted with many details of his
> personal as well as his country’s history. When he came to
> Detroit, in the month of February 1894, he was sought after
> by newspaper reporters day in and day out. It would be well
> to quote here what the Detroit Free Press, one of the leading
>
> 330 LIFE OF…

> with the most seductive fragrance of the Orient. The dusky gentleman
> uses poctical imagery as an artist uses colours, and the hues are laid
> on just where they belong, the result being somewhat bizarre in effect,
> and yet having a peculiar fascination. Kalcidoscopic were the swiftly
> succceding logical conclusions, and the deft manipulator was rewarded
> for his efforts from time to time by enthusiastic applause.’”
>

> to Detroit, Mrs. Mary C. Funke, a well-known society woman

> “Februrary 14th, 1894, stands out in my memory as a day apart, a

### Richelieu Hotel

**Location:** Richelieu Hotel, Detroit, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Hotel where Vivekananda stayed in Detroit


The Richelieu Hotel in Detroit, Michigan, served as Swami Vivekananda's base during his visit to the city in 1894. From here he ventured out for his various lecture engagements, spreading the message of Vedanta across the Detroit metropolitan area during his first Western tour.


**From the Complete Works** (88 references):

> (\uc0\u9632 Saginaw Courier-Herald, March 22, 1894) \
> \
> Seated in the lobby of the Hotel Vincent yesterday evening was a strong and regular featured man of fine presence, \
> whose swarthy skin made more pronounced the pearly whiteness of his even teeth. Under a broad and high \
> forehead his eyes betoken intelligence. This gentleman was Swami Vive Kananda, the Hindoo preacher. Mr. \
> Kananda's conversation is in pure and grammatically constructed English sentences, to which his slightly foreign \
> accent lends piquancy. Readers of the Detroit papers are aware that Mr. Kananda has lectured in that city a number \
> of times and aroused the animosity of some on account of his strictures upon…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> with the "Slayton Lyceum Bureau," of Chicago, to fill a three-years' engagement in this country. He will leave \
> tomorrow for Chicago, where he has an engagement for the night of the 25th. \
> \
> \
> \{Detroit Tribune, February 15, 1894) \
> \
> Last evening a good sized audience had the privilege of seeing and listening to the famous Hindu Monk of the \
> Brahmo Samaj, Swami Vive Kananda, as he lectured at the Unitarian Church under the auspices of the Unity Club. \
> He appeared in native costume and made with his handsome face and stalwart figure a distinguished appearance. \

### Temple Beth El

**Location:** Temple Beth El, Detroit, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Jewish temple where Vivekananda lectured


Swami Vivekananda lectured at Temple Beth El in Detroit, Michigan, in 1894. His willingness to speak at a Jewish house of worship exemplified his deep belief in the universality of religion — that all paths lead to the same divine truth — and earned him admiration across Detroit's diverse religious communities.

### Detroit Opera House

**Location:** Detroit Opera House, Detroit, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Major public lecture venue in Detroit


The Detroit Opera House was among the grandest venues where Swami Vivekananda delivered public lectures during his 1894 visit to Detroit. ### Detroit Auditorium

**Location:** Detroit Auditorium, Detroit, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Large public lecture hall in Detroit


The Detroit Auditorium hosted Swami Vivekananda's public lectures during his visit to Detroit, Michigan, in 1894. ### Bay City Opera House

**Location:** Bay City Opera House, Bay City, USA
**Period:** 1894
**Significance:** Lecture venue in Bay City, Michigan


Swami Vivekananda lectured at the Bay City Opera House in Bay City, Michigan, during his 1894 tour of the American Midwest. Even in smaller cities like Bay City, his reputation ensured enthusiastic audiences drawn to his talks on the universality of religion and the wisdom of India's Vedantic tradition.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> SWAMI VIVEKANANDA ON INDIA \
> \
> (Bay City Da/7v Tribune , March 21, 1894) \
> \
> Bay City had a distinguished visitor yesterday in the person of Swami Vive Kananda, the much talked of Hindoo \
> monk. He arrived at noon from Detroit where he has been the guest of Senator Palmer and proceeded immediately \
> to the Fraser house. There he was seen by a reporter for The Tribune. \
> \
> Kananda spoke entertainingly of his country and his impressions of this country. He came to America via the \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*
