### Bhaita (Vaita Village), Darjeeling

**Location:** Vaita Village, Darjeeling, India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Village visit


The village of Bhaita (Vaita) near Darjeeling was visited by Vivekananda during his Himalayan travels. He observed the lives of the hill people and interacted with them during his recuperative stay in the region.


**From the Complete Works** (36 references):

> To Swami Ramakrishnananda \
> \
> \
> Dear Shashi, \
> \
> \
> Darjeeling, \
> 20th April, 1897. \
> \
> \
> All of you have doubtless reached Madras by this time. I should think Biligiri is certainly taking great care of you, \
> and that Sadananda serves you as your attendant. In Madras the worship should be done in a completely Sattvic \
> manner, without a trace of Rajas in it. I hope Alasinga has by now returned to Madras. Don't enter into wrangles \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> I began to take a lot of exercise on horseback, both morning and evening. Since that I am very much better indeed. \
> I was so much better the first week of my gymnastics that I have scarcely felt so well since I was a boy and used to \
> have kusti (wrestling) exercises. I really began to feel that it was a pleasure to have a body. Every movement made \
> me conscious of strength \— every movement of the muscles was pleasurable. That exhilarating feeling has \
> subsided somewhat, yet I feel very strong. In a trial of strength I could make both G. G. and Niranjan go down \
> before me in a minute. In Darjeeling I always felt that I was not the same man. Here I feel that I have no disease…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> The Hindus are making processions and all that, but they cannot give money. The only help I got in the world was \
> in England, from Miss Muller, and Mr. Sevier. I thought there that a thousand pounds was sufficient to start at least \
> the principal centre in Calcutta, but my calculation was from the experience of Calcutta ten or twelve years ago. \
> Since then the prices have gone up three or four times. \
> \
> The work has been started anyhow. A rickety old little house has been rented for six or seven shillings, where \
> about twenty-four young men are being trained. I had to go to Darjeeling for a month to recover my health, and I \
> am glad to tell you I am very much better, and would you…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \'ab Chronology \
> \
> \'ab Addressee \
> \
> THE EDUCATION THAT INDIA NEEDS \
> \
> (Written to Shrimati Sarala Ghosal, B.A., Editor, Bharati, from Darjeeling, 24th April, 1897. (Translated from Bengali .) \
> \
> In reply to your questions about the methods of work, the most important thing I have to say is that the work \
> should be started on a scale which would be commensurate with the results desired. I have heard much of your \
> liberal mind, patriotism, and steady perseverance from my friend Miss Muller; and the proof of your erudition is \
> evident. I look upon it as a great good fortune that you are desirous to know what little this insignificant life has \
> been able to attempt; I shall state it to…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Dharamagarh Estate, Mayavati

**Location:** Dharamagarh, Mayavati, India
**Period:** 1901
**Significance:** Retreat


Dharamagarh estate near the Advaita Ashrama in Mayavati was a property associated with the Sevier family who established the Ashrama. Vivekananda visited this area during his 1901 pilgrimage to the Himalayas.


**From the Complete Works** (15 references):

### Mrs. Sevier's Bungalow, Mayavati

**Location:** Advaita Ashrama Campus, Mayavati, India
**Period:** 1898–1901
**Significance:** Sevier family home at Mayavati


The bungalow of Mr. and Mrs. Sevier at the Advaita Ashrama, Mayavati. Charlotte Sevier and her husband Captain Sevier were devoted disciples who established the Ashrama under Vivekananda's guidance. Vivekananda stayed here during his Himalayan visits.

**Location:** Advaita Ashrama, Mayavati, Mayavati, India
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Samadhi/memorial of Captain Sevier


Captain James Henry Sevier, one of Vivekananda's most devoted Western disciples, passed away at the Advaita Ashrama in Mayavati in 1900. His samadhi (memorial tomb) is located within the Ashrama grounds.

### Sister Nivedita's House, Almora

**Location:** Almora town, Almora, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Nivedita's Almora residence


The house in Almora where Sister Nivedita (Margaret Noble) stayed during Vivekananda's 1898 Himalayan tour. This was part of her introduction to Indian life and spirituality under Vivekananda's direct guidance.


**From the Complete Works** (111 references):

> \
> 10. Answering the remark of a disciple who felt that it would be better for her to come back to this life again and \
> again and help the causes that were of interest to her instead of striving for personal salvation with a deep longing \
> to get out of life, the Swami retorted quickly: "That\'s because you cannot overcome the idea of progress. But things \
> do not grow better. They remain as they are; and we grow better by the changes we make in them." \
> \
> 11. It was in Almora that a certain elderly man, with a face full of amiable weakness, came and put him a question \
> about Karma. What were they to do, he asked, whose Karma it was to see the strong oppress the weak? The Swami \
> turned on…

> REPLY TO THE ADDRESS OF WELCOME AT MADURA \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (10 references):

> time the Swami intended to make the pilgrimage to the Hima-
> layas from which Swami Akhandananda had just returned, with
> tales of marvellous interest and descriptions of far-off Tibet
> and beauteous Kashmir ; with glowing accounts of the Tibetan
> lamaseries and of the grandeur of Kedarnath, Naren said to
> him, “You are my man! You have faith! Come, let us be off
> together!” In a letter to a fellow monk, dated the 6th July.
> 1890, the Swami wrote: “I intend shortly, as soon as I can get
> a portion of my fare, to go up to Almora and thence to some
> place in Garhwal on the Ganga where I can settle down for a
> long meditation. Gangadhar is accompanying me. Indeed, it
> was with this desire and intention…

*— p. 202*

> begged to be taken along with him he said, “The attachment
> of Gurubhais is also Maya! If you fall ill I must look after
> you, and in case of my illness you must attend me. Thus onc
> is hindered in one’s resolutions and attainment of the goal. |
> am determined to have no longer any form of Maya about me!”
> And so, one morning in the latter part of January, 1891, he
> left his devoted brethren and journeyed on to Delhi by himself.
>
> > Sister Nivedita: The Master as I saw him, pp. 239-41.
>
> XV
> IN HISTORIC RAJPUTANA
>
> Tue Swami, with his scanty belongings and royal bearing,

*— p. 215*

> He found that he had left India much too early, as it was then
> only the middle of July; and to have come all the way from
> India and to have to wait all that length of time—for nothing!
> It was too much. He also discovered that he should have
> come as a representative of some recognised organisation. He
> wondered why he had been so foolish as to have listened to those
> sentimental schoolboys of Madras, who were ignorant of the
> necessary steps to be taken in order to become a delegate. ‘““To
> their unbounded faith it never occurred,” writes Sister Nivedita,
> “that they (the disciples) were demanding what was, humanly
> speaking, impossible. They thought that Vivekananda had only
> to appear, and he…

*— p. 305*

> Ict him depart from Bhagalpur. So, one day when I was away on some
> important business, he grasped this opportunity of leaving, after taking
> farewell of those at home. When I came back I made a strenuous search
> for him, but could discover no clue of him anywhere. And yet, why
> should I have thought that my will would avail! Why should Swamiji be
> like a frog in the well, when his ficld of work was the whole wide world!
>
> “He had expressed to me his intention of going to the Badarikashrama.
> Therefore, after he had left Bhagalpur, I even went up to Almora in the
> Himalayas in search of him. There Lala Badri Shah told me that he had
> left Almora some time before ; and knowing that he must have…

*— p. 206*


---

### Lala Badri Shah's House, Almora

**Location:** Almora, Almora, India
**Period:** 1898–1901
**Significance:** Devoted patron's home


Lala Badri Shah was a devoted patron and supporter of Vivekananda in Almora. His house was a regular halt for Vivekananda during his Himalayan visits, providing hospitality and support for the Swami's work.


**From the Complete Works** (111 references):

> \
> 10. Answering the remark of a disciple who felt that it would be better for her to come back to this life again and \
> again and help the causes that were of interest to her instead of striving for personal salvation with a deep longing \
> to get out of life, the Swami retorted quickly: "That\'s because you cannot overcome the idea of progress. But things \
> do not grow better. They remain as they are; and we grow better by the changes we make in them." \
> \
> 11. It was in Almora that a certain elderly man, with a face full of amiable weakness, came and put him a question \
> about Karma. What were they to do, he asked, whose Karma it was to see the strong oppress the weak? The Swami \
> turned on…

*— Notes of Class Talks \*

> together!” In a letter to a fellow monk, dated the 6th July.
> 1890, the Swami wrote: “I intend shortly, as soon as I can get
> a portion of my fare, to go up to Almora and thence to some
> place in Garhwal on the Ganga where I can settle down for a
> long meditation. Gangadhar is accompanying me. Indeed, it
> was with this desire and intention…

*— p. 202*

> Ict him depart from Bhagalpur. So, one day when I was away on some
> important business, he grasped this opportunity of leaving, after taking
> farewell of those at home. When I came back I made a strenuous search
> for him, but could discover no clue of him anywhere. And yet, why
> should I have thought that my will would avail! Why should Swamiji be
> like a frog in the well, when his ficld of work was the whole wide world!
>
> “He had expressed to me his intention of going to the Badarikashrama.
> Therefore, after he had left Bhagalpur, I even went up to Almora in the
> Himalayas in search of him. There Lala Badri Shah told me that he had
> left Almora some time before ; and knowing that he must have…

*— p. 206*

> to the latter of the necessity of religious festivals and other
> demonstrations of religious zeal and emotion for the general
> masses who cannot comprehend abstract ideas of Truth.
>
> A few days after his reply to the address of welcome by
> the Calcutta public, the Swami again lectured before it on
> “The Vedanta in All Its Phases”. This address was another of
> those masterpieces of philosophical dissertation which mark his
> progress from Colombo to Almora. Taking his stand upon the
> unassailable ground that the Vedas and the Upanishads are the
> basis of all systems of philosophy or religion in India, he touched
> upon the Sankhya, Yoga and Ramanuja systems, showing them
> as classifications of the…

*— p. 494*

> periodically by the conductor asking for the fare. He was very much
> ashamed of such occurrences, however, and worked hard to overcome them."”
>
> Mcanwhile news was pouring into India of the unparalleled
> success of the Swami in America. The Indian journals and
> magazines were filled with the American reports of his great
> address at the Parliament ; these quotations, extracts and com-
> ments were literally devoured by the Hindus, from Madras to
> Almora. from Calcutta to Bombay.
>
> The monks of the Ramakrishna Order at Baranagore also
> read the accounts of the Swami’s success, but though they could
> not recognise their Naren in the Swami Vivekananda yet some-
> thing told them in their heart of hearts…

*— p. 334*


---

### Vivekananda's Cottage, Almora

**Location:** Almora hill station, Almora, India
**Period:** 1898–1901
**Significance:** Primary residence in Almora


The cottage in Almora that served as Swami Vivekananda's primary residence during his multiple visits to the hill station. He conducted spiritual discourses and received visitors here, and composed some of his writings during this period.


**From the Complete Works** (111 references):

> \
> 10. Answering the remark of a disciple who felt that it would be better for her to come back to this life again and \
> again and help the causes that were of interest to her instead of striving for personal salvation with a deep longing \
> to get out of life, the Swami retorted quickly: "That\'s because you cannot overcome the idea of progress. But things \
> do not grow better. They remain as they are; and we grow better by the changes we make in them." \
> \
> 11. It was in Almora that a certain elderly man, with a face full of amiable weakness, came and put him a question \
> about Karma. What were they to do, he asked, whose Karma it was to see the strong oppress the weak? The Swami \
> turned on…

*— Notes of Class Talks \*

### Thompson House, Almora

**Location:** Almora, Almora, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Residence of Western disciples


Thompson House in Almora was a residence associated with Western disciples visiting Vivekananda in the Himalayas. It was part of the circle of homes and cottages used by the Swami and his growing international community during the Himalayan sojourns.


**From the Complete Works** (111 references):

> \
> 10. Answering the remark of a disciple who felt that it would be better for her to come back to this life again and \
> again and help the causes that were of interest to her instead of striving for personal salvation with a deep longing \
> to get out of life, the Swami retorted quickly: "That\'s because you cannot overcome the idea of progress. But things \
> do not grow better. They remain as they are; and we grow better by the changes we make in them." \
> \
> 11. It was in Almora that a certain elderly man, with a face full of amiable weakness, came and put him a question \
> about Karma. What were they to do, he asked, whose Karma it was to see the strong oppress the weak? The Swami \
> turned on…

*— Notes of Class Talks \*

### Kasar Devi Temple, Almora

**Location:** Kasar Devi Hill, Almora, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Sacred meditation site


The ancient Kasar Devi temple on a ridge above Almora was visited by Vivekananda for meditation. The location is famous for its unique electromagnetic energy. Vivekananda meditated here and declared it a powerful spiritual spot.


**From the Complete Works** (111 references):

> \
> 10. Answering the remark of a disciple who felt that it would be better for her to come back to this life again and \
> again and help the causes that were of interest to her instead of striving for personal salvation with a deep longing \
> to get out of life, the Swami retorted quickly: "That\'s because you cannot overcome the idea of progress. But things \
> do not grow better. They remain as they are; and we grow better by the changes we make in them." \
> \
> 11. It was in Almora that a certain elderly man, with a face full of amiable weakness, came and put him a question \
> about Karma. What were they to do, he asked, whose Karma it was to see the strong oppress the weak? The Swami \
> turned on…

### Syahi Devi Temple, Almora

**Location:** Syahi Devi, Almora, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Temple visit


Syahi Devi temple near Almora was visited by Vivekananda during his Himalayan travels. The forested hilltop shrine of Goddess Syahi Devi was a traditional pilgrimage destination in the Kumaon region.


**From the Complete Works** (111 references):

> \
> 10. Answering the remark of a disciple who felt that it would be better for her to come back to this life again and \
> again and help the causes that were of interest to her instead of striving for personal salvation with a deep longing \
> to get out of life, the Swami retorted quickly: "That\'s because you cannot overcome the idea of progress. But things \
> do not grow better. They remain as they are; and we grow better by the changes we make in them." \
> \

> about Karma. What were they to do, he asked, whose Karma it was to see the strong oppress the weak? The Swami \
> turned on…

> success of the Swami in America. The Indian journals and

> address at the Parliament ; these quotations, extracts and com-
> ments were literally devoured by the Hindus, from Madras to

### Sri Raghunath Temple, Almora

**Location:** Almora bazaar, Almora, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Temple visit


The historic Sri Raghunath temple in Almora's bazaar area was visited by Vivekananda during his stay in the hill town. He revered Lord Rama and visited Vaishnava shrines throughout his wandering years.


**From the Complete Works** (111 references):

> \
> 10. Answering the remark of a disciple who felt that it would be better for her to come back to this life again and \
> again and help the causes that were of interest to her instead of striving for personal salvation with a deep longing \
> to get out of life, the Swami retorted quickly: "That\'s because you cannot overcome the idea of progress. But things \
> do not grow better. They remain as they are; and we grow better by the changes we make in them." \
> \
> 11. It was in Almora that a certain elderly man, with a face full of amiable weakness, came and put him a question \
> about Karma. What were they to do, he asked, whose Karma it was to see the strong oppress the weak? The Swami \
> turned on…

### Bhim Tal

**Location:** Bhimtal Lake, Bhimtal, India
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Himalayan retreat


The picturesque lake town of Bhimtal in the Kumaon Himalayas was visited by Vivekananda during his final Himalayan journey. The serene lake setting provided an ideal environment for meditation.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (1 references):

> KSHIRBHAVANI
>
> Tue journey from Alora down to the plains through the hills
> covered with almost tropical forests was delightful. On the way
> the Swami pointed out a certain hill-side inhabited, so legend
> holds, by a race of centaurs, and he told of his own experience
> of once having actually seen such a phantom there before hear-
> ing the folk-tale. On June 12, the party rested above the beauti-
> ful lake, Bhim Tal. In his talks in the afternoon with his
> companions, the Swami translated some of the most charming
> Vedic verses, and sougs of Soordds and other poet-devotees, in
> his intense and poetic way intoning every line in the original
> before giving its English form. ‘The Rudra-prayer was…

*— p. 589*

### Roshanara Bagh, Delhi

**Location:** Roshanara Road, Delhi, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Delhi camp during return tour


Roshanara Bagh in north Delhi was associated with Vivekananda's visits to Delhi during his post-return Indian tour. The Mughal-era garden was a landmark meeting point where he addressed gatherings of devotees and seekers.


**From the Complete Works** (16 references):

> To Swami Brahmananda \
> \
> \
> MyDear Rakhal, \
> \
> \
> Delhi, \
> 8th December, 1897. \
> \
> \
> We shall start for Khetri tomorrow. Gradually the luggage has greatly increased. After Khetri I intend to send \
> everybody to the Math. I could get done through them none of the work which I had hoped. That is to say, it is \
> quite certain that none of them can do anything if he always remains with me. Unless each goes about \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> To Swami Brahmananda \
> \
> \
> My Dear Rakhal, \
> \
> \
> Delhi, \
> \
> 30th November, 1897. \
> \
> \
> Part of the money that Miss Muller promised has reached Calcutta. The balance will come afterwards in a short \
> while. We have also some amount. Miss Muller will deposit the money in your name as well as mine with Messrs. \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Concorde. Round this Place de la Concorde are statues in the fonn of women representing the eight chief towns of \
> France. One of these statues represents the district of Strasburg. This district was wrested from the hands of the \
> French by the Germans after the battle of 1870. The pain of this loss the French have not yet been able to get over, \
> and that statue is still covered with flowers and garlands offered in memory of its dead spirit, as it were. As men \
> place garlands over the tombs of their dead relations, so garlands are placed on that statue, at one time or another. \
> \
> It seems to me that the Chandni Chauk of Delhi might have been at one time somewhat like this Place de la…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> statues represents the district of Strasburg. This district was wrested from the hands of the \
> French by the Germans after the battle of 1870. The pain of this loss the French have not \
> yet been able to get over, and that statue is still covered with flowers and garlands offered \
> in memory of its dead spirit, as it were. As men place garlands over the tombs of their \
> dead relations, so garlands are placed on that statue, at one time or another. \
> \
> It seems to me that the Chandni Chauk of Delhi might have been at one time somewhat \
> like this Place de la Concorde. Here and there columns of victory, triumphal arches and \
> sculptural art in the fonn of huge statues of man and women,…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (10 references):

> discovered their beloved Leader. who was glad to see them.
> They said, “We did not know that you were staying here. We
> have come to Delhi to see only the old Imperial Capital. Here
> we heard of one Swami Vividishananda, an English-speaking
> monk. We were curious: to see him, and it is by mere accident
> that we met you.” The Swami replied, “My brethren, I have
> said that I desire to be left alone. I have asked you not to
> follow me. This I repeat once more. I do not want to be
> followed. Herewith I leave Delhi. No one must follow me
> or try to know my whereabouts. I demand that you obey me.
> Iam going to cut myself off from all old associations. Whither-
> soever the spirit leads, there shall I wander.…

*— p. 216*

> and his sufferings, and his heart lost in admiration of the genius of his foe!
> Such, said the Swami, should be our love also, like that of Vasishtha for
> Vishwamitra, without the slightest tinge of personal memory.”
>
> The whole party came back to Baramulla on October 11,
> and left for Lahore the next day. The European disciples had
> decided to accompany the Swami thither, and wait there for
> some days, and then go sight-seeing in some of the principal
> cities of Northern India such as Delhi, Agra, etc., with Swami
> Saradananda. The river trip to Baramulla was noticeable only
> for the extreme silence of the Swami, who preferred to be
> almost entirely by himself, and walked at the riverside…

*— p. 612*

> Kashmir and in Dharamshala, in the purchase of a tract of land
> for an institution for the training of Brahmaclirins.
>
> While he was at Dehra-Dun he received constant invita-
> tions from Khetri. The Raja of that State was exceedingly eager
> to give his subjects an opportunity of becoming acquainted
> with the Swami’s ideas. Besides, personally, he wished to see
> the Swami, his Guru. So the Swami started from Dehra-Dun
> for Rajputana. On the way he visited Delhi, Alwar, and Jai-
> pur. At Delhi he was the guest of Nata Krishna, a man in
> humble position, whom he had met in his Parivrajaka days at
> Hathras. Wealthy residents of Delhi pressed him to become
> their guest, but he preferred remaining with his…

*— p. 543*

> Tue Swami, with his scanty belongings and royal bearing,
> under the assumed name of Vividishananda entered Delhi, the
> city of sovereign memories. The crisp air, the grandeur of the
> place, its memories, its history, its atmosphere, filled him with
> physical and spiritual elation. He put up at the residence of
> Syamaldas who received him with open arms.
>
> Here in Delhi he went everywhere and saw everything.
> The royal sepulchres and palaces, the deserted sites of capitals,
> the ruins of royal and imperial greatness impressed the young
> monk with the ephemeral nature of all human glory and the
> permanence of the spirit which knows neither coming nor going.
> At the same time, the historian in him found…

*— p. 216*

### Dharamshala (Dharmsala)

**Location:** Dharamshala, Dharamshala, India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Himalayan tour stop


Dharamshala in the Kangra valley of Himachal Pradesh was visited by Vivekananda during his northern India tour. The town, set against the backdrop of the Dhauladhar range, was a refreshing hill station where he met devotees and rested.


**From the Complete Works** (3 references):

> go to Lahore. They should have each two thick gerua-coloured jerseys, and two blankets for bedding. I shall buy \
> them woollen chaddars, and other woollen necessities in Lahore. If the translation of Raja-Yoga has been \
> completed, get it published bearing all the cost. . . . Where the language is obscure, make it very simple and clear, \
> and let Tulsi make a Hindi translation of it if he can. If these books are published, they will help the Math very \
> greatly. \
> \
> I hope your health is now quite all right. Since reaching Dharamsala I have been all right. I like the cold places; \
> there the body keeps well. I have a desire either to visit a few places in Kashmir and then choose an…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> well what the people of our country are when it comes to money-matters. . . . On my behalf, you personally talk \
> with the friends there and politely make them understand that it is all right if they can find ways and means to bear \
> the expenses; but if they cannot do so, all of you come back to the Math at Calcutta or go to Rarnnad and establish \
> the Math there. \
> \
> \
> I am now going to the hills at Dharamsala. Niranjan, Dinu, Krishnalal, Latu, and Achyut will stay at Amritsar. Why \
> did you not, all these days, send Sadananda to the Math? If he is still there, then send him to the Punjab on receipt \
> of a letter from Niranjan from Amritsar. I intend to start work in the Punjab after a…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> Yogen tells me in a letter to buy the house at Baghbazar for Rs. 20,000. Even if we buy that house, there are still a \
> lot of difficulties; for example, we shall have to break it down in part and make the drawing room into a big hall, \
> and similar alterations and repairs. Moreover the house is very old and ramshackle. However, consult Girish Babu \
> and Atul and do what you decide to be best. Today I am leaving by the two o'clock train with all my party for \
> Kashmir. The recent stay at Dharamsala Hills has improved my health much, and the tonsillitis, fever, etc. have \
> completely disappeared. \
> \
> From a letter of yours I got all the news. Niranjan, Latu, Krishnalal, Dinanath, Gupta,…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (3 references):

> could meet on an equal footing of love and unity, exchange the
> highest ideals of each, and practise the Advaita philosophy,
> were much in the Swami’s thought. He had written to a friend
> that this monastery must be about 7,000 feet above sea-level,
> as he did not want to kill his Western disciples, who would
> come to work in India for the furtherance of his cause, by
> forcing on them the Indian mode of living in the fiery heat of
> the plains. On his tours he had himself looked for a suitable
> site in the hills in and about Dharamsala, Murree, Srinagar,
> Dehra-Dun and the town of Almora, but none answered the
> purpose satisfactorily. At length, when he went to Kashmir,

*— p. 646*

> trip. Even on the way to Khetri, after they had rested from

> and Mrs. Sevier arrived at Amritsar where also he was received

### Ambala

**Location:** Ambala, Ambala, India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** North India tour stop


Ambala was visited by Vivekananda during his northern India tour after his return from America. An important railway junction and military cantonment, it was a transit stop as he traveled between Delhi and Punjab.


**From the Complete Works** (7 references):

> To Swami Ramakrishnananda \
> \
> \
> Dear Shashi, \
> \
> \
> Ambala, \
> 19th August, 1897. \
> \
> \
> I am very much pained to hear that the work in Madras is not prospering for want of funds. I am glad to learn that \
> the amount borrowed by Alasinga's brother-in-law (sister's husband) has been received back in Alrnora. Goodwin \
> has written to me to inform the Reception Committee to take some money for expenses from the amount that is left \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> audience in English, and another to the Indian residents in Hindi. This was my maiden speech in Hindi, but \
> everyone liked it for all that. Of course the Westerners, as is their wont, were in raptures over it, as coming from a \
> "nigger"! "Oh, how wonderful!" and that sort of thing. Next Saturday there will be another lecture for the \
> Europeans. A big Association has been set on foot here \— let us wait and see how far it works in future. The object \
> of the Association is to impart education and religion. \
> \
> Monday next, trip to Bareilly then to Saharanpur, next to Ambala, thence, most probably, to Mussoorie with \
> Captain Sevier, and as soon as it is a little cool, return to the plains…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> If Brahmachari Hariprasanna can come, it will be very helpful. Mr. Sevier has become very impatient about \
> acquiring a house somewhere; it will be good if something is done quickly about it! Hariprasanna is an engineer; so \
> he will be able to do something quickly about it. Also he understands better about the suitability of places. They \
> (the Seviers) like to have a place somewhere near about Dehra Dun or Mussoorie; that is to say, the place must not \
> be too cold and must be habitable throughout the year. So send Hariprasanna at once straight to Sj. Shyamapada \
> Mukherjee, Medical Hall, Ambala Cantonment. As soon as I go down to the Punjab, I shall send Mr. Sevier along \
> with him. I…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> have succeeded in creating a centre in every place they visit. \
> \
> Just now I received a letter from Gangadhar. It is good news that he is determined to start a centre in that district. \
> Write to him saying that his friend, the Magistrate, has sent an excellent reply to my letter. As soon as we come \
> down to the plains from Kashmir, I shall send back Latu, Niranjan, Dinu, and Khoka. For there is no suitable work \
> for them here any more; also within three to four weeks send Shuddhananda, Sushil, and one other to me. Send \
> them to the house of Mr. Shyamacharan Mukhopadhyaya, Medical Hall, Cantonment, Ambala. From there I shall \
> go to Lahore. They should have each two thick gerua-coloured…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (2 references):

> about five or six years more. It was a significant prophetic
> utterance, though not treated seriously at the time, inasmuch
> as he Icft his body five years later on July 4, 1902.

### Pampore (Pampur)

**Location:** Pampore, Kashmir Valley, Pampore, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Kashmir valley tour


Pampore, the saffron town of Kashmir, was visited by Vivekananda during his 1898 Kashmir journey. Famous for its vast saffron fields, the town lies in the heart of the Kashmir valley south of Srinagar.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (10 references):

> message he was to give. He must settle down ; he must give
> himself up to contemplation ; he must solve all the problems
> of the soul, and of the land he was born in. And therefore
> after two months’ stay in the monastery he started out in July,
> 1890, with the same old determination—never to return. This
> time the Swami intended to make the pilgrimage to the Hima-
> layas from which Swami Akhandananda had just returned, with
> tales of marvellous interest and descriptions of far-off Tibet
> and beauteous Kashmir ; with glowing accounts of the Tibetan
> lamaseries and of the grandeur of Kedarnath, Naren said to
> him, “You are my man! You have faith! Come, let us be off

> The Swami was always grateful for any kindness shown to

> of Mysore. Now perhaps it would be a Kashmiri shawl, then

> exquisite silk or muslin, He also wrote to his Indian disciples
> to send him Rudraksha beads and Kushasanas, which he gave…

> held, however, in which he gave out his now-celebrated ideas

### Anant Nag

**Location:** Anantnag, Anantnag, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Kashmir pilgrimage


Anantnag in south Kashmir, historically known as Islamabad, was visited by Vivekananda during his 1898 Kashmir pilgrimage. The town is known for the Shiva Linga at Martand Sun Temple nearby and the sacred spring of Anantnag.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*

### Baramula

**Location:** Baramulla (Varahamula), Baramulla, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Gateway to Kashmir


Baramulla (ancient Varahamula), the gateway to the Kashmir valley on the Jhelum river, was a stop during Vivekananda's Kashmir tour. The town has ancient Hindu temple ruins and is a significant entry point to the valley.


**From the Complete Works** (7 references):

> \
> THE VALE OF KASHMIR \
> \
> PERSONS: The Swami Vivekananda and a party of Europeans and disciples, amongst whom were Dhira Mata, the \

> \
> Place: The River Jhelum \— Baramulla to Srinagar. \
> \
> Time: June 20 to June 22. \

### Jammu

**Location:** Jammu city, Jammu, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Vaishno Devi gateway


Jammu, the 'City of Temples,' was visited by Vivekananda as part of his Kashmir journey. The winter capital of J&K was the starting point for pilgrimages to Vaishno Devi and provided access to the Kashmir valley via the Banihal pass.


**From the Complete Works** (4 references):

> BHAKTI \
> \
> (.Delivered at Sialkote, Punjab ) \
> \
> In response to invitations from the Punjab and Kashmir, the Swami Vivekananda travelled through those parts. He \
> stayed in Kashmir for over a month and his work there was very much appreciated by the Maharaja and his \
> brothers. He then spent a few days in visiting Murree, Rawalpindi, and Jammu, and at each of these places he \
> delivered lectures. Subsequently he visited Sialkote and lectured twice, once in English and once in Hindi. The \
> subject of the Swamiji's Hindi lecture was Bhakti, a summary of which, translated into English, is given below: \
> \
> The various religions that exist in the world, although they differ in the form of…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> . . . Captain Sevier says he is very anxious for a site. He wishes to have a spot near Mussoorie or in some other \
> central place, as soon as possible. ... The thing is that we do not want a place which is too cold, at the same time it \
> must not be too hot. Dehra Dun is unbearable in summer, but pleasant in winter; Mussoorie itself is, I dare say, not \
> the right place for many in winter. Above or below it, that is, in British or Garhwal territory, some land is sure to \
> be found. At the same time there must be a supply of water at the place throughout the year, for drinking purposes \
> and for everyday use. My plan is this: With only Achyutananda and Gupta I go from Murree to Rawalpindi,…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> MY DEAR Miss Noble, (This was the last letter received in England by Sister Nivedita.) \
> \
> . . . Too much sentiment hurts work. "Hard as steel and soft as a flower" is the motto. \
> \
> \
> Jammu, \
> 3rd November, 1897. \
> \
> \
> I shall soon write to Sturdy. He is right to tell you that in case of trouble I will stand by you. You will have the \
> whole of it if I find a piece of bread in India \— you may rest assured of that. I am going to write to Sturdy from \
> Lahore, for which I start tomorrow. I have been here for 15 days to get some land in Kashmir from the Maharaja. I \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> under existing circumstances. Gradually the way will open to you. We must certainly have the orphanage, no \
> hesitating in that. We must not leave the girls in the lurch either. But then we must have a lady superintendent for \
> an orphanage of girls. I believe Mother will be a very good hand for that. Or engage for this task some aged widow \
> of the village who has no issue. And there must be separate places for the boys and girls. Captain Sevier is ready to \

> "To wait arrival", on the letter. I am soon going to Rawalpindi, tomorrow or the day after; then I visit Lahore and…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Kheer Bhawani temple (Kshir Bhavani)

**Location:** Tulmul, Kashmir, Ganderbal, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Sacred Devi shrine


Kheer Bhawani, the sacred spring temple of Goddess Ragnya Devi in Kashmir, was one of the most important spiritual stops for Vivekananda during his 1898 Kashmir pilgrimage. He had a profound spiritual experience here and it deeply influenced him.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> \
> August 20. \
> \

> new Math, as we called it, and had his boat moved close by ours so that he could be with us for a few days before \
> leaving for Ganderbal. \

### Amarnath cave temple

**Location:** Amarnath Cave, Himalaya, Amarnath, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Holiest Shiva pilgrimage


Vivekananda undertook the arduous pilgrimage to the Amarnath Cave in 1898, one of the holiest shrines of Lord Shiva. At an altitude of 3,888m, he had a profound mystical experience before the ice Shiva Lingam. He described it as one of the most powerful spiritual experiences of his life.


**From the Complete Works** (10 references):

> \
> And then the trend of conversation changed again and became all fun and merriment, jokes and stories. And as we \
> laughed and listened, the boats came up and talk was over for the day. \
> \
> \
> The whole of that afternoon and night the Swami lay in his boat, ill. But next day, when we landed at the temple of \
> Bijbehara \— already thronged with Amarnath pilgrims \— he was able to join us for a little while. "Quickly up and \
> quickly down", as he said of himself, was always his characteristic. After that he was with us most of the day, and \
> in the afternoon we reached Islamabad. . . . \
> \
> \
> In the dusk that evening one came into the little group amongst the apple trees and found the Master…

### Dal Lake (Dalh Lake)

**Location:** Dal Lake, Srinagar, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Houseboat stay, Kashmir


Vivekananda stayed on a houseboat on the famous Dal Lake in Srinagar during his 1898 Kashmir visit. He gave teachings to his Western disciples Nivedita, Josephine MacLeod and Sara Bull from his Dal Lake houseboat, creating an unforgettable atmosphere of spiritual instruction against the backdrop of the Himalayas.


**From the Complete Works** (32 references):

> To Swami Brahmananda \
> \
> \
> My Dear Rakhal, \
> \
> \
> Srinagar, \
> 17th July, 1898. \
> \
> \
> I got all the news from your letter. . . . My opinion regarding what you have written about Sarada is only that it is \
> difficult to make a magazine in Bengali paying; but if all of you together canvass subscribers from door to door, it \
> may be possible. In this matter do as you all decide. Poor Sarada has already been disappointed once. What harm is \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> To Swami Brahmananda \
> \
> \
> My Dear Rakhal, \
> \
> \
> Srinagar, \
> 1st August, 1898. \
> \
> \
> You are always under a delusion, and it does not leave you because of the strong influence, good or bad, of other \
> brains. It is this: whenever I write to you about accounts, you feel that I have no confidence in you. . . . My great \
> anxiety is this: the work has somehow been started, but it should go on and progress even when we are not here; \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> (Translated from Bengali ) \
> To Swami Brahmananda \
> \
> \
> C/o Rishibar Mukhopadhyaya, \
> Chief Justice, \
> Srinagar, Kashmir, \
> 13th September, 1897. \
> \
> My Dear Rakhal, \
> \
> Now Kashmir. The excellent accounts you heard of this place are all true. There is no place so beautiful as this; and \
> the people also are fair and good-looking, though their eyes are not beautiful. But I have also never seen elsewhere \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> To Swami Shuddhananda \
> \
> \
> C/o Rishibar Mukhopadhyaya, \
> Chief Justice, \
> Srinagar, Kashmir, \
> 15th September, 1897. \
> \
> My Dear Shuddhananda, \
> \
> We are in Kashmir at last. I need not tell you of all the beauties of the place. It is the one land fit for Yogis, to my \
> mind. But the land is now inhabited by a race who though possessing great physical beauty are extremely dirty. I \

### Ganderbal

**Location:** Ganderbal, Ganderbal, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Kashmir tour stop


Ganderbal on the Sindh river in central Kashmir was visited by Vivekananda during his 1898 tour of the valley. The town is associated with the sacred Kheer Bhawani temple nearby and the Sindh river route to Sonamarg.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> \
> August 20. \

> leaving for Ganderbal. \

### Achabal

**Location:** Achabal Garden, Anantnag, Anantnag, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Mughal garden and spring


Achabal, with its magnificent Mughal garden and the spring of the Achabal Nag, was visited by Vivekananda during his south Kashmir tour. The natural spring flowing through terraced gardens was a site of natural beauty that the Swami appreciated.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*

### Guwahati (Gauhati)

**Location:** Kamakhya, Guwahati, Guwahati, India
**Period:** 1901
**Significance:** Visit to Kamakhya Devi


Vivekananda visited Guwahati during his 1901 East Bengal tour. He visited the famous Kamakhya temple, one of the most sacred Shakti Peethas in India, located on the Nilachal Hill overlooking the Brahmaputra river.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> \

### Shillong

**Location:** Shillong, Shillong, India
**Period:** 1901
**Significance:** Hill capital of Assam


Vivekananda visited Shillong, then the capital of Assam and a picturesque hill station, in 1901. He rested in this pleasant town in the Khasi hills and gave discourses to the local community during his last major tour of eastern India.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references):

> asthma completely, I am sure. I get reduced, of course, during a fit, but then it takes me no time to lay on a few \
> layers of fat. I have a lot of cows, goats, a few sheep, dogs, geese, ducks, one tame gazelle, and very soon I am \
> going to have some milk buffaloes. These are not your American bison, but huge things \— hairless, half-aquatic in \
> habits, and [that] give an enormous quantity of very rich milk. \
> \
> Within the last few months, I got two fits [of asthma] by going to two of the dampest hill stations in Bengal \— \
> Shillong and Darjeeling. I am not going to try the Bengalee mountains any more. \
> \
> Mrs. Bull and Nivedita are in Norway. I don't know when they [will] come over to…

*— Letters are sometimes, dear Christina, like mercy \'97 good to the one that sends and the other that receives. \*

> \
> I am so glad you are enjoying Japan \— especially Japanese art. You are perfectly correct in saying that we will \
> have to learn many things from Japan. The help that Japan will give us will be with great sympathy and respect, \
> whereas that from the West unsympathetic and destructive. Certainly it is very desirable to establish a connection \
> between India and Japan. \
> \
> As for me, I was thrown hors de combat in Assam. The climate of the Math is just reviving me a bit. At Shillong \
> \— the hill sanatorium of Assam \— I had fever, asthma, increase of albumen, and my body swelled to almost twice \
> ills normal size. These symptoms subsided, however, as soon as I reached the Math. It is…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (4 references):

> From Dacca he next proceeded to the famous places of
> pilgrimage, Chandranath and Kamakhya, sojourning for some
> days at Goalpara and at the beautiful station of Gauhati in
> Assam. At Gauhati he delivered three lectures.
>

> went from bad to worse. He decided to go to the delightful
> hill-station of Shillong, where the air being much drier, it was
> thought his health might improve. Shillong was then the seat
> of the Assam Government, and the late Sir H. E. A. Cotton,
> a champion of the cause of India, was the Chief Commissioner.
> He had heard much of Swami Vivekananda and was anxious to
> meet him. At his request, the Swami delivered a lecture…

> The Swami’s health was failing rapidly. Besides diabetes
> from which he had been suffering, he had had at Dacca another

> given them enough for fifteen hundred years!” He felt that
> he could die in peace now that he had given his message to the
> world, and that if the Western nations accepted his spiritual
> ideals and India adopted his plans for her regeneration, there
> was work ahead of both sufficient for fifteen hundred years.

### Murree

**Location:** Murree hill station, Murree, Pre-Independence India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Hill station near Rawalpindi


Murree, the famous hill station near Rawalpindi and Islamabad, was visited by Vivekananda during his 1897 North India tour. The cool mountain resort was a popular retreat for both British officials and Indian travelers at the time.


**From the Complete Works** (12 references):

> \
> "Shankaracharya", he was saying, "had caught the rhythm of the Vedas, the national cadence. Indeed I always \
> imagine", he went on suddenly with dreamy voice and far-away look, "I always imagine that he had some vision \
> such as mine when he was young, and recovered the ancient music that way. Anyway, his whole life's work is \
> nothing but that, the throbbing of the beauty of the Vedas and Upanishads". . . . \
> \
> From Rawalpindi to Murree we went by tonga, and there we spent some days before setting out for Kashmir. Here \
> the Swami came to the conclusion that any effort which he might make to induce the orthodox to accept a \
> European as a fellow-disciple, or in the direction of woman's…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> To Swami Brahmananda \
> \
> \
> My Dear Rakhal, \
> \
> \
> Murree, \
> 12th October, 1897. \
> \
> \
> I wrote at length in yesterday's letter. I think it desirable to give you special directions about certain matters. ... (1) \
> To all those who collect money and send it to the Math . . . the acknowledgment of the amounts will be issued from \
> the Math. (2) The acknowledgment must be in duplicate, one for the sender, and one for filing in the Math. (3) \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> To Swami Brahmananda \
> \
> \
> My Dear Rakhal, \
> \
> \
> Murree, \
> 11th October, 1897. \
> \
> \
> I feel I have been working as if under an irresistible impulse for the last ten days, beginning from Kashmir. It may \
> be either a physical or a mental disease. Now I have come to the conclusion that I am unfit for further work. ... I \
> now understand that I have been very harsh to all of you. But I knew, however, that you would bear with all my \

*— lectures, perhaps, in the Punjab, otherwise none. The people of our country have not yet offered me even as much \*

> BHAKTI \
> \
> (.Delivered at Sialkote, Punjab ) \
> \
> In response to invitations from the Punjab and Kashmir, the Swami Vivekananda travelled through those parts. He \
> stayed in Kashmir for over a month and his work there was very much appreciated by the Maharaja and his \
> brothers. He then spent a few days in visiting Murree, Rawalpindi, and Jammu, and at each of these places he \
> delivered lectures. Subsequently he visited Sialkote and lectured twice, once in English and once in Hindi. The \
> subject of the Swamiji's Hindi lecture was Bhakti, a summary of which, translated into English, is given below: \
> \
> The various religions that exist in the world, although they differ in the form of…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> The Raja received the Swami with great cordiality and
> honour making him take his seat on a chair, while he himself

### Sialkot

**Location:** Sialkot city, Sialkot, Pre-Independence India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Punjab tour


Sialkot, an ancient city in Punjab (now Pakistan), was visited by Vivekananda during his 1897 Punjab tour. The city near the foothills of the Himalayas has deep historical roots, and he addressed gatherings of devotees there.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references):

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> BHAKTI \
> \
> (.Delivered at Sialkote, Punjab ) \
> \
> In response to invitations from the Punjab and Kashmir, the Swami Vivekananda travelled through those parts. He \
> stayed in Kashmir for over a month and his work there was very much appreciated by the Maharaja and his \
> brothers. He then spent a few days in visiting Murree, Rawalpindi, and Jammu, and at each of these places he \
> delivered lectures. Subsequently he visited Sialkote and lectured twice, once in English and once in Hindi. The \
> subject of the Swamiji's Hindi lecture was Bhakti, a summary of which, translated into English, is given below: \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> I hope you and Hari are now in good health. The work in Lahore went off with great eclat. Now I go to Dehra \
> Dun. The Sind tour is postponed. I have yet no news whether Dinu, Latu, and Krishnalal have reached Jaipur. Babu \
> Nagendranath Gupta will collect subscriptions and donations from here and send them to the Math to meet \
> expenses. Send him regular receipts. Let me know if you have received anything from Murree, Rawalpindi, and \
> Sialkot. \
> \
> \
> Reply to me C/o Post Master, Dehra Dun. Other letters you may send me after hearing from me from Dehra Dun. \
> My health is good; only I have to get up at night once or twice. I am having sound sleep; sleep is not spoiled even \
> after…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (2 references):

> 528 LIFE OF SWAMI VIVEKANANDA

> Jammu, lecturing there in Hindi to a most appreciative audience. From

> one in Hindi. One theme was common to all these lectures, as to all which

### Captain Sevier's Bungalow, Mayavati

**Location:** Advaita Ashrama Campus, Mayavati, India
**Period:** 1898–1900
**Significance:** Home of Captain Sevier at Mayavati


The bungalow of Captain James Henry Sevier at the Advaita Ashrama, Mayavati. Captain Sevier, one of Vivekananda's most devoted Western disciples, worked tirelessly to establish the Ashrama and lived here until his death in October 1900. He laboured so hard clearing the tea-estate grounds that he died prematurely, and was cremated by the nearby Sarada River according to Hindu rites.


**From the Complete Works** (15 references):

> version was first published in the collection of poetry entitled In Search of God and Other Poems (Mayavati: \
> Advaita Ashrama, 1968). \

### Captain Sevier's Point, Mayavati

**Location:** Captain Sevier's Point, Mayavati, India
**Period:** 1898–1900
**Significance:** Himalayan viewpoint


A viewpoint within the Mayavati estate named in memory of Captain James Henry Sevier, who along with Mrs. Charlotte Sevier co-founded the Advaita Ashrama at Vivekananda's inspiration. The point offers panoramic views of the Himalayan range, a landscape Sevier loved deeply. Dharamagarh, the highest hill within the Mayavati boundaries, affords the finest views of the snow range from this area.

### Sister Nivedita cottage (Oakley House)

**Location:** Oakley House, Almora, Almora, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Western disciples' residence at Almora


Oakley House in Almora — now known as Nivedita House — was the residence where Vivekananda's Western disciples stayed during the May 1898 Himalayan visit. Sara Bull, Josephine MacLeod, Sister Nivedita and Mrs. Patterson lodged here while Vivekananda stayed nearby at Lala Badri Shah's house and visited every morning for breakfast. It was under a tree at Oakley House that Vivekananda blessed Sister Nivedita, helping her overcome distraction in meditation. Here Nivedita first truly learned the art of meditation under Swamiji's guidance.


**From the Complete Works** (111 references):

> \
> 10. Answering the remark of a disciple who felt that it would be better for her to come back to this life again and \
> again and help the causes that were of interest to her instead of striving for personal salvation with a deep longing \
> to get out of life, the Swami retorted quickly: "That\'s because you cannot overcome the idea of progress. But things \
> do not grow better. They remain as they are; and we grow better by the changes we make in them." \
> \
> 11. It was in Almora that a certain elderly man, with a face full of amiable weakness, came and put him a question \
> about Karma. What were they to do, he asked, whose Karma it was to see the strong oppress the weak? The Swami \
> turned on…

### Swami Vivekananda memorial rest hall

**Location:** Almora town, Almora, India
**Period:** 1897, 1898
**Significance:** Memorial to Vivekananda's stays in Almora


The Swami Vivekananda Memorial Rest Hall in Almora commemorates the Swami's repeated stays in the hill town during his lecture tours and Himalayan retreats. Vivekananda visited Almora in 1897 after his triumphant return from the West and again in 1898 with Sister Nivedita and other Western disciples. The hall serves as a gathering place for devotees and preserves the memory of these transformative Himalayan sojourns.

### Government Inter College

**Location:** Government Inter College, Almora, Almora, India
**Period:** 27 July 1897
**Significance:** First Hindi lecture by Vivekananda


On 27 July 1897, Vivekananda delivered a public lecture at the Government Inter College, Almora, on the subject 'Vedic Teaching in Theory and Practice' — marking the very first time Swamiji addressed an audience in Hindi. The lecture was a landmark event in his post-Western tour of India. A statue of Vivekananda and a commemorative photograph were later unveiled at the college in his honour. The college hall was subsequently proposed to be named 'Swami Vivekananda Sabhagaar'.


**From the Complete Works** (111 references):

> \
> 10. Answering the remark of a disciple who felt that it would be better for her to come back to this life again and \
> again and help the causes that were of interest to her instead of striving for personal salvation with a deep longing \
> to get out of life, the Swami retorted quickly: "That\'s because you cannot overcome the idea of progress. But things \
> do not grow better. They remain as they are; and we grow better by the changes we make in them." \
> \
> 11. It was in Almora that a certain elderly man, with a face full of amiable weakness, came and put him a question \
> about Karma. What were they to do, he asked, whose Karma it was to see the strong oppress the weak? The Swami \
> turned on…

### Stimpson Mansion

**Location:** Stimpson Mansion, Los Angeles, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Hosted Vivekananda in Los Angeles


The Stimpson Mansion in Los Angeles was among the grand residences where Swami Vivekananda was received during his 1900 visit to Southern California. His second Western tour brought him to California's growing cities, where he attracted devoted followers among the spiritually-minded residents of Los Angeles.


**From the Complete Works** (29 references):

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE OPEN SECRET \
> \
> (.Delivered at Los Angeles, Calif., 5th Januaiy 1900 ) \
> \
> Whichever way we turn in trying to understand things in their reality, if we analyse far enough, we find that at last \
> we come to a peculiar state of things, seemingly a contradiction: something which our reason cannot grasp and yet \
> is a fact. We take up something \— we know it is finite; but as soon as we begin to analyse it, it leads us beyond our \
> reason, and we never find an end to all its qualities, its possibilities, its powers, its relations. It has become infinite. \
> Take even a common flower, that is finite enough; but who is there that can say he knows all about the flower? \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> CHRIST, THE MESSENGER \
> \
> \{Delivered at Los Angeles, California, 1900 ) \
> \
> The wave rises on the ocean, and there is a hollow. Again another wave rises, perhaps bigger than the former, to \
> fall down again, similarly, again to rise \— driving onward. In the march of events, we notice the rise and fall, and \
> we generally look towards the rise, forgetting the fall. But both are necessary, and both are great. This is the nature \
> of the universe. Whether in the world of our thoughts, the world of our relations in society, or in our spiritual \
> affairs, the same movement of succession, of rises and falls, is going on. Hence great predominances in the march \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE POWERS OF THE MIND \
> \
> (.Delivered at Los Angeles, California, January 8, 1900) \
> \
> All over the world there has been the belief in the supernatural throughout the ages. All of us have heard of \
> extraordinary happenings, and many of us have had some personal experience of them. I would rather introduce the \
> subject by telling you certain facts which have come within my own experience. I once heard of a man who, if any \
> one went to him with questions in his mind, would answer them immediately; and I was also informed that he \
> foretold events. I was curious and went to see him with a few friends. We each had something in our minds to ask, \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> WORK AND ITS SECRET \
> \
> (.Delivered at Los Angeles, California, January 4, 1900 ) \
> \
> One of the greatest lessons I have learnt in my life is to pay as much attention to the means of work as to its end. \
> He was a great man from whom I learnt it, and his own life was a practical demonstration of this great principle I \
> have been always learning great lessons from that one principle, and it appears to me that all the secret of success is \
> there; to pay as much attention to the means as to the end. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> guest of the Reverend Dr. Benjamin Fay Mills of Oakland, at

> at the game, assuring him that it was not so easy as it looked.
> ‘Then the Swami took a gun and successively hit about a dozen
> shells! They were all astonished and thought that he must
> evidently be a practised hand. But he assured them to the
> contrary, saying that he had never handled a gun before, and
> that the secret of his success lay in the concentration of the mind.
>
> The Swami found his California work prospering beyond
> measure. In Los Angeles and Pasadena, Vedanta meetings were
> being held by his students regularly, and ‘the Swami received
> many letters begging him to return there, but this was at present
> impossible as his work in the northern part of the State absorbed

*— p. 680*

> his teaching and had not only gained understanding by reading

> and Indian things. Here, also, he visited several outlying

> in the first days of December and did not return to New York
> until June 7 of the following year.
>
> The Swami’s immediate destination was Los Angeles where
> he was the guest of Mrs. Blodgett who moved in distinguished
> intellectual circles. Miss MacLeod’s brother had died in that
> house ; but she still continued there to be near the Swami. He
> remained in Los Angeles till the middle of February.…

*— p. 676*

### Roxie Blodgett Cottage

**Location:** Roxie Blodgett Cottage, Los Angeles, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Home of a devoted follower in Los Angeles


The cottage of Roxie Blodgett in Los Angeles was a place where Swami Vivekananda was welcomed and hosted during his 1900 California visit. Such devoted American followers provided him with comfortable stays and helped organise his lecture engagements in Southern California during his second Western tour.

### Blanchard Hall

**Location:** Blanchard Hall, Los Angeles, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Lecture hall in Los Angeles


Blanchard Hall in Los Angeles was one of the venues where Swami Vivekananda delivered public lectures during his 1900 visit to Southern California. The hall provided a public platform for his talks on Vedanta, Raja Yoga, and the spiritual traditions of India to Los Angeles audiences.

### Unity Church

**Location:** Unity Church, Los Angeles, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Church venue in Los Angeles


Swami Vivekananda spoke at the Unity Church in Los Angeles during his 1900 California visit. The Unity movement's emphasis on the practical application of spiritual principles aligned naturally with Vivekananda's own teachings on practical Vedanta, making this an especially receptive audience for his message.


**From the Complete Works** (363 references):

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE POWERS OF THE MIND \
> \
> (.Delivered at Los Angeles, California, January 8, 1900) \
> \
> All over the world there has been the belief in the supernatural throughout the ages. All of us have heard of \
> extraordinary happenings, and many of us have had some personal experience of them. I would rather introduce the \
> subject by telling you certain facts which have come within my own experience. I once heard of a man who, if any \
> one went to him with questions in his mind, would answer them immediately; and I was also informed that he \
> foretold events. I was curious and went to see him with a few friends. We each had something in our minds to ask, \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> WORK AND ITS SECRET \
> \
> (.Delivered at Los Angeles, California, January 4, 1900 ) \
> \
> One of the greatest lessons I have learnt in my life is to pay as much attention to the means of work as to its end. \
> He was a great man from whom I learnt it, and his own life was a practical demonstration of this great principle I \
> have been always learning great lessons from that one principle, and it appears to me that all the secret of success is \
> there; to pay as much attention to the means as to the end. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> HINTS ON PRACTICAL SPIRITUALITY \
> \
> (Delivered at the Home of Truth, Los Angeles, California ) \
> \
> This morning I shall try to present to you some ideas about breathing and other exercises. We have been discussing \
> theories so long that now it will be well to have a little of the practical. A great many books have been written in \
> India upon this subject. Just as your people are practical in many things, so it seems our people are practical in this \
> line. Five persons in this country will join their heads together and say, "We will have a joint-stock company", and \
> in five hours it is done; in India they could not do it in fifty years; they are so unpractical in matters…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


---

### Payne's Hall

**Location:** Payne's Hall, Los Angeles, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Lecture hall in Los Angeles


Payne's Hall in Los Angeles was a venue where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his 1900 visit to Southern California. Along with other lecture halls and private residences, it formed part of the circuit through which he spread the teachings of Vedanta among Los Angeles audiences during his second Western tour.

> All over the world there has been the belief in the supernatural throughout the ages. All of us have heard of \
> extraordinary happenings, and many of us have had some personal experience of them. I would rather introduce the \
> subject by telling you certain facts which have come within my own experience. I once heard of a man who, if any \
> one went to him with questions in his mind, would answer them immediately; and I was also informed that he \
> foretold events. I was curious and went to see him with a few friends. We each had something in our minds to ask, \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \'ab \
> \
> WORK AND ITS SECRET \
> \
> (.Delivered at Los Angeles, California, January 4, 1900 ) \
> \
> One of the greatest lessons I have learnt in my life is to pay as much attention to the means of work as to its end. \
> He was a great man from whom I learnt it, and his own life was a practical demonstration of this great principle I \
> have been always learning great lessons from that one principle, and it appears to me that all the secret of success is \
> there; to pay as much attention to the means as to the end. \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Home of Truth

**Location:** Home of Truth, Los Angeles, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** New Thought spiritual centre in Los Angeles


The Home of Truth in Los Angeles was a New Thought spiritual centre associated with Swami Vivekananda's activities during his 1900 California visit. The Home of Truth movement, which emphasised spiritual healing and the power of mind, intersected with Vivekananda's Vedantic teachings, attracting seekers from across Los Angeles.


**From the Complete Works** (31 references):

> that is by this path of the Istha-Nishtha or "steadfast devotion to the chosen ideal". Every sect of every religion \
> presents only one ideal of its own to mankind, but the eternal Vedantic religion opens to mankind an infinite \
> number of doors for ingress into the inner shrine of divinity, and places before humanity an almost inexhaustible \
> array of ideals, there being in each of them a manifestation of the Eternal One. With the kindest solicitude, the \
> Vedanta points out to aspiring men and women the numerous roads, hewn out of the solid rock of the realities of \
> human life, by the glorious sons, or human manifestations, of God, in the past and in the present, and stands with…

*— letters 3 Tf (A.U.M.), pronounced in combination as Om, may well be the generalised symbol of all possible \*

> \'ab \
> \
> THE OPEN SECRET \
> \
> (.Delivered at Los Angeles, Calif., 5th Januaiy 1900 ) \
> \
> Whichever way we turn in trying to understand things in their reality, if we analyse far enough, we find that at last \
> we come to a peculiar state of things, seemingly a contradiction: something which our reason cannot grasp and yet \
> is a fact. We take up something \— we know it is finite; but as soon as we begin to analyse it, it leads us beyond our \
> reason, and we never find an end to all its qualities, its possibilities, its powers, its relations. It has become infinite. \
> Take even a common flower, that is finite enough; but who is there that can say he knows all about the flower? \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> CHRIST, THE MESSENGER \
> \
> \{Delivered at Los Angeles, California, 1900 ) \

> The wave rises on the ocean, and there is a hollow. Again another wave rises, perhaps bigger than the former, to \

> we generally look towards the rise, forgetting the fall. But both are necessary, and both are great. This is the nature \
> of the universe. Whether in the world of our thoughts, the world of our relations in society, or in our spiritual \
> affairs, the same movement of succession, of rises and falls, is going on. Hence great predominances in the march \

### Echo Mountain House

**Location:** Echo Mountain House, Altadena, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Mountain retreat near Los Angeles


Echo Mountain House, situated in the San Gabriel Mountains above Pasadena, was a destination Swami Vivekananda visited during his 1900 California stay. The mountain setting offered him a tranquil retreat from the busy lecture circuit, and the panoramic views of Southern California provided a fitting backdrop for contemplation.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> Murmuring rivers and brooks, rippling lakes \
> With restless Bhramaras* wheeling over \
> Gently waving lotuses unnumbered; \
> \
> Foaming flow cascades \— a streaming music \— \
> \
> To which echo mountain caves in return; \
> \
> Warblers, full of sweet-flowing melody, \
> \
> Hidden in leaves, pour hearts out \— love discourse; \
> \
> The rising orb of day, the painter divine, \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Mount Lowe

**Location:** Mount Lowe, Altadena, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Mountain excursion site near Los Angeles


Mount Lowe in the San Gabriel Mountains was among the natural landmarks visited by Swami Vivekananda during his stay in the Los Angeles area in 1900. The Mount Lowe Railway was one of the great tourist attractions of California at the time, and Vivekananda's excursion there offered him an experience of the grand California landscape.

The home of Mrs. Newton in South Pasadena, California, was one of the residences where Swami Vivekananda stayed during his 1900 visit to the Pasadena area. The leafy, pleasant suburb provided a restful setting between his lecture engagements in Los Angeles during his second Western tour.

The home of Mrs. E. F. Bowler in South Pasadena, California, was among the private residences where Swami Vivekananda was hosted during his 1900 visit to Southern California. These warm-hearted American hostesses played an important role in supporting his mission by providing comfortable accommodation and organising gatherings of spiritual seekers.

**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Hotel in the Pasadena area


The Green Hotel in the Pasadena area of Southern California was associated with Swami Vivekananda's stay during his 1900 California visit. Pasadena's elegant hotels and cultural venues provided a congenial setting for this period of his second Western tour.

The Shakespeare Club in South Pasadena, California, hosted Swami Vivekananda during his 1900 California visit. Such literary and cultural clubs were frequently the venues for his talks on Indian philosophy and Vedanta, as their educated, culturally-minded memberships proved receptive audiences for his eloquent discourses.

> SWAMI VlVEKANANDA: "Some persons desire to ask questions about Hindu Philosophy before the lecture and to \

### Universalist Church

**Location:** Universalist Church, South Pasadena, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Church venue in South Pasadena


The Universalist Church in South Pasadena, California, was a venue where Swami Vivekananda spoke during his 1900 California visit. Universalist congregations, with their belief in the salvation of all souls and the harmony of religions, were naturally sympathetic to Vivekananda's Vedantic message of universal spirituality.

### First Unitarian Church

**Location:** First Unitarian Church, Oakland, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Lecture venue in Oakland, California


Swami Vivekananda lectured at the First Unitarian Church in Oakland, California, during his 1900 West Coast visit. Oakland's Unitarian congregation, known for its progressive and intellectually open approach to religion, provided an ideal audience for his teachings on the universality of Vedanta and the common spiritual core of all religions.


**From the Complete Works** (16 references):

> \
> \
> "Swami Vivekananda's second trip to the West occurred in 1899-1900. During the first half of 1900 he worked in \
> and around San Francisco, California. I was a resident of that city, twenty-two years old at the time. ... I heard him \
> lecture perhaps a score of times from March to May of 1900, and recorded seventeen of his talks. ... \
> \
> "The lectures were given in San Francisco, Oakland, and Alameda, in churches, in the Alameda and San Francisco \
> Homes of Truth, and in rented halls. ... Altogether Swamiji gave, besides nearly daily interviews and informal \
> classes, at least thirty or forty major addresses in March, April, and May. ... \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> These two are continually at work within him; what elevates him is virtue, that which degenerates is evil. \

### George H.Perry house

**Location:** George H.Perry house, Alameda, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Hosted Vivekananda in Alameda, California


The home of George H. Perry in Alameda, California, was one of the Bay Area residences where Swami Vivekananda was hosted during his 1900 California visit. Alameda, situated on an island in San Francisco Bay, was home to several of his devoted California followers who welcomed him warmly during his second Western tour.


**From the Complete Works** (7 references):

> \
> \
> "Swami Vivekananda's second trip to the West occurred in 1899-1900. During the first half of 1900 he worked in \
> and around San Francisco, California. I was a resident of that city, twenty-two years old at the time. ... I heard him \
> lecture perhaps a score of times from March to May of 1900, and recorded seventeen of his talks. ... \
> \
> "The lectures were given in San Francisco, Oakland, and Alameda, in churches, in the Alameda and San Francisco \
> Homes of Truth, and in rented halls. ... Altogether Swamiji gave, besides nearly daily interviews and informal \
> classes, at least thirty or forty major addresses in March, April, and May. ... \
> \
> "I was long hesitant about transcribing and…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Swami Vivekananda (turning quietly to her): You say that, and you have seen me? \
> \
> A GREETING \
> \
> \{New Discoveries, Vol. 6, p. 136.) \
> \
> From Mr. Thomas Allan's reminiscences of Swami Vivekananda's visit to Alameda, California, 1900: \
> \
> Mr. Allan: Well, Swami, I see you are in Alameda! \
> \
> Swami Vivekananda: No, Mr. Allan, I am not in Alameda; Alameda is in me. \
> \
> "THIS WORLD IS A CIRCUS RING" \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE PRACTICE OF RELIGION \
> \
> (.Delivered at Alameda, California, on April 18, 1900*) \
> \
> We read many books, many scriptures. We get various ideas from our childhood, and change them every now and \
> then. We understand what is meant by theoretical religion. We think we understand what is meant by practical \
> religion. Now I am going to present to you my idea of practical religion. \
> \
> We hear all around us about practical religion, and analysing all that, we find that it can be brought down to one \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> To an American friend \
> \
> \
> Mother is becoming propitious once more. Things are looking up. They must. \
> \
> \
> Alameda, California, \
> 12th April, 1900. \
> \
> \
> Work always brings evil with it. I have paid for the accumulated evil with bad health. I am glad. My mind is all the \
> better for it. There is a mellowness and a calmness in life now, which was never there before. I am learning now \
> how to be detached as well as attached, and mentally becoming my own master. . . . \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Alameda Home of Truth

**Location:** Alameda Home of Truth, Alameda, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** New Thought spiritual centre in Alameda


The Alameda Home of Truth was a New Thought spiritual centre in Alameda, California, associated with Swami Vivekananda's Bay Area activities in 1900. The Home of Truth network across California provided organised venues and receptive audiences for his teachings on Vedanta and the practical application of spiritual principles.

### Tucker Hall

**Location:** Tucker Hall, Alameda, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Lecture venue in Alameda, California


Tucker Hall in Alameda, California, was a venue where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his 1900 Bay Area visit. As part of his extensive California lecture circuit, Tucker Hall allowed him to bring his message of Vedanta to the residents of this Bay Area community during his second and final Western tour.
