### Home of Truth

**Location:** Home of Truth, San Francisco, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** New Thought spiritual centre in San Francisco


The Home of Truth in San Francisco was one of the key New Thought centres associated with Swami Vivekananda's activities during his 1900 California visit. San Francisco's vibrant spiritual scene embraced his Vedantic teachings, and the Home of Truth network provided organised venues and dedicated audiences for his lectures and classes.


**From the Complete Works** (84 references):

> \
> MOHAMMED \
> \
> This article was recorded by Ida Anseli in shorthand. As, however, Swamiji's speed was too great for her in her early days, dots are put in the \
> articles to indicate the omissions, while the words within square brackets are added by way of linking up the disconnected parts. \
> \
> (.Delivered on March 25, 1900, in the San Francisco Bay Area) \
> \
> The ancient message of Krishna is one harmonising three \— Buddha's, Christ's and Mohammed\'s. Each of the three \
> started an idea and carried it to its extreme. Krishna antedates all the other prophets. [Yet, we might say,] Krishna \
> takes the old ideas and synthesises them, [although] his is the most ancient message. His message was…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> THE SOUL AND GOD \
> \
> This article was recorded by Ida Anseli in shorthand. As, however, Swamiji's speed was too great for her in her early days, dots are put in the \
> articles to indicate the omissions, while the words within square brackets are added by way of linking up the disconnected parts. \
> \
> (Delivered in San Francisco, March 23, 1900) \
> \
> Whether it was fear or mere inquisitiveness which first led man to think of powers superior to himself, we need not \
> discuss. ... These raised in the mind peculiar worship tendencies, and so on. There never have been [times in the \
> history of mankind] without [some ideal] of worship. Why? What makes us all struggle for something beyond what…

*— poems of love. In all Sanskrit books, the writers first of all salute their Gurus. So he saluted that girl as his first \*

> \
> THE GITA I \
> \
> This article was recorded by Ida Anseli in shorthand. As, however, Swamiji's speed was too great for her in her early days, dots are put in the \
> articles to indicate the omissions, while the words within square brackets are added by way of linking up the disconnected parts. \
> \
> (.Delivered in San Francisco, on May 26, 1900) \
> \
> To understand the Gita requires its historical background. The Gita is a commentary on the Upanishads. The \
> Upanishads are the Bible of India. They occupy the same place as the New Testament does. There are [more than] \
> a hundred books comprising the Upanishads, some very small and some big, each a separate treatise. The \
> Upanishads do not reveal…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> THE GITA II \
> \
> This article was recorded by Ida Anseli in shorthand. As, however, Swamiji's speed was too great for her in her early days, dots are put in the \
> articles to indicate the omissions, while the words within square brackets are added by way of linking up the disconnected parts. \
> \
> (.Delivered in San Francisco, on May 28, 1900) \
> \
> The Gita requires a little preliminary introduction. The scene is laid on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. There were \
> two branches of the same race fighting for the empire of India about five thousand years ago. The Pandavas had the \
> right, but the Kauravas had the might. The Pandavas were five brothers, and they were living in a forest. Krishna…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

*— p. 684*

> fect. It was twelve miles from the famous Lick Observatory on
> Mt. Hamilton. Being thus removed far from the conflicting
> influences of worldly life, the name Shanti Ashrama or “Peace
> Retreat” was appropriately given to it. On August 2, Swami
> Turiyananda went there for the first time with twelve students
> whom he trained régularly in meditation, living with them the
> austere monastic life as in India. These annual retirements of
> one month in the year were continued for some time by the
> Swami in charge of the San Francisco centre.
>
> Late in the spring ot 1900, in the company of friends the
> Swami retired to Camp Taylor, in the country, for a short
> vacation. The end of the lecture course found him…

*— p. 681*

> and Secretary of the newly-formed Vedanta Society in San Fran-
> cisco. The San Francisco Vedanta students were eager to have
> a Swami and a permanent Vedanta centre. So, like the Los
> Angeles disciples, they beggcd the Swami to send them another
> teacher when he should depart ; and this he promised to do. In
> fact, he wrote to Swami Turiyananda to come at once, but this
> was not practicable as he was then conducting the classes in New
> York in the place of Swami Abhedananda, who was away on a
> lecture tour. he Swami stayed in San Francisco and its vicinity
> until the end of May.

> Teachers of the World”. In fact, between Los Angeles and

> most salutary for him, and he worked at his best.

### California St. Home of Truth

**Location:** California St. Home of Truth, San Francisco, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Home of Truth centre on California Street, San Francisco


The California Street Home of Truth in San Francisco was a New Thought centre on one of the city's prominent thoroughfares, associated with Swami Vivekananda's 1900 San Francisco activities. Situated in an affluent neighbourhood, it attracted an educated, spiritually curious audience for his Vedanta classes and lectures.

### 1719 Turk St. House

**Location:** 1719 Turk St. House, San Francisco, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Residence associated with Vivekananda's San Francisco stay


The house at 1719 Turk Street in San Francisco was one of the residences associated with Swami Vivekananda's 1900 stay in the city. ### Golden Gate Hall

**Location:** Golden Gate Hall, San Francisco, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Public lecture hall in San Francisco


Golden Gate Hall in San Francisco was one of the public venues where Swami Vivekananda delivered lectures during his 1900 California visit. The hall's central location in the city ensured a broad cross-section of San Francisco's population could attend his inspiring talks on Vedanta, Raja Yoga, and the spiritual heritage of India.

### Washington Hall

**Location:** Washington Hall, San Francisco, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Lecture venue in San Francisco


Washington Hall in San Francisco was a venue where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his 1900 California visit. Along with other San Francisco halls and lecture rooms, it served as part of the extensive circuit through which he delivered his teachings on Vedanta to Bay Area audiences during his second and final Western tour.

### Union Square Hall

**Location:** Union Square Hall, San Francisco, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Lecture hall near Union Square, San Francisco


Union Square Hall in San Francisco, located near the city's famous public square, was one of the venues where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his 1900 visit. The central, accessible location of Union Square made it an ideal gathering place for the many San Franciscans drawn to his teachings on Vedanta and universal religion.

### Mount Shasta, CA

**Location:** Mount Shasta, Mount Shasta, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Sacred mountain visited by Vivekananda in California


Mount Shasta, the majestic volcanic peak in northern California, was among the natural landmarks visited by Swami Vivekananda during his 1900 California sojourn. Long considered a sacred site by indigenous peoples and spiritual seekers alike, the mountain's awe-inspiring presence deeply resonated with Vivekananda's contemplative spirit.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*

### Dr.Milburn Logan's home

**Location:** Dr.Milburn Logan's home, San Francisco, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Hosted Vivekananda in San Francisco


The home of Dr. Milburn Logan in San Francisco was one of the residences where Swami Vivekananda was hosted during his 1900 Bay Area visit. Dr. Logan was among the California devotees who provided warm hospitality and support for Vivekananda's Vedanta mission on the West Coast during his second Western tour.

### Camp Irving

**Location:** Camp Irving, Camp Taylor, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Summer retreat camp in Marin County, California


Camp Irving at Camp Taylor in Marin County, California, was a retreat destination associated with Swami Vivekananda's 1900 California visit. The redwood forests and peaceful natural setting of Marin County provided an ideal environment for meditation and the informal spiritual gatherings that Vivekananda favoured during his time in the San Francisco Bay Area.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*

> Swami retired to Camp Taylor, in the country, for a short

> advisable that he should stop at the residence of…

> 28 and 29.

### Rue Anatole

**Location:** Rue Anatole, Perros-Guirec, France
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Address in Brittany where Vivekananda rested


Rue Anatole in Perros-Guirec, a coastal town in Brittany, France, was the address where Swami Vivekananda rested during his stay in this picturesque corner of northern France in 1900. The rugged Breton coastline and peaceful atmosphere of Perros-Guirec offered him much-needed respite during his second Western tour.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*

### Mont Blanc

**Location:** Mont Blanc, Chamonix, France
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Europe's highest peak, visited by Vivekananda


Swami Vivekananda visited the Mont Blanc massif near Chamonix during his 1900 European travels. The majestic alpine scenery of the highest peak in the Alps deeply impressed him, and his time in the mountains of Chamonix provided both physical refreshment and spiritual inspiration during his second and final Western tour.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (1 references):

> Geneva is a great bathing-resort, and the Swami availed

> we are actually in the midst of the snows. In India the snows

### Mont-Saint-Michel

**Location:** Mont-Saint-Michel, Mont-Saint-Michel, France
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Historic island abbey visited by Vivekananda in Normandy


Swami Vivekananda visited the iconic Mont-Saint-Michel in Normandy, France, during his 1900 European travels. The ancient Benedictine abbey perched on a tidal island in the English Channel left a deep impression on him, and he marvelled at the devotion and architectural genius that had produced this magnificent monument of medieval Christian spirituality.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> \
> "What! rise at the expense of another! I didn\'t come to earth for that! ... If I am grateful to my white-skinned \
> Aryan ancestor, I am far more so to my yellow-skinned Mongolian ancestor and, most so of all, to the black\'ac \
> skinned Negritoid!" ( CWSN 1: 153) \
> \
> \
> 85. Commenting on the dungeon-cages of mediaeval prisoners on Mont-Saint-Michel: \
> \
> CWSN 1: 154) \
> \
> "Oh, I know I have wandered over the whole earth, but in India I have looked for nothing save the cave in which \
> to meditate!" (Ibid.) \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*

### Istanbul (Constantinople), Turkey

**Location:** Istanbul, Istanbul, Turkey
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Historic city visited by Vivekananda on his return journey


Swami Vivekananda visited Istanbul (then known as Constantinople) during his travels in 1900. The city, straddling Europe and Asia, with its magnificent mosques, bazaars, and the grandeur of the former Byzantine and Ottoman empires, fascinated him deeply. He saw in this great city a living symbol of the meeting of East and West that was so central to his own vision of universal civilisation.


**From the Complete Works** (21 references):

> ADDENDA \
> \
> (These interesting jottings were found among Swamiji's papers \— Ed.) \
> \{Translatedfrom Bengali I \
> \
> \
> The first view of Constantinople we had from the train. It is an ancient city, with big drains running across the \
> walls, narrow and crooked lanes full of dirt, and wooden houses, etc., but in them there is a certain beauty owing to \
> their novelty. At the station we had great trouble over our books. Mademoiselle Calve and Jules Bois tried much, in \
> French, to reason with the octroi officers, which gradually led to a quarrel between the parties. The head of the \
> officers was a Turk, and his dinner was ready; so the quarrel ended without further complications. They returned…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> regard is for China and his devotion, for Confucianism. He is in the habit of writing occasionally in the newspapers, \

> forth. He cannot at all bear the Christian missionaries preaching their religion in China! His wife also is just like \
> her husband in her regard for China and hatred of Christianity! Maxim has no issue; he is an old man, and \
> immensely rich. \
> \
> The tour programme was as follows \— from Paris to Vienna, and thence to Constantinople, by rail; then by steamer \
> to Athens and Greece, then across the Mediterranean to Egypt, then Asia Minor, Jerusalem, and so on.…

> Empire, the Hungarians yet possess vitality. All the races of Europe, except one or two small ones, belong to the \

> present Persian royal line,…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> monopoly of the Government, as for instance, tobacco. Again, in Russia and Turkey, you are totally forbidden to \

### Vienna, Austria

**Location:** Vienna, Vienna, Austria
**Period:** 1896
**Significance:** Capital of Austria visited during European travels


Swami Vivekananda visited Vienna, the imperial capital of Austria-Hungary, during his European travels in 1896. The city of Beethoven, Mozart, and Freud was a centre of European intellectual and artistic life, and Vivekananda was deeply interested in engaging with the cultural and philosophical currents of fin-de-siècle Vienna.


**From the Complete Works** (10 references):

> regard is for China and his devotion, for Confucianism. He is in the habit of writing occasionally in the newspapers, \

> mixing milk. Tea or coffee becomes injurious like poison if you mix milk with it. The real tea-drinking races, the \
> Chinese, Japanese, Russians, and the inhabitants of Central Asia, take tea without milk. Similarly, the original \
> coffee-drinking races, such as the Turks, drink coffee without milk. Only in Russia they put a slice of lemon and a \
> lump of sugar into the tea. The poor people place a lump of sugar in the mouth and drink tea over it, and when one \

> \

### Athens, Greeze

**Location:** Athens, Athens, Greece
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Ancient capital of Greece visited by Vivekananda


Swami Vivekananda visited Athens, the cradle of Western civilisation, during his 1900 European and Middle Eastern travels. The Acropolis, the Parthenon, and the philosophical legacy of Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle resonated deeply with him, and he saw in ancient Greek philosophy important parallels with the non-dualistic tradition of Advaita Vedanta.


**From the Complete Works** (60 references):

> foremost and most obvious manifestation of Prana is the breathing motion. Prana acts upon air, and not air upon it. \
> Controlling the breathing motion is pranayama. Pranayama is practised to get mastery over this motion; the end is \
> not merely to control the breath or to make the lungs strong. That is Delsarte, not Pranayama. These Pranas are the \
> vital forces which manipulate the whole body, while they in their turn are manipulated by other organs in the body, \
> which are called mind or internal organs. So far so good. The psychology is very clear and most precise; and yet it \
> is the oldest rational thought in the world! Wherever there is any philosophy or rational thought, it owes…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> So much is this the case that it has become a truism. \
> \
> But fortunately we must inquire into the beyond. This present, this expressed, is only one part of that unexpressed. \
> The sense universe is, as it were, only one portion, one bit of that infinite spiritual universe projected into the plane \
> of sense consciousness. How can this little bit of projection be explained, be understood, without, knowing that \
> which is beyond? It is said of Socrates that one day while lecturing at Athens, he met a Brahmin who had travelled \
> into Greece, and Socrates told the Brahmin that the greatest study for mankind is man. The Brahmin sharply \
> retorted: "How can you kn ow man until you kn ow Gods"…

> You say we ill-treat our women. What nation in the world has not ill-treated its women? In Europe or America a \
> man can marry a woman for money, and, after capturing her dollars, can kick her out. In India, on the contrary, \
> when a woman marries for money, her children are considered slaves, according to our teaching, and when a rich \
> man marries, his money passes into the hands of his wife, so that he would be scarcely likely to turn the keeper of \
> his money out of doors. \

> You say we are heathens, we are uneducated, uncultivated, but we laugh in our sleeves at your want of refinement \
> in telling us such things. With us, quality and birth make caste, not money. No amount of money…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> to base everything upon this one idea of unity. But as we have seen, in the same country, there were other minds \

### Amsterdam, Holland

**Location:** Amsterdam, Amsterdam, Netherlands
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Dutch capital visited during European travels


Swami Vivekananda visited Amsterdam in Holland during his 1900 European travels. The prosperous Dutch capital, with its rich tradition of religious tolerance and philosophical inquiry — home to Spinoza's pantheistic philosophy — provided a stimulating environment for his own reflections on the unity of all existence central to Vedanta.


**From the Complete Works** (5 references):

> Just weigh the matter in your mind. Those uncared-for lower classes of India \— the peasants and weavers and the \
> rest, who have been conquered by foreigners and are looked down upon by their own people \— it is they who from \
> time immemorial have been working silently, without even getting the remuneration of their labours! But what \
> great changes are taking place slowly, all over the world, in pursuance of nature's law! Countries, civilisations, and \
> supremacy are undergoing revolutions. Ye labouring classes of India, as a result of your silent, constant labours \
> Babylon, Persia, Alexandria, Greece, Rome, Venice, Genoa, Baghdad, Samarqand, Spain, Portugal, France, \
> Denmark, Holland,…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> October 6, 1896 \
> \
> Dear Christina, \
> \
> I am sure you got my letter from Switzerland. \
> \
> I am now in London, back after having travelled through Germany and Holland. \
> \
> How are things going with you? Had you a nice summer? How are you physically and spiritually? How is Mrs. \
> Fhunkey [Funke] and all the other friends? Have you any news of Baby?* Where is Kr [Kripananda] and what is \
> he doing now? \
> \
> I have another Sannyasin over here with me now, who will work here whilst I am away to India, where I go this \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Professor of Philosophy in the University of Kiel. I have seen professors of Sanskrit in America and in Europe. \
> Some of them are very sympathetic towards Vedantic thought. I admire their intellectual acumen and their lives of \
> unselfish labour. But Paul Deussen \— or as he prefers to be called in Sanskrit, Deva-Sena \— and the veteran Max \
> Muller have impressed me as being the truest friends of India and Indian thought. It will always be among the most \
> pleasing episodes in my life \— my first visit to this ardent Vedantist at Kiel, his gentle wife who travelled with him \
> in India, and his little daughter, the darling of his heart \— and our travelling together through Germany and…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> To Mr. E. T. Sturdy \

> C/o Miss MacLeod, \

> Rue de La Paix, \

> \
> 5th September, 1895. \
> \
> Dear and Blessed Friend, \

### Geneva

**Location:** Geneva, Geneva, Switzerland
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Swiss city visited during European travels


Swami Vivekananda visited Geneva during his 1900 European travels. The cosmopolitan Swiss city on the shores of Lac Léman, long a centre of international diplomacy and humanitarian ideals, appealed to his own vision of universal brotherhood. He spent time here resting and meeting with European admirers of his teachings.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> \
> July 7, 1896 \
> \
> \
> [On the] 18th of this month I start for Switzerland for a holiday. I will come back to London again to work in the \
> Autumn. The work in England bids fair to be much better and deeper than in the U.S. And here in London is the \
> heart of India also. Where are you now? I am passing through Geneva on my way to the Hills. I will be there a day \
> or two. \
> \
> \
> If you be somewhere near, I will make it a point to come to see you. Did you hear Annie Besant? How did you like \
> her? What about your plans of going to India next winter? What about the innocents (Mary and Harriet Hale and Isabelle \
> and Harriet McKindley.) at home? I haven't had any news of them. My love to Father…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (1 references):

> 422 LIFE OF SWAMI VIVEKANANDA
>
> passage to Calais. The English Channel, often choppy, chanced
> on this occasion to be comparatively calm. In order to break
> the long journey between Calais ahd Geneva the travellers spent
> the night in Paris. On the following day, they resumed their
> journey, arriving in excellent spirits at Geneva. The hotel in
> which the party found accommodation overlooked the beautiful
> and peaceful lake. The cool invigorating air, the intense blue
> of the waters, the sky and the fields, the picturesqueness of the
> houses, and the novelty of things about him deeply appealed
> to the Swami.

### Zermatt, Switcherland

**Location:** Zermatt, Zermatt, Switzerland
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Alpine resort below the Matterhorn, visited by Vivekananda


Swami Vivekananda visited Zermatt in the Swiss Alps during his 1900 European travels. The dramatic alpine village beneath the Matterhorn offered him the grandeur of the high mountains that always stirred his spiritual sensibility. The Swiss Alps reminded him of the Himalayas of his homeland, and he spent time here in rest and meditation.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*

### Saas Fee, Switzerland

**Location:** Saas-Fee, Saas-Fee, Switzerland
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Alpine village in Switzerland visited by Vivekananda


Saas-Fee, the 'Pearl of the Alps' in the Swiss canton of Valais, was visited by Swami Vivekananda during his 1900 Swiss sojourn. This high alpine village surrounded by glaciers and peaks over 4,000 metres offered him a place of deep tranquillity and natural splendour during the last major journey of his life.

### Lucerne, Switzerland

**Location:** Lucerne, Lucerne, Switzerland
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Swiss city visited during European travels


Swami Vivekananda visited Lucerne in central Switzerland during his 1900 European travels. The picturesque city on Lake Lucerne, with its famous Chapel Bridge and panoramic views of the Alps, charmed him greatly. He rested here between his engagements in other European cities during his second and final Western tour.

### Schaffhausen, Switzerland

**Location:** Schaffhausen, Schaffhausen, Switzerland
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Swiss town near Rhine Falls visited by Vivekananda


Schaffhausen in northern Switzerland, famous for the nearby Rhine Falls — the largest waterfall in Europe — was visited by Swami Vivekananda during his 1900 European travels. The thundering power of the Rhine Falls deeply impressed him, and the experience of this natural spectacle resonated with his contemplative spirit.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*

---


## Phase: Second Western Tour (1899–1900)

*Second visit to the West — USA, England, France (Congress of Religions, 1900) and Egypt — despite deteriorating health. Established Vedanta Society of San Francisco and deepened contacts in Paris before returning via Cairo.*


---

### San Francisco

**Location:** Vedanta Society of San Francisco, San Francisco, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Founded Vedanta Society of San Francisco


During his second visit to America, Vivekananda established the Vedanta Society of San Francisco in 1900. He lectured extensively in the Bay Area and attracted devoted students who went on to carry Vedanta in the American West. The society remains active today.


**From the Complete Works** (81 references):

> \
> MOHAMMED \
> \

> articles to indicate the omissions, while the words within square brackets are added by way of linking up the disconnected parts. \
> \
> (.Delivered on March 25, 1900, in the San Francisco Bay Area) \
> \
> The ancient message of Krishna is one harmonising three \— Buddha's, Christ's and Mohammed\'s. Each of the three \
> started an idea and carried it to its extreme. Krishna antedates all the other prophets. [Yet, we might say,] Krishna \
> takes the old ideas and synthesises them, [although] his is the most ancient message. His message was…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Los Angeles

**Location:** Los Angeles, California, Los Angeles, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** California lecture tour


Vivekananda lectured in Los Angeles during his 1900 California visit, drawing large audiences. His lectures on practical Vedanta and Jnana Yoga were warmly received by Southern California's spiritually curious population.


**From the Complete Works** (29 references):

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE OPEN SECRET \
> \
> (.Delivered at Los Angeles, Calif., 5th Januaiy 1900 ) \
> \
> Whichever way we turn in trying to understand things in their reality, if we analyse far enough, we find that at last \
> we come to a peculiar state of things, seemingly a contradiction: something which our reason cannot grasp and yet \
> is a fact. We take up something \— we know it is finite; but as soon as we begin to analyse it, it leads us beyond our \
> reason, and we never find an end to all its qualities, its possibilities, its powers, its relations. It has become infinite. \
> Take even a common flower, that is finite enough; but who is there that can say he knows all about the flower? \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> CHRIST, THE MESSENGER \
> \
> \{Delivered at Los Angeles, California, 1900 ) \
> \
> The wave rises on the ocean, and there is a hollow. Again another wave rises, perhaps bigger than the former, to \
> fall down again, similarly, again to rise \— driving onward. In the march of events, we notice the rise and fall, and \
> we generally look towards the rise, forgetting the fall. But both are necessary, and both are great. This is the nature \
> of the universe. Whether in the world of our thoughts, the world of our relations in society, or in our spiritual \
> affairs, the same movement of succession, of rises and falls, is going on. Hence great predominances in the march \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE POWERS OF THE MIND \
> \
> (.Delivered at Los Angeles, California, January 8, 1900) \
> \
> All over the world there has been the belief in the supernatural throughout the ages. All of us have heard of \
> extraordinary happenings, and many of us have had some personal experience of them. I would rather introduce the \
> subject by telling you certain facts which have come within my own experience. I once heard of a man who, if any \
> one went to him with questions in his mind, would answer them immediately; and I was also informed that he \

> One of the greatest lessons I have learnt in my life is to pay as much attention to the means of work as to its end. \

> there; to pay as much attention to the means as to the end. \

### Pasadena

**Location:** Pasadena, California, Pasadena, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** California lectures


Vivekananda gave several lectures in Pasadena during his California tour in 1900. Mrs. Kate Sanborn and other wealthy Californians became his devotees and supporters of the Vedanta work on the West Coast.

### Camp Taylor (Shanti Ashrama)

**Location:** San Antonio Valley, California, Camp Taylor, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Spiritual camp retreat


Vivekananda's favourite retreat in America. He called this place 'Shanti Ashrama' (Abode of Peace) in California's San Antonio Valley. He spent time here in meditation and teaching, living simply in the hills and finding it reminiscent of India's Himalayas.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (4 references):

> the purpose, being fifty miles from a railway station and twelve
> miles from the nearest habitation, except the post-office, three
> miles distant. It was virgin soil, surrounded by forests and hills,
> being situated on the uplands in the southern part of the valley
> of the San Antone on the eastern slope of Mount Hamilton in
> Santa Clara County of California at an elevation of about 2,500
> fect. It was twelve miles from the famous Lick Observatory on
> Mt. Hamilton. Being thus removed far from the conflicting
> influences of worldly life, the name Shanti Ashrama or “Peace
> Retreat” was appropriately given to it. On August 2, Swami
> Turiyananda went there for the first time with twelve students
> whom he…

### Paris

**Location:** Congress of Religions, Paris, Paris, France
**Period:** August 1900
**Significance:** Congress of Religions — second Parliament


Vivekananda attended and spoke at the Congress of History of Religions in Paris in August 1900, held alongside the World's Fair. He also spent time with French intellectuals and scholars, and visited the Louvre. He met Jules Bois and other notable Parisian thinkers.


**From the Complete Works** (154 references):

> cataclysms of the world. Within a few centuries of its birth, the barefooted, shaven-headed missionaries of Buddha \
> had spread over all the then known civilised world, and they penetrated even further \— from Lapland on the one \
> side to the Philippine Islands on the other. They had spread widely within a few centuries of Buddha\'s birth; and in \
> India itself, the religion of Buddha had at one time nearly swallowed up two-thirds of the population. \
> \
> The whole of India was never Buddhistic. It stood outside. Buddhism had the same fate as Christianity had with the \
> Jews; the majority of the Jews stood aloof. So the old Indian religion lived on. But the comparison stops here.…

*— Lectures from Colombo to Almora \*

> intellect. So, you see, the word practical is also relative. \
> \
> We are always making this mistake in judging others; we are always inclined to think that our little mental universe \
> is all that is; our ethics, our morality, our sense of duty, our sense of utility, are the only things that are worth \
> having. The other day when I was going to Europe, I was passing through Marseilles, where a bull-fight was being \
> held. All the Englishmen in the steamer were mad with excitement, abusing and criticising the whole thing as cruel. \
> When I reached England, I heard of a party of prize-fighters who had been to Paris, and were kicked out \
> unceremoniously by the French, who thought…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> This science calls for more application than any business can ever require. Many men can succeed in business; \
> very few in this. Because so much depends upon the particular constitution of the person studying it. As in business \
> all may not make a fortune, but everyone can make something, so in the study of this science each one can get a \
> glimpse which will convince him of its truth and of the fact that there have been men who realised it fully. \
> \
> This is the outline of the science. It stands upon its own feet and in its own light, and challenges comparison with \
> any other science. There have been charlatans, there have been magicians, there have been cheats, and more here \
> than…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> have means, many bathe daily and among Americans the number is larger; the Germans \

> and night, and yet no bath! Ghosts must surely run away from them, what to say of men! \
> What is meant by bath in the West? Why, the washing of face, head, and hands, i.e. only \
> those parts which are exposed. A millionaire friend of mine once invited me to come over \

### Cairo, Egypt

**Location:** Egyptian Museum / Old Cairo, Cairo, Egypt
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Return to India — transit


Vivekananda visited Cairo on his return journey from the West in 1900. He explored the Pyramids and ancient Egyptian ruins with great curiosity, reflecting on the parallels between Egyptian and Hindu spiritual traditions.


**From the Complete Works** (73 references):

> latter, surrounded on every side by all that was sublime, and born and nurtured in a climate which did not allow of \
> much physical exercise \— developed two peculiar and different ideals of civilization. The study of the Greeks was \
> the outer infinite, while that of the Aryans was the inner infinite; one studied the macrocosm, and the other the \
> microcosm. Each had its distinct part to play in the civilisation of the world. Not that one was required to borrow \
> from the other, but if they compared notes both would be the gainers. The Aryans were by nature an analytical \
> race. In the sciences of mathematics and grammar wonderful fruits were gained, and by the analysis of mind the \
> full…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> smashed up by other theories, and so on it went. \
> \
> The Swami then proceeded to explain different ideas of worship. With the ancient Babylonians, the soul was only a \
> double, having no individuality of its own and not able to break its connection with the body. This double was \
> believed to suffer hunger and thirst, feelings and emotions like those of the old body. Another idea was that if the \
> first body was injured the double would be injured also; when the first was annihilated, the double also perished; so \
> the tendency grew to preserve the body, and thus mummies, tombs, and graves came into existence. The Egyptians, \
> the Babylonians, and the Jews never got any farther than this…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> all our knowledge through experience; that is the only way. What we call experiences are on the plane of \
> consciousness. For illustration: A man plays a tune on a piano, he places each finger on each key consciously. He \
> repeats this process till the movement of the fingers becomes a habit. He then plays a tune without having to pay \
> special attention to each particular key. Similarly, we find in regard to ourselves that our tendencies are the result \
> of past conscious actions. A child is bom with certain tendencies. Whence do they come? No child is born with a \
> tabula rasa \— with a clean, bla nk page \— of a mind. The page has been written on previously. The old Greek and \
> Egyptian…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> when no man died unless he wished to die, when he could keep his body as long as he liked, and his mind was pure \
> and strong. There was no evil and no misery; and the present age is a corruption of that state of perfection. Side by \

> be a corruption of a former age by every religion. It went on becoming more and more corrupt until the deluge \
> swept away a large portion of mankind, and again the ascending series began. It is going up slowly again to reach \
> once more that early state of purity. You are all aware of the story of the deluge in the Old Testament. The…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Port Said

**Location:** Suez Canal Gateway, Port Said, Egypt
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Transit point at Suez


Vivekananda passed through Port Said at the entrance of the Suez Canal on his return voyage from the West. This port city at the junction of Europe and Asia symbolised the crossing between two worlds that defined his life's work.


**From the Complete Works** (4 references):

> Dampfer, "Prinz-Regent Leopold" \
> 3rd January, 1897. \
> \
> \
> I received your letter forwarded from London in Rome. It was very very kind of you to write such a beautiful letter, \
> and I enjoyed every bit of it. I do not know anything about the evolution of the orchestra in Europe. We are nearing \
> Port Said after four days of frightfully bad sailing from Naples. The ship is rolling as hard as she can, and you \
> must pardon my scrawls under such circumstances. \
> \
> From Suez begins Asia. Once more Asia. What am I? Asiatic, European, or American? I feel a curious medley of \
> personalities in me. You didn\'t write anything about Dhannapala, his goings and doings. I am much more interested \
> in…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> Port Tewfick \
> 26th November, 1900. \
> \
> \
> The steamer was late; so I am waiting. Thank goodness, it entered the Canal this morning at Port Said. That means \
> it will arrive some time in the evening if everything goes right. \
> \
> Of course it is like solitary imprisonment these two days, and I am holding my soul in patience. \
> \
> But they say the change is thrice dear. Mr. Gaze's agent gave me all wrong directions. In the first place, there was \
> nobody here to tell me a thing, not to speak of receiving me. Secondly, I was not told that I had to change my \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> To Mr. E. T. Sturdy \
> \
> \
> My Dear Sturdy, \
> \
> \
> Port Said, \
> 14th July, 1899. \
> \
> \
> I got your letter all right just now. I have one from M. Nobel of Paris too. Miss Noble has several from America. \
> \
> M. Nobel writes to me to defer my visit to him at Paris to some other date, from London, as he will have to be \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> \
> On board Prinz Regent Luitpold \
> 3rd January 1897. \
> \
> \
> By two p.m. today I reach Port Said. Asia once more. I have not heard from you [for] long. Hope everything is \
> going on well with you. How are Mrs. Funke, Mrs. Phelps, and all other friends? \
> \
> \
> My love to all. Write when you feel like it. \
> \
> \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (1 references):

> 448 LIFE OF SWAMI VIVEKANANDA
>
> reach Colombo on January 15, 1897. They were to be many
> days on the ocean, but the voyage was not tedious. The Swami

### Heidelberg, Germany

**Location:** Heidelberg, Heidelberg, Germany
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** European tour stop


Vivekananda visited Heidelberg during his 1900 European tour. The famous university city on the Neckar river was known for its philosophical and scientific traditions, and Vivekananda engaged with German scholars and Indologists during his stay.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (1 references):

> and so bidding farewell to his disciple, Miss Miiller, he and
> Mr. and Mrs. Sevier journeyed to Zermatt, one of the beauty
> spots of Switzerland, where he hoped to climb the Kornergrat
> and to secure the view of the Matterhorn. But of the party
> only Mr. Sevier succeeded in reaching the summit, the air being
> too rarefied for the other two. The next move was made to
> Schaffhausen, where the Falls of the Rhine are seen at their best.
>
> From Schaffhausen the three tourists went to Heidelberg,
> the centre of one of the greatest German Universities, where
> two days were spent. A visit to the University and the castle
>

### Koblenz (Coblenz), Germany

**Location:** Koblenz, Koblenz, Germany
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Rhine valley journey


Vivekananda passed through Koblenz at the confluence of the Rhine and Moselle rivers during his 1900 European travels. The scenic Rhine valley landscape between Koblenz and Cologne formed a memorable part of his European journey.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Cologne (Köln)

**Location:** Cologne Cathedral, Cologne, Germany
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** European tour stop


Vivekananda visited Cologne during his 1900 European tour. The city's magnificent Gothic cathedral, one of the greatest masterpieces of Gothic architecture, made a deep impression on him. He appreciated the religious devotion embedded in European art and architecture.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (1 references):

### Milan, Italy

**Location:** Milan, Milan, Italy
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Italian tour stop


Milan was visited by Vivekananda during his 1900 European tour. The cultural and fashion capital of Italy, with its magnificent Duomo and Leonardo da Vinci's 'The Last Supper', offered rich artistic and spiritual experiences for the Swami.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*

> it is now the holy land, the place of pilgrimage, the Tirtha!”

### Florence, Italy

**Location:** Florence (Firenze), Florence, Italy
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Renaissance art and culture


Vivekananda visited Florence during his 1900 European tour. The cradle of the Renaissance deeply affected him with its extraordinary artistic heritage. He saw parallels between Italian art and Indian aesthetic spirituality, and appreciated the way beauty was used as a vehicle for the divine.


**From the Complete Works** (13 references):

> To Swami Brahmananda \
> \
> \
> DearRakhal, \
> \
> \
> Hotel Minerva, Florence, \
> 20th December, 1896. \
> \
> \
> As you see, by this time I am on my way. Before leaving London, I got your letter and the pamphlet. Take no heed \
> of Mazoomdar's madness. He surely has gone crazy with jealousy. Such foul language as he has used would only \
> make people laugh at him in a civilised country. He has defeated his purpose by the use of such vulgar words. \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Sometimes it is hard work to be tied to the shoestrings of a great name. And that was just what happened to my \
> letter. You wrote on the 22nd January, 1901. You tied me to the latchet of a great name, Miss MacLeod. \
> Consequently the letter has been following her up and down the world. Now it reached me yesterday from Japan, \
> where Miss MacLeod is at present. Well, this, therefore, is the solution of the sphinx's riddle. "Thou shalt not join \
> a great name with a small one." \
> \
> So, Mary, you have been enjoying Florence and Italy, and I do not know where you be by this time. So, fat old \
> "laidy", I throw this letter to the mercy of Monroe & Co., 7 rue Scribe. \
> \
> Now, old "laidy" \— so…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Rome, Italy

**Location:** Rome (Roma), Rome, Italy
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Seat of Christianity


Vivekananda visited Rome during his 1900 European tour. The Eternal City, with its ancient history and as the seat of the Catholic Church, provided deep reflections on the universality of religious impulse. He visited the Vatican and was moved by the beauty of St. Peter's Basilica.


**From the Complete Works** (48 references):

> fold your hands and stand by and see things go on. Do not injure, if you cannot render help. Say not a word against \
> any man\'s convictions so far as they are sincere. Secondly, take man where he stands, and from there give him a \
> lift. If it be true that God is the centre of all religions, and that each of us is moving towards Him along one of \
> these radii, then it is certain that all of us must reach that centre. And at the centre, where all the radii meet, all our \
> differences will cease; but until we reach there, differences there must be. All these radii converge to the same \
> centre. One, according to his nature, travels along one of these lines, and another, along another; and if…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Then arose the great reformer Shankaracharya and his followers, and during these hundreds of years, since his time \
> to the present day, there has been the slow bringing back of the Indian masses to the pristine purity of the Vedantic \

> and in modern times, wonderful ideas have been carried forward from one race to another. In ancient and in \

### Naples, Italy

**Location:** Naples (Napoli), Naples, Italy
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Departure point for Egypt


Naples was a stop for Vivekananda during his 1900 European tour. The southern Italian city, with Vesuvius in the background, was one of the major European ports. His journey homeward to India passed through Naples and then Egypt.


**From the Complete Works** (10 references):

> of cranes and drop it, without touching, on the Suez boats which carry it ashore. The agent of the Company has \
> come near the ship in a small launch, but he is not allowed to board her. From the launch he is talking with the \
> Captain who is in his ship. You must know this is not India, where the white man is beyond the plague regulations \
> and all \— here is the beginning of Europe. And all this precaution is taken lest the rat-borne plague finds an \
> entrance into this heaven. The incubation period of plague-germs is ten days; hence the quarantine for ten days. We \
> have however passed that period, so the disaster has been averted for us. But we shall be quarantined for ten days \
> more…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Westminster, S. W., \
> 11th November, 1896. \
> \
> Dear Alasinga, \
> \
> I shall most probably start on the 16th of December, or may be a day or two later. I go from here to Italy, and after \
> seeing a few places there, join the steamer at Naples. Miss Muller, Mr. and Mrs. Sevier, and a young man called \
> Goodwin are accompanying me. The Seviers are going to settle at Almora. So is Miss Muller. Sevier was an officer \
> in the Indian army for 5 years. So he kn ows India a good deal. Miss Muller was a Theosophist who adopted \
> Akshay. Goodwin is an Englishman, through whose shorthand notes it has been possible for the pamphlets to be \
> published. \
> \
> I arrive at Madras first from Colombo. The other…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> 39 Victoria Street, \
> London, S. W., \
> 20th November, 1896. \
> \
> \
> I am leaving England on the 16th of December for Italy, and shall catch the North German Lloyd S. S. Prinz \
> Regent Luitpold at Naples. The steamer is due at Colombo on the 14th of January next. \
> \
> I intend to see a little of Ceylon, and shall then go to Madras. I am being accompanied by three English friends \— \
> Capt. and Mrs. Sevier and Mr. Goodwin. Mr. Sever and his wife are going to start a place near Almora in the \
> Himalayas which I intend to make my Himalayan Centre, as well as a place for Western disciples to live as \
> Brahmacharins and Sannyasins. Goodwin is an unmarried young man who is going to travel and live…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Dampfer, "Prinz-Regent Leopold" \
> 3rd January, 1897. \
> \
> \
> I received your letter forwarded from London in Rome. It was very very kind of you to write such a beautiful letter, \
> and I enjoyed every bit of it. I do not know anything about the evolution of the orchestra in Europe. We are nearing \
> Port Said after four days of frightfully bad sailing from Naples. The ship is rolling as hard as she can, and you \
> must pardon my scrawls under such circumstances. \
> \
> From Suez begins Asia. Once more Asia. What am I? Asiatic, European, or American? I feel a curious medley of \
> personalities in me. You didn\'t write anything about Dhannapala, his goings and doings. I am much more interested \
> in…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Hotel continental

**Location:** Rue de Rivoli, Paris, Paris, France
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Paris residence


Hotel Continental on the Rue de Rivoli near the Tuileries was one of the luxury hotels where Vivekananda stayed during his Paris visit in 1900. He used this as his base while attending the Congress of the History of Religions at the Paris World's Fair.


**From the Complete Works** (155 references):

> cataclysms of the world. Within a few centuries of its birth, the barefooted, shaven-headed missionaries of Buddha \
> had spread over all the then known civilised world, and they penetrated even further \— from Lapland on the one \
> side to the Philippine Islands on the other. They had spread widely within a few centuries of Buddha\'s birth; and in \
> India itself, the religion of Buddha had at one time nearly swallowed up two-thirds of the population. \
> \
> The whole of India was never Buddhistic. It stood outside. Buddhism had the same fate as Christianity had with the \
> Jews; the majority of the Jews stood aloof. So the old Indian religion lived on. But the comparison stops here.…

*— Lectures from Colombo to Almora \*

> intellect. So, you see, the word practical is also relative. \
> \
> We are always making this mistake in judging others; we are always inclined to think that our little mental universe \
> is all that is; our ethics, our morality, our sense of duty, our sense of utility, are the only things that are worth \
> having. The other day when I was going to Europe, I was passing through Marseilles, where a bull-fight was being \
> held. All the Englishmen in the steamer were mad with excitement, abusing and criticising the whole thing as cruel. \
> When I reached England, I heard of a party of prize-fighters who had been to Paris, and were kicked out \
> unceremoniously by the French, who thought…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> This science calls for more application than any business can ever require. Many men can succeed in business; \
> very few in this. Because so much depends upon the particular constitution of the person studying it. As in business \
> all may not make a fortune, but everyone can make something, so in the study of this science each one can get a \
> glimpse which will convince him of its truth and of the fact that there have been men who realised it fully. \
> \
> This is the outline of the science. It stands upon its own feet and in its own light, and challenges comparison with \
> any other science. There have been charlatans, there have been magicians, there have been cheats, and more here \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### American Cathedral

**Location:** Avenue George V, Paris, Paris, France
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Discourse venue in Paris


The American Cathedral (Cathedral of the Holy Trinity) on Avenue George V, Paris, was one of the venues where Vivekananda spoke during his 1900 Paris visit. He gave talks on Vedanta and Indian philosophy to audiences of American and European seekers.


**From the Complete Works** (154 references):

> cataclysms of the world. Within a few centuries of its birth, the barefooted, shaven-headed missionaries of Buddha \
> had spread over all the then known civilised world, and they penetrated even further \— from Lapland on the one \
> side to the Philippine Islands on the other. They had spread widely within a few centuries of Buddha\'s birth; and in \
> India itself, the religion of Buddha had at one time nearly swallowed up two-thirds of the population. \
> \
> The whole of India was never Buddhistic. It stood outside. Buddhism had the same fate as Christianity had with the \
> Jews; the majority of the Jews stood aloof. So the old Indian religion lived on. But the comparison stops here.…

*— Lectures from Colombo to Almora \*

> intellect. So, you see, the word practical is also relative. \
> \
> We are always making this mistake in judging others; we are always inclined to think that our little mental universe \
> is all that is; our ethics, our morality, our sense of duty, our sense of utility, are the only things that are worth \
> having. The other day when I was going to Europe, I was passing through Marseilles, where a bull-fight was being \
> held. All the Englishmen in the steamer were mad with excitement, abusing and criticising the whole thing as cruel. \
> When I reached England, I heard of a party of prize-fighters who had been to Paris, and were kicked out \
> unceremoniously by the French, who thought…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> This science calls for more application than any business can ever require. Many men can succeed in business; \
> very few in this. Because so much depends upon the particular constitution of the person studying it. As in business \
> all may not make a fortune, but everyone can make something, so in the study of this science each one can get a \
> glimpse which will convince him of its truth and of the fact that there have been men who realised it fully. \
> \
> This is the outline of the science. It stands upon its own feet and in its own light, and challenges comparison with \
> any other science. There have been charlatans, there have been magicians, there have been cheats, and more here \
> than…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> and night, and yet no bath! Ghosts must surely run away from them, what to say of men! \

> those parts which are exposed. A millionaire friend of mine once invited me to come over \
> to Paris: Paris, which is the capital of modem civilisation \— Paris, the heaven of luxury, \

### Leggetts' house

**Location:** Paris, Paris, France
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Residence with American disciples


The house of the Leggett family (Francis and his sister Alberta) in Paris was a center of Vivekananda's social activity during his 1900 Paris visit. Francis Leggett was a devoted American disciple who had arranged Vivekananda's stays in New York and London, and the family followed him to Paris.

### Jules Bois' home

**Location:** Paris, Paris, France
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Meeting with French occultist-writer


Jules Bois was a prominent French occultist and writer who hosted Vivekananda at his Paris home. Bois, known for his study of occultism and Eastern mysticism, was one of the many European intellectuals who came under Vivekananda's influence during his Paris visit.


**From the Complete Works** (154 references):

### Perros-Guirec, Brittany

**Location:** Perros-Guirec, Perros-Guirec, France
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Summer retreat in Brittany


Perros-Guirec on the Pink Granite Coast of Brittany was where Vivekananda spent time recuperating in the summer of 1900. His devoted disciples, including Josephine MacLeod and others, accompanied him to this seaside retreat on the rugged Breton coast.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> Well, I am getting enamoured of Paris. I now am living with a M. Jules Bois, a French savant, who has been a \
> student and admirer of my works. \
> \
> He talks very little English; in consequence, I have to trot out my jargon French and am succeeding well, he says. I \
> can now understand if he will talk slowly. \
> \
> Day after tomorrow I go to Bretagne [Brittany] where our American friends are enjoying the sea breeze \— and the \
> massage.* \
> \
> I go with M. Bois for a short visit; apres cet [after that] I don't know where I go. I am getting quite Frenchy, \
> connaissezvous [do you know]? I am also studying grammaire and hard at work. [Sentence torn off] In a few \
> months I hope to be Frenchy, but…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (5 references):

> XXXVI
> THE PARIS CONGRESS AND A TOUR IN EUROPE
>
> From Aucust 1, 1900, when he is seen in Paris, until the middle
> of the following December when he returned unexpectedly to
> India, the Swami stayed mostly in Paris with short visits to
> Lannion in the province of Brittany, Vienna, Constantinople,
> Athens and Egypt.
>
> In Paris he was at first the guest of Mr. and Mrs. Francis
> Leggett at their handsome residence in the Place des Etats
> Unis. Later, on his return from Brittany, where he was the
> guest of Mrs. Ole Bull, he lived with Monsieur Jules Bois, a
> famous philosopher, journalist, writer, and student of compara-
> tive religion, in order that thereby he might become more profi-

*— p. 696*

> allegiance. He had seen so many betrayals of hononr that he
> seemed always to be ready for a new desertion. In any case,
> the moment was critical to the fate of the disciple, and this he
> did not fail to realise. Before she had left India, in his company,
> he had told her that she must resume, as if she had never
> broken them off, all her old habits and social custoins of the
> West.
>
> When he returned from Brittany to Paris, the Swami again
> moved in the most distinguished circles. In all his talks he
> missed no opportunity of showing, in ways distinctly his own,
> the influence of India over the entire thought of mankind. He
> would refer to the unmistakable evidences of Hindu religious
> idéas having…

*— p. 703*

> Shankaracharya against Buddha, as it were, by calling in Advaita
> to the aid of Buddhism. The combination of the heart of
> Buddha and the intellect of Shankaracharya, he considered the
> highest possibility of humanity, and this he saw only in his
> own Master amongst the muster-roll of the world’s Teachers
> and Saviours.
>
> The Swami was always the religious observer. In some
> small chapel in Brittany, or in the great cathedrals of Paris, he
> saw the points of similarity between the ritual of Hinduism and
> Roman Catholicism; and in this sense he once proclaimed,
> “Christianity is not foreign to the Hindu mind.” It was in
> Brittany, when he paid a visit on Michaelmas Day with his
> hostess and…

*— p. 702*

> gathering, when a disciple of a certain celebrated English
> scientist laid claim to the fact that her master was experiment-
> ing on the growth of a stunted lily, the Swami replied humor-
> ously, “O, that’s nothing! Bose will make the very pot in
> which the lily grows respond!”
>
> It was after the Congress of the History of Religions that
> the Swami accepted the invitation of Mrs. Ole Bull, to become
> her guest in a cottage she had taken at Lannion in Brittany.
> Here he gave himself up to leisure and retreat, though his
> conversations with those who surrounded him, including Sister
> Nivedita, now returned from America and likewise the guest
> of Mrs. Bull, were unusually luminous. The story of…

*— p. 700*


---

### Chamonix

**Location:** Chamonix-Mont-Blanc, Chamonix, France
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Alpine retreat


Chamonix at the foot of Mont Blanc was visited by Vivekananda during his 1900 European journey. The spectacular Alpine scenery reminded him of the Himalayas that he loved so dearly, and he spent time in meditation amidst the grandeur of the Alps.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (1 references):

> Geneva is a great bathing-resort, and the Swami availed
> himself twice of the opportunity for full water bathing. A visit
> to the Castle of Chillon ended a three days’ sojourn in this
> historic city. They originally intended to remain longer, but
> the programme was suddenly changed, and we next find the
> travellers in the far-famed retreat of Chamounix, some forty
> miles away. When they approached this place, the grand spec-
> tacle of Mont Blanc opened up to view, presenting a vision
> which the Swami said he had not enjoyed even amidst the
> Himalayas. He cried out, “This is really wonderful! Here
> we are actually in the midst of the snows. In India the snows
> are so far distant. One walks for days and…

*— p. 433*


---

### Normandy

**Location:** Normandy coast, France, Normandy, France
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Coastal retreat


Normandy's scenic coast was another area visited by Vivekananda and his disciples during the summer of 1900. The rolling countryside and rugged coastline of this historic French region provided a peaceful environment for rest and reflection.


*Complete Works: no direct reference found.*


*Biography: no direct reference found.*


---

### Wimbledon, London

**Location:** Wimbledon, London, United Kingdom
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Retreat with Western disciples


Wimbledon was one of the London suburbs where Vivekananda stayed during his second UK visit in 1900. He held private classes and meetings with devoted disciples, continuing his work of teaching Vedanta in England before returning to India.


**From the Complete Works** (190 references):

> Switzerland in the summer of 1896, he said: \
> \
> "For she also is the Mother." (ND 4: 276) \
> \
> \
> 10. From Mr. J. J. Goodwin\'s October 23, 1896 letter to Mrs. Ole Bull, quoting Swami Vivekananda's \
> conversation at Greycoat Gardens in London \
> \
> "It is very good to have a high ideal, but don't make it too high. A high ideal raises mankind, but an impossible \
> ideal lowers them from the very impossibility of the case." (ND 4: 385) \
> \
> \
> 11. A November 20, 1896 entry from Swami Abhedananda's diary, quoting Swami Vivekananda\'s observation of \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Now, coming back to the practical. The subject of the practical application of psychology has been taken up in \
> India from very early times. About fourteen hundred years before Christ, there flourished in India a great \
> philosopher, Patanjali by name. He collected ah the facts, evidences, and researches in psychology and took \
> advantage of ah the experiences accumulated in the past. Remember, this world is very old; it was not created only \
> two or three thousand years ago. It is taught here in the West that society began eighteen hundred years ago, with \
> the New Testament. Before that there was no society. That may be true with regard to the West, but it is not true as \
> regards the…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> worn in society by the ladies of the wealthy classes, the dress of the women in general is \
> ugly. The Sari of our women, and the Choga, Chapkan, and turban of our men defy \
> comparison as regards beauty in dress. The tight dresses cannot approach in beauty the \
> loose ones that fall in natural folds. But all our dresses being flowing, and in folds, are not \
> suited for doing work; in doing work, they are spoiled and done for. There is such a thing \
> as fashion in the West. Their fashion is in dress, ours in ornaments, though nowadays it is \
> entering a little into clothes also. Paris is the centre of fashion for ladies' dress and London \
> for men's. The actresses of Paris often set the…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> copied in their own country by their own dressmakers. But the ladies' hats must be of \
> French make. As a matter of fact, the dress of the English and the Gennan women is not \
> good; they do not generally follow the Paris fashions \— except, of course, a few of the rich \
> and the higher classes. So, the women of other countries indulge in jokes at their expense. \
> But men in England mostly dress very well. The American men and women, without \
> distinction, wear very fashionable dress. Though the American Government imposes heavy \
> duties on all dresses imported from London or Paris, to keep out foreign goods from the \
> country \— yet, all the same, the women order their dress from Paris,…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (10 references):

> required many months, she herself confesses, for her to accept
> the Swami’s philosophy in toto.
>
> Miss Noble pondered for a long time upon the Swami's
> words; and before he left England for America she already
> called him “Master”, The description of her first mecting with
> the Swami is charming. She writes:
>
> “Even in far away London indeed, the first time I saw him, the oc
> sion must have stirred in his mind, as it docs in mine, recalling it now, a
> host of associations connected with his own sunstecped land. The time was
> a cold Sunday afternoon in November, and the place, it is true, a West-end
> drawing-room. But he was scated, facing a half-circle of listeners, with the
> fire on the hearth behind…

*— p. 387*

> station in the Himalayas. He was a man of means and learning
> and position, and his name lent weight among the ciicle of his
> friends who went to the Swami’s classes. Among the early
> visitors to the Swami’s class-rooms was Lady Isabel Margesson
> and several of the nobility. The Swami worked day in and day
> out, even as he had done in New York, without respite, giving
> his whole spirit to those who came to be taught.
>
> Feeling that the London public should hear his philosophy
> expounded to them, his friends arranged to have him give a
> public lecture at Princes’ Hall, Piccadilly, one of the most
> fashionable places in the metropolis, on the evening of October
> 22. And here the Swami delivered his…

*— p. 385*

> outlines of the universal principles of the Vedanta and to let
> each apply them to his own concrete forms. He said, “I am the
> exponent of no occult societies, nor do I believe that good can
> come of such bodies. Truth stands on its own authority, and
> truth can bear the light of day.” The correspondent of the
> Gazette wrote of all the Swami’s ideals and of his brilliant
> success in America, and concluded by remarking, “I then took
> my leave from one of the most original of men that I have had
> the honour of meeting.” Thus the London public were informed
> of the Swami’s being a monk and a teacher, and scores gathered
> at his quarters, seeking instruction, or desiring to satisfy their
> curiosity.
>
> It…

*— p. 386*

> had received a letter from his disciples in India, warning him

> aries had been criticising his diet in the West and many of the
> Hindus who had read this became opposed to the Swami and
> attacked him in strong terms. Naturally he was vexed, and so
> he writes from Paris on the day previous to his departure for
> London, to say:

> s nonsense so seriously.

### Shanti Ashrama (Camp Taylor)

**Location:** Camp Taylor, San Jose County, San Jose, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** West Coast Vedanta center


Shanti Ashrama (Camp Taylor) in northern California was established as the West Coast retreat center of the Vedanta Society during Vivekananda's second American tour in 1900. He spent several weeks here in meditation and teaching, greatly enjoying the redwood forests.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references):

> My dear Mrs. Hansbrough \— \
> \
> This is to introduce Swami Turiyananda. The lady who gave the piece of land for Vedanta work belongs to Los \
> Angeles. She has taken Turiyananda with her. He is a great spiritual teacher \— but has no experience in platform \
> work. \
> \
> The best thing would be to help him to start a centre for quiet and rest and meditation in the land near San Jose. \
> With all love to the holy Trinity.* \
> \
> \
> Ever yours in the Lord, \
> \
> VlVEKANANDA \

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*


**From *The Life of Swami Vivekananda* (1960)** (4 references):

> the purpose, being fifty miles from a railway station and twelve
> miles from the nearest habitation, except the post-office, three
> miles distant. It was virgin soil, surrounded by forests and hills,
> being situated on the uplands in the southern part of the valley
> of the San Antone on the eastern slope of Mount Hamilton in
> Santa Clara County of California at an elevation of about 2,500
> fect. It was twelve miles from the famous Lick Observatory on
> Mt. Hamilton. Being thus removed far from the conflicting
> influences of worldly life, the name Shanti Ashrama or “Peace
> Retreat” was appropriately given to it. On August 2, Swami
> Turiyananda went there for the first time with twelve students
> whom he…

*— p. 681*

> Turiyananda, who had been lecturing at the Society rooms since
> April, and holding Children’s Classes in Vedanta, of his inten-
> tion of sending him to California at once. At first the Swami
> found it extremely hard to persuade Swami Turiyananda to
> take charge of the Shanti Ashraina. The brother-disciple always
> hesitated to plunge headlong into any work—and tried to avoid
> all responsibilities. Devoted to meditation and austerity, he was
> averse to activity. Failing to persuade Swami Turiyananda
> by arguments to take charge of the Shanti Ashrama, the Swarni
> said at last, “It is the will of the Mother that you should take
> charge of the work there.” At this the brother-disciple said
> jocosely,…

*— p. 685*

> Street, and at the private hall at 770 Oak Street on May 24, 26,
> 28 and 29.
>
> There were many occasions, here in California, when the
> Swami gave himself over to recreation and communion with

> walks in the open country and felt himself much improved

---


## Phase: Final Years in India (1900–1902)

*Returned to Belur Math for his final years. Made last pilgrimage tours to Varanasi, Bodh Gaya and other sacred sites. Attained Mahasamadhi on 4 July 1902 at Belur Math at age 39.*


---

### Sarnath

**Location:** Sarnath, Varanasi, India
**Period:** 1901
**Significance:** Buddhist pilgrimage


Where the Buddha gave his first sermon. Vivekananda visited Sarnath during his final pilgrimage, reflecting on the universality of religious truth that united all great spiritual traditions of India.


**From the Complete Works** (60 references):

> was a time in India when Dharma was compatible with Mukti. There were worshippers of \
> Dharma, such as Yudhishthira, Arjuna, Duryodhana, Bhishma, and Kama, side by side \
> with the aspirants of Mukti, such as Vyasa, Shuka, and Janaka. On the advent of \
> Buddhism, Dharma was entirely neglected, and the path of Moksha alone became \
> predominant. Hence, we read in the Agni Purana, in the language of similes, that the \
> demon Gayasura \— that is, Buddha (Swamiji afterwards changed this view with reference to Buddha, \
> as is evident from the letter dated Varanasi, the 9th February. 1902. in this volume.) \— tried to \
> destroy the world by showing the path of Moksha to all; and therefore the Devas…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> which the Hindus conceive is called the Brahmaloka; and in this, the Truth is seen much more clearly, like light \
> and shade, but not yet quite distinctly. But as a man sees his own face in a mirror, perfect, distinct, and clear, so is \
> the Truth shining in the soul of man. The highest heaven, therefore, is in our own souls; the greatest temple of \
> worship is the human soul, greater than all heavens, says the Vedanta; for in no heaven anywhere, can we \
> understand the reality as distinctly and clearly as in this life, in our own soul. Changing places does not help one \
> much. I thought while I was in India that the cave would give me clearer vision. I found it was not so. Then I \
> thought…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> Sutras of Vyasa will unquestionably be pointed out as constituting all that. \
> \

> (Chit), Is beloved (Ananda) \—the three indicatives of Brahman.) amidst the heart-stopping solemnities of the Himalayan \
> forests, mixing with the solemn cadence of the river of heaven, or listens to the cooing of the Piya, Pitam in the \
> beautiful bowers of the grove of Vrinda: whether one mingles with the sedate meditations of the monasteries of \
> Varanasi or the ecstatic dances of the followers of the Prophet of Nadia; whether one sits at the feet of the teacher \
> of the Vishishtadvaita…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

### Bodh Gaya

**Location:** Mahabodhi Temple, Bodh Gaya, India
**Period:** 1901
**Significance:** Buddhist sacred site


Where the Buddha attained enlightenment beneath the Bodhi Tree. Vivekananda visited Bodh Gaya during his final pilgrimage. He experienced a profound state of samadhi here and later told a disciple: 'When I sat in meditation at Bodh Gaya, it was as if I became the Buddha himself.'


**From the Complete Works** (12 references):

### Varanasi — Last Pilgrimage

**Location:** Advaita Ashrama / Kashi, Varanasi, India
**Period:** January 1902
**Significance:** Final visit to the sacred city


In January 1902, months before his death, Vivekananda made his final pilgrimage to Varanasi. He spoke to disciples about the Divine Mother and his imminent passing. He inaugurated the Advaita Ashrama here and gave profound discourses on Vedanta.


**From the Complete Works** (74 references):

> was a time in India when Dharma was compatible with Mukti. There were worshippers of \

### Kashmir — Final Himalayas

**Location:** Vale of Kashmir, Srinagar, India
**Period:** 1901
**Significance:** Last Himalayan journey


Vivekananda's last visit to the Kashmir Valley in 1901. Despite being in deteriorating health, he travelled to this beloved region for meditation and rest. He spoke of his readiness to leave the body and expressed deep peace.


**From the Complete Works** (96 references):

> 13. From Swami Saradananda's letter to a brother-disciple, concerning Swami Vivekananda's last days: \
> \
> Sometimes he would say, "Death has come to my bedside; I have been through enough of work and play; let the \
> world realize what contribution I have made; it will take quite a long time to understand that". (ND 4: 521) \
> \
> \
> 14. In an October 13, 1898 letter to Mrs. Ashton Jonson, written from Kashmir, Sister Nivedita described \
> Swami Vivekananda's spiritual mood: \
> \
> To him at this moment "doing good" seems horrible. "Only the Mother does anything. Patriotism is a mistake. \
> Everything is a mistake. It is all Mother. . . . All men are good. Only we cannot reach all. ... I am never…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> \
> "In my own opinion, this statement not only applies to aboriginal tribes, to outlying nations, and to almost all our \

> \
> "Ceremonies of expiation are no doubt suitable in the case of willing converts, returning to their Mother-Church, as \
> it were; but on those who were alienated by conquest \— as in Kashmir and Nepal \— or on strangers wishing to join \
> us, no penance should be imposed." \
> \

> be assimilated into the great body of Hindus. Where shall we look…

*— Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda*

> delivered lectures. Subsequently he visited Sialkote and lectured twice, once in English and once in Hindi. The \

> The various religions that exist in the world, although they differ in the form…

### Belur Math — Mahasamadhi

**Location:** Belur Math, Belur, Howrah, India
**Period:** 4 July 1902
**Significance:** Mahasamadhi — attainment of Nirvana


On 4 July 1902, Swami Vivekananda attained Mahasamadhi (conscious exit from the body) at Belur Math at the age of 39. He had meditated in his room alone that evening, instructing disciples not to disturb him. At 9:20 pm, his head drooped — he had left his body. His passing fulfilled his own prediction that he would not live to forty. He was cremated on the banks of the Ganges at Belur Math.


**From the Complete Works** (99 references):

### Bodh Gaya — Last Pilgrimage

**Location:** Mahabodhi Temple, Bodh Gaya, India
**Period:** 1901
**Significance:** Profound samadhi at Buddha's seat


Vivekananda visited Bodh Gaya in 1901 on his last pilgrimage. He sat in deep meditation under the Bodhi Tree and entered a state of samadhi. He told a disciple afterwards: 'When I sat there I became the Buddha.' This experience deepened his vision of the unity of all great religions.


---

### Swami Swarupananda Point, Mayavati

**Location:** Swarupananda Point, Mayavati, India
**Period:** 1901
**Significance:** Himalayan viewpoint


A scenic viewpoint at 6,500 ft within the Mayavati estate, named after Swami Swarupananda — the first president of the Advaita Ashrama and a direct monastic disciple of Vivekananda. Swarupananda played a key role in scouting the Mayavati estate in 1898 and led the Ashrama until his death in 1906. The point commands sweeping views of the snow-capped Himalayan range.


**From the Complete Works** (15 references):

> version was first published in the collection of poetry entitled In Search of God and Other Poems (Mayavati: \
